#### renunciation, detachment, service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/f_wiIfJJMok?t=30
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Real renunciation is acting basis option number three."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of finding a wallet to illustrate different types of renunciation, highlighting that true renunciation involves recognizing that everything belongs to the Lord and using it in His service.

How many of us were there last to last class when we talked about renunciation, where a person is walking down the street, he finds a wallet? How many of us were there? Before we get into the discussion, what is renunciation? Anyone can help me with an answer. What is renunciation? What is true renunciation? Surrender to God. Let's think about it. What is true renunciation? In that topic, in that discussion, we talked about what is false renunciation, falgu vairagya, and what is real renunciation. It is not, if a person just gives up his wealth, family, house, no, no, it's all material, that necessarily is not renunciation. What is real renunciation? So, just to repeat, some of you are not there. A person walking down the road, he sees a wallet full of 2000 rupee notes. He was walking in the US dollars. And predominantly, there are three ways in which a person, three options a person has. Let's go by option number one. What is option one? With notes coming out of that wallet, very tempting. So, a person is walking down, he sees the wallet, he's having serious financial challenges also. Option one, what does he say? He says, it's not mine. Why I should take? It's not mine. And he just walks away. It's tempting, but he says, it's not mine. He leaves it and walks away. He doesn't get tempted. So, that is one kind of renunciation. You agreed, everyone? Another person or the same person, another scenario or another option is that he sees this wallet. He sees left, he sees right, he sees in the front, he sees in the backside, he sees up, he sees down. Make sure that nobody is watching. What he does? Takes the wallet, puts in his pocket. That's second way of acting in this scenario. Is that okay? That is second type. Third type, third option, third way of acting when you see a wallet. What is that? You pick it up, understand it is not mine. Understand that it belongs to someone. And go and get it and give back to him. Again, it is not mine. Wallet is in your hand. Nobody is watching. You realize it is not mine. You don't enjoy it, knowing that it is not mine. Still, it belongs to someone. Find out to whom it belongs to and go and give it back to him. Of the three options, which one is best? Everyone agrees? Third one. Real renunciation is acting basis option number three. You have wallet in your hand. You are amidst lot of opulence. Lord has blessed you with prosperity. You realize it is not mine. It belongs to Lord. And you use it in the service of the Lord, glorification of the Lord. To offer it back to the Lord with gratitude. That is real renunciation.

#### renunciation, purification, karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/f_wiIfJJMok?t=288
**Verse References:** BG 3.9
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "That purification means we get purified of our mentality of Aham Mameti. I and mine."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that real renunciation involves cultivating detachment from material possessions and the mentality of 'I and mine' (Aham Mameti), emphasizing that actions performed without attachment to results, as a yagya for the satisfaction of the Supreme, do not bind us.

And that real renunciation has to be cultivated by artificially abnegating, giving up our material possessions. The person who abnegates will not necessarily bring about renunciation. For example, the person is saying it is not mine and just gives up, will not necessarily bring about renunciation. That purification means we get purified of our mentality of Aham Mameti. I and mine. It is mine. It is meant for my enjoyment. That's why Krishna in many many places in the Gita says, do not be attached to the results. One who enjoys the result of his activity is a miser. He will get implicated, he will get entangled. Yagnad karmano anyatra loko yam karma bandhana. Any activity we do which is not done as a yagya, yagya means for the satisfaction of the Supreme, loko yam karma bandhana, that activity binds us.

Some people go on pilgrimages, extended period of time, according to them that is renunciation. So different people have different opinions about renunciation. So this is, if Bhagavad Gita is read and understood in the spirit that these are the words spoken by the Supreme Personality of Godhead who is above illusion. We all, no matter how great a person be. We all are subject to four divisions. We all are subject to four divisions.

#### knowledge, imperfection, authority
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/f_wiIfJJMok?t=471
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "That's why words of Acharyas have to be heard submissively because they are above these four defects."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the four inherent defects of conditioned souls—imperfect senses, the tendency to cheat, susceptibility to illusion, and the propensity to commit mistakes—and highlights the importance of hearing from the Lord and the Acharyas, who are free from these defects.

Defects we inherently have four defects can anyone tell me what are the four defects go one by one we all have imperfect senses to give an example of imperfect senses sun is 14 000 times bigger than earth but seeing with your eyes you cannot figure out today because we have studied it in science we have faith in science we believe when the sun is rising on the horizon it looks like a small disk of fire but it is 14 000 times bigger than earth we believe that how many of us believe that sun is 14 000 times bigger than earth but our senses give us some other information so our senses are limited second we have tendency to cheat this is the imperfection inbuilt in a conditioned soul third we are subject to illusion and fourth we commit mistakes so that way any man-made knowledge or knowledge given by any great man even though the intention is not to cheat intention is not to mislead inherently these four defects creep into that understanding i have given this example let's say a person who is a merchant he is weighing things and giving to the customers he is very honest he doesn't want to cheat the customers but if there is inherent defect in the bane scale or in the weights actually this is 990 grammes even though his intention is correct even though he doesn't want to cheat he ends up cheating the customers so that's why if we read gita in the spirit that these are the words spoken by the lord who is effect, who is above these four defects. That's why words of Acharyas have to be heard submissively because they are above these four defects. So here the Lord is personally present and he is saying, now hear from me what is real renunciation.

What is renunciation in three types. Text 5, acts of sacrifice charity and penance are not to be given up but should be performed. So, sometimes people might think this charity, sacrifice, penance, why we should do all those things? All those things should be renounced, should be given up. So Krishna saying, should not be given up but should be performed. Indeed, charity, sacrifice and penance purify even great souls. Because these acts, what does these acts do? Penance, sacrifice and what does it do? It purifies us, frees us from, frees us from? Maya, illusion, frees us from I and mine. That understanding that this is mine, it is meant for my enjoyment, acts of sacrifice, acts of penance, austerities, frees us from that understanding. **Summary:** The speaker uses the example of Vivaha Yagya (marriage) to illustrate how scriptures regulate our tendencies and urges, emphasizing that unless we control our senses, we are disqualified for higher understanding, and all authorized sacrifices are meant for achieving the Supreme Lord.

Just like, for example, Yagya, this Vivaha Yagya. What is Vivaha? Marriage. It's a Yagya. Our senses are going berserk, uncontrolled, untamed. In human form of life, our scriptures, all scriptures for that matter, have a concept of marriage. Animals don't have that. Why? To regulate our tendencies, our urges. Because unless we are regulated, unless we are controlled, unless we tame our senses, higher understanding is, we are disqualified for higher understanding. So that's why these sacrifices are there. These sacrifices, these Yagyas, these rituals are part of our culture. All prescribed sacrifices are meant for achieving the Supreme Lord. It may look little far-fetched, indirect, but all these sacrifices which are authorized, which are mentioned in the scriptures, all the vratas, even Ekadashi.

Why do we follow Ekadashi vrata? Ekadashi is one example. There are a lot of fasting days, right? Half-day fasting, fasting till dusk. Why fasting? How the Lord is pleased. Did the Lord give food? Why on festival days? Why on festival days? The spirit behind all such vratas is, we minimize our bodily, fulfilling bodily demands and focus on fulfilling the demands of soul. Generally, we are so absorbed fulfilling the demands of the body, fulfilling the needs of the soul takes a back seat. We all believe in God. How many of us have strong faith in existence of God? God exists. God is there. Why do we believe in God? Okay, this, I think I have shared long, long back. There was a person who was a very devout Christian. Strong, unflinching faith in Christ. He used to regularly attend the church. The church, the mass, Sunday mass, would regularly read Bible. And one day, after having attended the church, came back home and he went to the market to buy some groceries. And unfortunately, he was also carrying his two year old son with him. Just because he was all alone in the home. So he carried him. And he also took him for purchase of groceries. He was carrying him on his shoulders. And then he passed through a street and unfortunately on that day, there was some fight between two gangs. In every place there are some rowdies, some gangs. And that day the fight became quite violent. And even one of the gangs started shooting. Started actually using guns. They started shooting. And this person was going with the child. Unfortunately, one bullet hit the child. And before he could take him to the hospital, he was dead. The spot. Imagine, the person was a very devout Christian. Reading Bible. Has strong faith in Jesus. Goes to the church regularly. On that day, just going to the market. He is not involved in any fight. Peace loving man. Religious. Pious. God fearing. Has a child in his lap. And gets caught in this fight between these two gangs. Gets hit by a bullet. And the son dies. Now this was too much for him to take. Imagine a small child, two year old. For no fault of his. Innocent child. Getting hit by a bullet. And killed on spot. So he was very grief stricken. Very much. Although we may have higher knowledge. We are not the body. We are spirit, soul. But something which is very dear to us. We are very attached to. Very close to us. It is snatched away from us. Definitely we will be grief stricken. So he was very grief stricken. Never expected. Sometimes somebody old in the family dies. We know that he has to go one day. Some elderly person 85 years old. He is in ICU. Mentally we are also prepared. But here a two year old child. So this person was so grief stricken. So shattered. So shattered by this incident. He started questioning the very fundamentals of the tenets of Christianity. Which says that the Lord is there. The Lord is omnipotent. What is omnipotent means? Lord is all powerful. There is another tenet which says that Lord is omniscient. What is omniscient means? He is all knowing. He knows everything. He knows past. He knows present. He knows future. He knows that gang fight is going to happen. He knows that bullet is going to be sprawled. He knows that this is the path in which if he goes there is a danger. He is omniscient. He is all knowing. And Bible also says that Lord is all kind. All merciful. So when he thought very deeply. And keeping these so called tenets from scriptures. That Lord is omnipotent. Lord is omniscient. He is all knowing. He is all kind. All loving. All merciful. And then when he is comparing what has happened to him. He is saying one of the statement has to be false. If the Lord is all powerful. Assuming he is all powerful. There is no other power superior to him. Stronger than him. More powerful than him. He is all powerful. And he is omniscient. He knows everything. And if he has allowed my son to die. That means he is not all kind. If first and second statement is true. That means he is not kind. How can he allow a two year old child to be killed mercilessly. He is not kind. Or he thought that if this Lord is all kind. He is all merciful. He wanted this child to be saved. But maybe he is not all powerful. There is some higher power. And at times the Lord is helpless. Can't do anything. The Lord wanted to protect. The Lord knew that it is going to be gang fight. The Lord knew that he should not pass by this thing. The Lord was very merciful. He didn't want me to go through this grief. This thing. But maybe he is not powerful. He was helpless. Or if he is all powerful. And he is all kind and merciful. Maybe he didn't knew about it. We say that he is all knowing. Maybe somehow he couldn't figure out that fight is going to happen. He is very kind. He is omnipotent. But unfortunately. Wrong timing. He was not aware of it. And his devotee passed through that thing. And he got hit. And then this person wrote a book. What is the name of the book? Why bad things happen to good people. In fact. Forest fires. Beyond the control. There was one airplane from America. Three people. They are called water tankers. Air water tankers. To douse that fire. That got crashed. Three people died. So. Because they don't have that higher understanding. From our scriptures. From the right source. They conclude. That there is no God. And many people end up becoming atheist. More so if. Very try. They die. How many people die in a parent's womb. More. Why. As we expand this topic. We are trying to during this week. As we expand this topic. We are trying to during this week. Anathalaya. Anathalaya. What is anathalaya? Orphanage. A place where you will find orphans. You will not find, when you go to an orphanage, you go to Anathalaya, you are very clear about your expectation. There will be orphans there. Anathalaya is meant for orphans. You will not find celebrities staying there, international school there. It's Anathalaya. What is Granthalaya? Granth means... scriptures. #### modes of nature, sacrifice, faith
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/dpwrS_9yWJo?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "In this chapter, Krishna is talking about three kinds of sacrifices, three kinds of food, three kinds of faith, like that, under the influence of three modes of material nature."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the topic of the lecture, which is the three kinds of sacrifices, food, and faith as described in the Bhagavad Gita, influenced by the three modes of material nature.

So, today we will be discussing from the 17th chapter, verse number 11 to 13, three verses. And these verses are talking about three kinds of sacrifices. In fact, in this chapter, Krishna is talking about three kinds of sacrifices, three kinds of food, three kinds of faith, like that, under the influence of three modes of material nature. When faith of a person who is predominantly in sattva guna, his faith will be of one type. A person who is influenced predominantly by raja guna, his faith will be of one type. A person who is in tamo guna, his faith will be of one type. Same way, food. A person who is predominantly in tamo guna, his choice of food, his liking, his taste would be of one type. That's why many times you might realize, sometimes, you know, you go to the market, there are all kinds of things being sold, people are buying it, but you may not want to eat those things. So, because the modes influencing decides what kind of food a person will like. And today's verse will be talking about sacrifices. Sacrifices in the mode of goodness, in the mode of passion, and in the mode of ignorance.

#### sacrifice, goodness, duty
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/dpwrS_9yWJo?t=111
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Of sacrifices, that sacrifice performed according to duty and to scriptural rules, not whimsically, and with no expectation of reward, is of the nature of goodness."
**Summary:** The speaker reads and explains text 11 from the 17th chapter of the Bhagavad Gita, defining sacrifice in the mode of goodness as that which is performed according to duty, scriptural rules, and without expectation of reward.

Based on the teachings of Srila Prabhupada. Text 11, the 17th chapter. Of sacrifices, that sacrifice performed according to duty and to scriptural rules, not whimsically, and with no expectation of reward, is of the nature of goodness. Very important points here. According to duty, as per scriptural rules, and with no expectation of reward, is of the nature of goodness. A person may sacrifice, a person may contribute, a person may do something good, but there is no expectation of any reward. That sacrifice is said to be mode of goodness. General tendency is, when a person does any sacrifice or does any good work, there is an expectation of some reward. If not grossly, at least subtly. Now, before we get into the sacrifices and the mode of goodness, why is sacrifice important? Point one. Point two is, what is sacrifice?

**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of paying utility bills and rent to illustrate the concept of being indebted for the resources and services we use, emphasizing the need to reciprocate for what we receive.

If He has given it, take it with you. Why we have to give it back to Him? Okay. So, how many of us pay the electricity bill every month? You may not directly pay. Maybe you will get it delegated. Somebody else will pay on your behalf or maybe the owner in which you are staying, he is paying. But we all use electricity and we pay the bill. Am I right? If we don't pay the bill, what will happen? If 2 months, 3 months, 4 months we don't pay, it will get cut. Same is the case with water connection. You are enjoying the benefit of water supply from the municipal corporation and if you don't pay in return for what you are enjoying, you are a defaulter and the supply will be cut. When the water board or the water department is expecting you to pay for water, is it wrong on their behalf? So it is very very clear in our mind, very very clear, we don't question, we may question that why the bill has come more, but it is very clear that I have to pay the water bill, it is very clear that I have to pay the electricity bill, otherwise it will get cut. I am enjoying electricity and I have to pay for it, I am enjoying water supply and I have to pay for it, it is very clear understand and there are some people who don't want to pay, who are called defaulters or they tap the electricity and illegally use it, lot of electricity in India, water gets tapped and leaked in the process of supply. Okay. A gentle man, a law-abiding man, a good man understands that I am benefiting from it, I am a tenant, I am staying in somebody's house, I have to pay the rent, am I right? Because when we are using something supplied by someone, we are Rini, we are debtor to him and we in turn pay.

#### gratitude, debt, devas
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/dpwrS_9yWJo?t=577
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So in that sense, we are debtor, we are enjoying and we have to pay back."
**Summary:** The speaker extends the analogy to natural resources like sunshine and rain, explaining that we are debtors to the devas, rishis, and other living entities for the benefits we receive from them.

Now you see we are enjoying sunshine, sunlight, heat from the sun. We have taken it for granted. We are enjoying the rains. Because of rains today, there is water in your tap. We are enjoying the rains. So in that sense, we are debtor, we are enjoying and we have to pay back. So there is a verse which says, devarshi bhutaptaninam pitranam, we are debtors to devas, the different devatas who are supplying various necessities. Indra, Chandra, Vayu, Surya. We are debtors to the Devatas. These are People. De frag rishi. deasam charna zasaham khing, They were Rishis, Rishis who have given us this Transcendental Knowledge, Spiritual Knowledge. If there is some sanity in this world, it is because some Yajna is happening this knowledge is there? Still some acts of sacrifices are happening, still the world is intact, still the supplies happening, still rains are happening? Because some Yajna is happening, based on the knowledge given by the Rishis. We are also debtors to so many living entities. We go to D-Mart, we go to Hyco and buy the vegetables, buy this thing, buy that thing, load the trolley, come to the cashier, we pay it. Somebody has worked for it. Somebody has tilled the land in hot sun. Somebody has transported the whole thing. Somebody has packed it. Somebody has put it on the shelves. So we are indebted to all the people who were instrumental in reaching that food on my plate. So Devarshi, Bhutapta, Ninam. Ninam means so many other living entities. Ninam, Pitrinam, our forefathers, ancestors, our relatives. We are debtors to them. We have taken from them. Kinkaram. Kinkaram means we are debtors. We have a debt to pay. We are obligated. We are indebted to them for the services what we have utilized. Water and electricity is very gross. It's evident to us. But sunlight is also a facility which we have used. Rain is also a facility which we have used. If 5 days, 6 days, 10 days sun don't rise or it's very gloomy, many of the people will get into mode of depression. It brightens our day. So Na Kinkaram Naayam Rini. Rini means debtor. We are all debtors to these people. Devarshi, Bhutapta, Ninam, Pitrinam. Na Kinkaram Naayam Rini Charajan. We are Rini to them. We are a debtor to them. We are obligated to them for the services what we have utilized from them.

**Summary:** The speaker quotes Srimad Bhagavatam to explain that the recommended yagya for this age is sankirtana yagya, performed by intelligent and wise individuals, emphasizing that chanting is not merely for peace of mind but a duty.

And it is not whimsical. This is, in the Srimad Bhagavatam, which is the work of Vyasa Dev, it says, Krishna varnam tvisha akrishnam. He is referring to Lord Chaitanya. Krishna varnam. He is of the same category, same tattva of Krishna. Krishna varnam tvisha. Tvisha means complexion. Akrishnam is not like Krishna. It is not Shyama. It is not blackish. Sangopangastha pashadam. Sangopangastha pashadam. And his associates. He is along with his associates. Sango. Sango means association. Accomplice. Pashadam. His devotees. And what are they doing? Yagnair. Yagna means sacrifice. Sankirtana prayar. And what are they doing? They are doing yagya. What is that yagya? Sankirtana prayar. And yajanti. Who will do this yagya? He sumedh asa who are intelligent, who are wise. So chanting is not just for our peace of mind. Chanting is, we generally say, thoda chant kuro, acha focus rahega, peace of mind rahega. That is all by-products. It is not an optional thing. Paying your electricity bill is not optional. If you have to enjoy uninterrupted supply of electricity, remembering the Lord, thinking of the Lord, thanking the Lord, doing yagna to please the Lord is not an optional thing if we have to be happy in this world. It is a duty of a gentleman, a wise man. Sumedh asa, an intelligent person will worship. How will he worship? Yagnai sankirtana prayar, yajanti hi sumedh asa. What they will do? They will do yagya. What yagya? Sankirtana yagya.

**Summary:** The speaker tells a story about a nurse giving a sleeping pill to a sleeping patient to illustrate the meaning of following religious acts without understanding the intent behind them, emphasizing the importance of understanding the purpose of sacrifice.

What is that sacrifice? Sacrifice is, you have got so much, you are debtor already. To say, thank you. So, real purpose is completely forgotten. Why we are doing that sacrifice? Why we are doing that Yajna? So, talking about, forgetting the purpose, the real intent, and just doing, so called, religiously, an activity. There is one story in this connection. One time, there was a multi-speciality hospital, and it had, you know, many patients who were admitted, who were this thing. And one of the patient who was admitted was in a very serious condition. He would not get sleep. And, for many days, he had not slept, anxiety, and some kind of a depression, and all that. So, you know, in hospital wards, there are nurses, and the nurses, the doctors have given prescription. It is the nurses duty to give the medication, as per the doctor's prescription. Doctor comes on the round, and they give, they write and give. And the nurses administer, based on the directions of the doctor. So, this doctor told the nurse that, you know, this person has to be given these pills, these medicines. And one of the medicine was a sleeping pill, so that this person can sleep peacefully, that anxiety, the depression, all that irritation, what he is going through, it subsides. So, on that particular day, this person who was in the hospital, admitted, somehow fell asleep at 9 pm in the night, and by 11 pm, was in deep sleep, snoring. This nurse went to that person, woke him up, wake up, wake up, it is time for medicine, wake up, wake up, wake up, and gave her a sleeping pill. No, no, it is time, wake up, wake up, it is time, you have to take medicine, you have to take medicine with the doctor, what is that medicine? Sleeping pill. It is waking up a person who is in deep sleep to give him a sleeping pill. So, this is the meaning of following religiously, without knowing the intent behind, why that act was prescribed in the first place.

**Key Quote:** "It's not enough, to have an intent, to help others."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of understanding the intent behind religious acts, stating that the original intent is to thank God, and then shares a discussion with some boys about helping others and whether it is enough to have that intent.

That sleeping pill was prescribed to give sleep, the person is already sleeping, and then, the nurse is waking up to give him a sleeping pill. So, many times, the so-called religious acts are also performed without knowing the intent behind those acts. Why are we chanting? Why are we going to the temple? The original intent is to thank God. To think of the, Man Mana, Bhav Madh Bhakto, become my devotee, Madh Yaachi, worship me, Maam Namaskuru, offer obeisances to me, Mamai Vaishya Si Sattam, you will come back to me, you will get freed from this material existence. Intent is forgotten. Today, we go to the temple to ask more benediction. Today, we go to temple to fulfilment of some more material, not to thank Lord, for getting more. Text 13, And that sacrifice, performed in defiance of scriptural injunctions. Today morning, I was having some discussion with some boys. So one of them said, I am obsessed with helping others. I want to help others. I am not religious. I don't necessarily go to the temples, and all that. But I am obsessed with, for me, for me, Dharma is to help others. Is there anything wrong in it?

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### Three kinds of knowledge | Bhagavad Gita 18.19 - 18.22 | HG Amitasana Das | HKM Mumbai
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### modes of material nature, variety, influence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mW4OIc2U99k?t=0
**Verse References:** BG 14
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "All this is because of the influence of modes of material nature."
**Summary:** The lecture begins with an introduction to the three modes of material nature and how they influence people's behavior and attitudes, leading to a wide variety of personalities and desires.

So today we'll start the discussion. We're going to cover four verses from the 18th chapter, verse number 19 to 22. Yes, and the topic for the day is three kinds of knowledge. In the Bhagavad Gita, there is one chapter where Krishna extensively talks about the modes of material nature. Three modes of material nature. Anyone knows which chapter is that? Yes. So based on the divine knowledge revealed to us by Srila Prabhupada, we're going to discuss today's topic. Text 19, in accordance with the three modes of material nature, there are three kinds of knowledge, action, and performers of action. Listen as I describe them. In this world, we see many varieties of people, isn't it? Different tastes, different inclinations. Some are very materialistic, some are very spiritually inclined. Some are very, very short-tempered. Some are very calm, composed. Some are very gentle, some are very cruel, wicked. All of us agree with that? Even in our workplace, or for that matter, even within a family. I think I've shared this earlier one time. We used to conduct classes in Bangalore, in a place, in particular community. We had a lot of buildings there. And in a family, there were two children, two sons, one of them, the older one, was very, very sober, calm. Suppose, he gets into some kind of a brawl or a fight with his friends, he would just come back, maybe he would get hit but he would not retaliate, but the younger brother was exactly the opposite. If somebody does anything to his brother, he would beat them up, the elder brother's friends, and he would also pick up quarrels, he would start fighting with others. So father and mother was wondering, sometimes it happens, no, you're born in a different family, so we were wondering why he's like that. So they said, "Prabhuji, do something, bless him, let's do something in the house so that..." So we see a lot of variety in the terms of people's behavior, attitudes. So here it says all this is because of the influence of modes of material nature, and 14th chapter Krishna extensively talks about the material modes which are nothing but sattva guna, raja guna, and tamo guna.

Now example is given, just like one time there was a king who wanted to celebrate his birthday. So a lot of ministers made arrangements for his celebration, decorated the whole palace. And he said, I would like to go to a prison house and with my own hand distribute some sweets to the prisoners. He was, he went to the prison house. And when he entered the prison house, you know, always there is some kind of rumor mongering. We start, you know, speculating. So the two prisoners who were there who saw the king coming in, they started talking among them. What was the talk? He also is like one of us. He is also a prisoner. He must have also done something, some criminal act because of which now today he is in the prison house. Now mind you, although the king is in the prison house, there's a difference between him and the prisoners. #### Maya, modes of nature, vision
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mW4OIc2U99k?t=1027
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Maya or modes of material nature cannot influence the Lord, who is the master of material nature, who is the creator of the modes of material nature."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of the sun and clouds to explain how Maya, acting through the modes of nature, cannot cover the Lord but can obscure our vision of Him.

So example is given, just like the sun is the cause of clouds, the water, because of sunshine, the water evaporates and the clouds get formed. And if you are having clouds in the sky on a particular day, cloudy sky, you are not able to see the sun. Sometime you even say, you know, you see lot of clouds, it's very cloudy. You say, I can't see the sun today. All this is day time. Even though the clouds are miles and miles long, compared to sun, they are nothing. In fact, in one of the class we discussed, sun is 14000 times bigger than earth. So in that sense, clouds which are few miles long have no power whatsoever to cover the sun. But what do the clouds do? Why we say the sun is not visible? They cover our vision. So Maya or modes of material nature cannot influence the Lord, who is the master of material nature, who is the creator of the modes of material nature. Krishna says, Devi Yesha Gunamahi Mama Maya. This Maya acts through the influence of modes. So just like the clouds cannot cover the sun, although it appears that the sun is covered, sun is not in the sky on a cloudy day. Likewise, when we have clouds of Maya in our consciousness, the Lord is not visible. Although Maya has no power to cover the Lord.

#### spirituality, modes of nature, purification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mW4OIc2U99k?t=1137
**Verse References:** —

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers

**Summary:** The speaker explains that genuine spirituality involves graduating from lower to higher modes, but even sattva guna, the mode of goodness, is entangling.

All the modes of material nature, including Satva Guna, binds us and entangles us in this world. That's why genuine spirituality means, a person slowly graduates. If a person's consciousness is influenced by Tamo Guna, he graduates by following certain rules, regulations, processes, practices. He graduates from Tamo Guna to Raja Guna, Raja Guna to Satva Guna. That's why different paths recommend fasting, eating Satvik Ahaar, certain do's and don'ts. As one starts treading this path, avoid non-veg. So all this is to graduate us from the influence of lower modes to higher modes. Now all along we think that, what is the highest, what is the best mode of material nature, anyone? So we get a feeling that, Tamo Guna, Raja Guna and Satva Guna. Satva Guna is the best. That's why it says, Satvik Jeevan Jiyo. Satvik Ahaar Khao. And lot of people these days who are health conscious, they are eating plant-based diets, they are eating Satvik Ahaar, eating at the right time, regulated lifestyle. And a person may get a feeling that he has made it in life. Because he is eating very Satvik food, he has become a vegan now. From one perspective, a person may think that this is the best. I have achieved the zenith of purification. But according to scriptures, even that Satva Guna is entangling.

So, the knowledge by which one undivided spiritual nature, one undivided spiritual nature, what is that? One undivided spiritual nature, what is that? Atma. Undivided spiritual nature. Is seen in all existences. That's why Krishna in the Gita said, Pandita Samadarshina. A Pandita, a wise man, a learned man, sees the same Atma is there. In a dog, same Atma in a cat, same Atma in a human being. And hence, he is, with that knowledge, he is seizing as they are. Many times people believe that all animals are killed or are meant for our eating. It's meant for us. That's the perspective. That's the knowledge they have. All animals are created by Lord for our food. This knowledge, where one can say, I see this one undivided in all existence, is called the knowledge in mode of goodness. A person who sees one spirit soul in every living being.

---
#### karma, desire, body
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mW4OIc2U99k?t=2139
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So long as we have material desires in this world, we will continue to get material bodies."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the soul gets different bodies according to its karma, desires, and vasanas, and that the purpose of human life is to purify these desires.

Now, why that soul gets different bodies? Anyone can help me with an answer? Why different bodies? Why there are 84 lakh species, 84 lakh yoniya? Why? Why? According to? Laws of karma and vasanas and desires. We have permutation and combination of desires. If a living entity, Atma, develops a very intense desire to eat flesh, then the nature provides a body which is conducive for fulfillment of the desire to eat flesh. And you can get a body of a carnivore. Some Atma is very comfortable. He wants to be in water, play in water, be in water, okay. You have a body which is conducive for that. So, because there are so many permutation and combination of desires, to facilitate that, a body is given to that jantu, to that living entity. So long as we have material desires in this world, we will continue to get material bodies. So, a body that is in one form or other, to fulfill those desires. That's why a scripture is saying, human form of life, we have to purify our desires.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### Three modes of Material nature | 2016-04-13 | SB 3.19.29-30 | HG Amitasana Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### modes of material nature, consciousness, prakriti and purusha
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-Q3kCW0Gd9E?t=693
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So Prabhupada gives a very nice example to explain that how we lose our consciousness when in contact with the material nature."
**Summary:** This segment explains how living entities in the material world are prakriti, meant for the pleasure of the soul, and how contact with material nature contaminates our original Krishna consciousness, using the analogy of a spark of fire falling on different types of grass or water to illustrate the effects of sattva, raja, and tamo guna.

So the living entity, when he wants to be the enjoyer, all living entities being amsa of the Lord are actually prakriti, male or female in this material world. Although male is said to be the enjoyer, female prakriti is said to be enjoyed. But in this material world, all living entities, irrespective of what bodies we may be in, are actually prakriti, meant for the pleasure of the soul. When we take the position of purusha—enjoyer—then our original consciousness, which is pure consciousness, which in Krishna is consciousness, is contaminated. So Prabhupada gives a very nice example to explain how we lose our consciousness when in contact with the material nature, the original Krishna consciousness. Prabhupada also very emphatically says Krishna consciousness is not an artificial imposition of the mind. It is the original consciousness of the living entity. The example is given just like a spark of a fire. It already is fire. It is part of fire, qualitatively same as fire. Quantitatively may be much lesser than the original fire. But that spark, if it falls on drugs, then it can ignite rather than it getting doused, getting extinguished. It can ignite the dry grass and again become fire. So this Prabhupada compares to a living entity, a spark, a part and parcel, amsa of the Supreme Lord predominantly in sattva guna. So when we are in sattva guna, it is very easy to transcend the modes, although even sattva guna is binding. Sattva guna brings in element of peace, clarity of thought. But still, unless we are blessed with the mercy of a pure devotee of the Lord, transcendental knowledge, we can be conditioned even in sattva guna. We have to cultivate sattva guna, but sattva guna is not the ultimate thing. Sattva guna is a stepping stone, the springboard where we can enter into vishuddha sattva, shuddha sattva. So spark falls down on dry grass, it can immediately ignite the grass. This is a comparison given to sattva guna. If the spark falls down on water, immediately fiery quality is extinguished. That is the influence of tamo guna. And those who are captured by tamo guna, they are lazy and sleepy. These are the symptoms. Tamo guna means they are lazy and sleepy.

---
#### modes of material nature, consciousness, maya
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-Q3kCW0Gd9E?t=1049
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Although we are qualitatively one with the Lord, Sat, Chit and Ananda, but under the influence of these modes, we have lost our original consciousness."
**Summary:** This segment discusses how the modes of material nature influence our consciousness, leading us away from our original Krishna consciousness, and how Vedic practices aim to uplift individuals from tamo guna to raja guna and eventually to sattva guna, emphasizing that even sattva guna is not the ultimate goal. It also introduces the concept of Maya Devi and her stringent control over living entities in the material world.

Although we are qualitatively one with the Lord, Sat, Chit and Ananda, but under the influence of these modes, we have lost our original consciousness. And depending on which mode we are predominantly influenced by, to that extent we lose our original consciousness. And when we, from tamo guna to raja guna to sattva guna, the whole Vedas, different karma kanda portion, it's all about how the living entity can gradually be uplifted. Some sacrifices, some rituals, some austerity, some penances, maybe for material gain. It is slowly to lift the living entity who has fallen deep under the influence of tamo guna gradually to raja guna, for their own material, artha, dharma, artha, kama, and eventually moksha. How the living entity can be brought to sattva guna and then from sattva guna, if he gets the mercy of a pure devotee of the Lord, he gets transcendental knowledge, he can come to shuddha sattva and original pure Krishna consciousness. So Prabhupada in one place says this material world, just like here he says, consists of three modes, goodness, passion and ignorance, three modes of material nature. And the grip on the living entity is very, very stringent. Deviyesha gunamayi. These gunas, the influence, the grip on the living entity. Deviyesha gunamayi mamamaya dhuratyaya. Just by knowledge, just by scholarship we cannot come out of this grip. Sometimes we as devotees say, also theoretically we know, but helplessly we get influence and act in a way which later on we regret. Deviyesha gunamayi mamamaya dhuratyaya. Prabhupada compares that this material world, this maya, maya devi, which is keeping the living entities entrapped in the material existence, durga devi. We all have seen the picture of durga. She has ten hands. So Prabhupada says these ten hands represent the ten directions: north, east, south, west, the four corners, north-east, north-west, south-east, south-west, up and down. These ten directions.

#### maya, surrender, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-Q3kCW0Gd9E?t=1223
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "And the only way to come out of this grip of the material nature, stringent laws of material nature, these modes, Krishna says, is surrender to me."
**Summary:** This segment uses the analogy of a prison house with CCTV cameras in all directions to illustrate Maya's constant watch over us, emphasizing that the only way to escape the grip of material nature is to surrender to Krishna. It also contrasts true devotion with the superficial religiosity of figures like Ravana, who sought to exploit their devotion for personal enjoyment.

Maya is keeping an eye on us, keeping a grip on us. Imagine you are in a prison house. This material world is also called a prison house. Imagine you are in a prison house and you are being watched, that you don't come out of the prison house. Not by one, not from one direction. There are CCTV cameras up, down, east, west, all directions. Moment the person makes some attempt to come out, the supervisor or the jail superintendent is alerted. So then Krishna in the same verse says, and the only way to come out of this grip of the material nature, stringent laws of material nature, these modes, Krishna says, is surrender to me. People worship different gods, many people who are religious. Just like in one sense, Prabhupada was explaining that even Ravana was quite religious, very pious in that sense. He was also very austere. They also had to believe in God, but they wanted to be an enjoyer. They wanted to occupy the post of God. They wanted to be an enjoyer just like Ravana. Although he was a great worshipper of Shiva, but he wanted to enjoy Sita, take the position of Lord Ramachandra and enjoy Sita.

#### surrender, Krishna's mercy, demigods
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-Q3kCW0Gd9E?t=1343
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So Prabhupada says just like that, Krishna has the power to take us out of the influence of the mode."
**Summary:** This segment uses the analogy of a person condemned to death, where only the President can grant a reprieve, to illustrate Krishna's power to liberate us from the influence of the modes. It also contrasts the demigods, who understand their duty to serve the Lord, with those overpowered by maya, who seek only material enjoyment.

So, Maya Devi has very stringent laws. Prabhupada explains that just like when a person is condemned, when he is condemned to be executed or may be killed, sometimes we have people who are given capital punishment. They are hanged or various other means. So Prabhupada says there is only one way where the person can get a reprieve. Just like even in Indian law, even the Supreme Court may give a judgement. You may have lower trial courts and then other courts. Finally the case goes to the Supreme Court. Even the Supreme Court gives a judgement. Only one person has the power to overrule that judgement and to transform that punishment into life imprisonment or any other kind of thing, and that is the President of the country. So Prabhupada says just like that, Krishna has the power to take us out of the influence of the mode. All the impurities, all the contaminations, abhadras, the influence of the modes can all be vanquished when we remember Krishna. So Prabhupada says that the demigods, they are godly living entities. They are also living entities like us, but they are called godly, they are as good as God, they are called demigods because they have realized that they are not the enjoyers. The living entities who know this perfectly well are called demigods, godly or almost god. Since the living entity is part and parcel of the Supreme Lord, it is his duty to serve the Lord and to please Him. They are overpowered by maya, completely convinced that how we can squeeze out more and more enjoyment from this material world.

#### devotion, Krishna consciousness, transformation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-Q3kCW0Gd9E?t=1478
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Likewise, when our consciousness is Krishna consciousness, naturally what will come out in terms of our thinking, feeling and willing, will be service to the Lord, will be pleasure of the Lord, will be satisfaction."
**Summary:** This segment explains that when we become purified, our consciousness naturally shifts from seeking enjoyment to giving pleasure to the Supreme Lord. It uses the story of the orange to illustrate that what comes out of us when we are 'squeezed' depends on what is inside, emphasizing the importance of cultivating Krishna consciousness.

Whereas when we get purified, we become godly. Naturally, our consciousness changes from being an enjoyer to give pleasure to the Supreme Lord. The demigods are all attached to the personality of Godhead and for their pleasure, the demons, who was a source of trouble to the Lord, to the world, was killed. So when we become godly and when we start serving the Lord, when we desire serving the Lord, it is not that the Lord is a one-way traffic. The Lord is simply enjoying our service. The Lord is also reciprocating the service of His devotee by giving pleasure to the devotee, by giving protection. In other words, serving the devotee. And how is the Lord reciprocating to the demigods? The demigods were troubled by the demon, and for their pleasure, the demon, for whose pleasure? The devatas' pleasure. Devatas are godly living entities. For their pleasure, the Lord killed the demon. The demon, who was a source of trouble to the world, purified life is meant to please the Lord, and all sacrifices performed in purified life are called Krishna Consciousness. One time, there were two friends, and one of them took an orange in his hand and said, "If I squeeze this orange very, very tightly, what will come out?" So the other person looked at him, "What will come out? Juice will come out." He says, "Which juice will come out? Will it be watermelon juice?" He again looked at him. "Something has gone wrong with this person. What do you mean?" He says, "Will the grapefruit juice come out?" He says, "Orange juice will come out." Then this person asked, "Why?" Again, you know, it was becoming too much for him. He said, "It's because orange, that's what it is inside. So when you squeeze an orange, orange juice will come out. What's the big deal? Why are you asking?" So he said, "What if, just for sake of discussion, you are in place of orange? And someone squeezes you. Squeezes you means offends you, troubles you, gives pain to you. What will come out?" So of course, what is inside me? So likewise, this example was saying that how many times when people offend us or give pain to us or trouble us, different people react differently. A lot depends on what's going on, what's going on inside us, what modes we are influenced by. Different people respond differently.

---
#### Krishna consciousness, devotional service, transformation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-Q3kCW0Gd9E?t=1688
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This Krishna consciousness is developed by devotional service as clearly mentioned here."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes that Krishna consciousness is developed through devotional service, leading to a natural desire to serve Krishna and Guru. It contrasts this with material consciousness, which leads to selfish actions driven by the senses and mind. The speaker highlights that serving is the constitutional position of the living entity and that Krishna consciousness is not an artificial imposition but a natural outcome of purifying our consciousness.

So likewise, when our consciousness is Krishna consciousness, naturally what will come out in terms of our thinking, feeling and willing, will be service to the Lord, will be pleasure of the Lord, will be satisfaction. And if our consciousness is material consciousness, then we will exercise our free will to choose our own satisfaction, our own pleasure and act in a very, very selfish way. So this organization, this society which Prabhupada created, it is all about trust. It is all about the transformation of consciousness, what's going on inside us. And that is what many times we may not be able to control the circumstances, situations around us, the family members, but we can control very easily in one sense the consciousness, what's going on inside us. And when we transform that, when we purify that consciousness, how does we purify the consciousness? Prabhupada says, this Krishna consciousness is developed by devotional service as clearly mentioned here. When we develop our Krishna consciousness, naturally we will become purified. Naturally we will have desire to serve Krishna, Guru and Krishna. Prabhupada in another place says, that when we are unselfish, under the influence of Maya, these modes of material nature, naturally we will have inclination to serve the dictates of our senses and mind. Serving is the constitutional position of the living entity, Dharma. We cannot separate it out. All of us are servants, right from President of United States to a small beggar in the street. Everyone is serving. So when we are under the influence of Maya, when we are under the stringent grip of the modes of material nature, naturally we will exercise our thinking, feeling and willing to serve the dictates of our senses and the mind. When our consciousness is transformed into Krishna consciousness, we will naturally, it will be a natural outcome. That's why Prabhupada says, it is not an artificial repression. It is not an artificial imposition. It is a natural consciousness. When the consciousness is transformed into Krishna consciousness, by remembering Krishna, thinking of Krishna, chanting Krishna's holy name, when the consciousness is transformed into Krishna consciousness, natural outcome would be, our thinking, feeling and will. We will exercise our free will, to please Krishna, to serve Krishna, to remember Krishna. Since the living entity is part and parcel of the Supreme Lord, it is his duty to serve the Lord and to please Him.

---
#### purification, Krishna consciousness, spiritual world
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-Q3kCW0Gd9E?t=1892
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Actually that oneness is in terms of desires. We have no other desire other than the desires of the Lord."
**Summary:** This segment explains the process of purification through devotional service, leading to the development of pure Krishna consciousness and the cessation of selfish desires. It contrasts this with the impersonalists' concept of merging with the Lord, clarifying that true oneness lies in sharing the same desires as the Lord. The segment also touches on the concept of life after death and the spiritual world as our original home, where everyone blissfully serves Krishna.

Right now if we see our qualities, it is opposite. We are conditioned souls. We are trying to get purified. By engaging in devotional service, slowly purification happens. Tapo Divyam Shudhyet Brahmasaukhyam Tvanantam. What will happen by doing divine tapasya, by engaging in devotional service, all the contaminations, all the abhadras, all the dirty consciousness will get cleared off, and we will come to our original, pure, pristine Krishna consciousness, which is Krishna consciousness. And when we are situated in Krishna consciousness, then 24 hours, we have no other desire but to serve the desires of the Lord. Many time impersonalists, mayavadis say that we merge with the Lord, we become one with the Lord. Actually that oneness is in terms of desires. We have no other desire other than the desires of the Lord. The individuality is still there, but we have no separate desires. So in the previous verse, Brahma is mentioning that these two personal assistants of the Supreme Lord, having been cursed, were destined to take birth in the same family. So each one of us also are born in this material world, different circumstances. After a few births, they will return to their original positions, own positions. Many people, even so called religious people, don't believe in life after death. Even though they may be pious, they may be religious, even so called religions, they proclaim that this life is all in all. But from Bhagavatam, we get an understanding that how the living entity is amsa of the Lord, and parastasmatu bhavanyo vyakta avyakta sanatana. There is a spiritual world, our original home, and when we get purified, our consciousness gets purified, become eligible to go back to our original home, and blissfully serve Krishna. Unless we cultivate the desire to serve Krishna, give pleasure to Krishna, we are in the material world. And when we are trained, when our consciousness is purified, Krishna consciousness is developed, then we are eligible to go back to the spiritual world, and in spiritual world only one activity is happening, and that is everyone is blissfully serving Krishna. So why Maya is captive? Because Maya does not want living entities who still have a desire to enjoy go to the spiritual world and disturb the arrangement happening there. In the spiritual world, Krishna is the center of enjoyment, everyone is serving the Lord, and everyone is very convinced that Krishna is to be pleased, Krishna is to be served, Krishna is to be satisfied. Ichcha dvesha samuthena. When that Ichcha comes, that no, I want to be served, I want to be the enjoyer, then we have this material creation. So it gives complete clarity what the material world is, what the spiritual world is, what is the purpose of life. Big, big erudite scholars sometimes if you ask a common man or even a so-called intelligent man, what you ask, what is the purpose of life, he will fumble. I have asked this question to many, many people.

#### purpose of life, realization, purification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-Q3kCW0Gd9E?t=2112
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "And as the grip loosened, we start relishing real pleasure, real happiness, and finally, when we are completely purified of all these contaminations, we develop our pure consciousness, which is our original consciousness, we go back to the spiritual world."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the common misconceptions about the purpose of life and contrasts them with the scriptural understanding that we are all trying to be enjoyers, which leads to suffering. It emphasizes that as we become purified, the grip of material nature loosens, allowing us to experience real pleasure and happiness, ultimately leading us back to our original consciousness and the spiritual world.

Some will say, those who are influenced by goodness, the purpose of life is to be good, do good. Somebody, the purpose of life is to go back from this world smiling. You come in this world weeping, purpose of life, act in such a way that the world smiles, the world cries and you smile. That's the purpose of life. So from scriptures we get a clear understanding and if we analyze ourselves, a little bit of realization, we all are trying to be enjoyers, then we get slaps. We can see that, little bit realizations we have. And as and when we become purified, the stringent grip is loosened. And as the grip loosened, we start relishing real pleasure, real happiness, and finally, when we are completely purified of all these contaminations, we develop our pure consciousness, which is our original consciousness, we go back to the spiritual world. We stop here.

#### karma, vikarma, good, bad
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/W_7s-zfBI-8?t=2437
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If you can't do something good, then don't do bad. At least we have come to that level, we all understand don't do bad."

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### UNDERSTANDING MAYA'S TRAP | HG AMITASANA DAS | GITA LIFE Live
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### Maya, Bhagavad Gita, Devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xAGKTxpgXiE?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So we'll try to understand these principles based on the teachings of Bhagavad Gita."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the topic of Maya's trap, outlining the session's focus on understanding Maya, how we get trapped, and how to safeguard ourselves, all based on the teachings of the Bhagavad Gita, followed by prayers.

Hare Krishna, welcome all of you for today's session. The topic for today's discussion is understanding Maya's trap. All of us agree that we are in Maya's trap. So today's session is all about understanding what is this Maya, second, how do we get trapped, what are the various traps laid out by Maya Devi, which is energy of the Lord, and third, how do we safeguard. When there are traps, it's important that we remain protected. We safeguard ourselves. So we'll try to understand these principles based on the teachings of Bhagavad Gita. So we'll start with prayers. Request all of you to kindly join me in offering these prayers. You can fold your hands and if required, you can chant these prayers along with me. Otherwise just fold your hands and be with me till the completion of these prayers.
