#### death, suffering, material world, karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9k_j_1KSDYg?t=990
**Verse References:** BG 8.16
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Even if we go to Brahmaloka, which is the highest planet in this material world, the heavenly planet, where the lifespan is much much higher... when that pious credits gets depleted, then punar avritino, we again come back to this fallen condition."
**Summary:** The speaker uses examples of natural disasters in the US and the limitations of infrastructure in India to show that death and suffering exist everywhere. He then explains that even reaching Brahmaloka is temporary, as pious credits eventually deplete, leading to rebirth.

In the US, just last year I was reading, there was an article which says that when there was a hurricane and a tornado, the government was quite, they can have systems where they can warn the citizens well in advance, they can forecast all the things. But then, once the tornado or the hurricane was coming, 36 people died. Although they evacuated a lot of people, they were asked to move to some shelter, some strong places, but still 36 people died. Sometimes we think that death happens only in third world countries or countries like India, where the infrastructure is very poor. Sometimes when, especially in smaller towns or villages, you call a government dispensary, and maybe if you are in need of an ambulance, who knows, you may get an answer that ambulance has gone to drop the doctor's children to school. Or who knows, the wife of the doctor has, is using ambulance to go and purchase some vegetables, gone to purchase some vegetables from the mandi. So we think that in a country like this, where the infrastructure is not up to the mark, we have deaths happening, we have people suffering because of old age, but that's not true. Death is there even in America. Death is there even in all prosperous countries. Old age is there. Diseases are there. And again, birth is there. So therefore our shastras say, Abhramma bhuvana loko punar avritino arjuna. Even if we go to Brahmaloka, which is the highest planet in this material world, the heavenly planet, where the lifespan is much much higher, Abhramma bhuvana loko punar avritino arjuna and once our, so when we enjoy, our pious credits gets depleted. Just like in a bank account, we have some money, basis that money you can have a standard of living, you can buy latest gadgets, you can go and shop for the things you want. But as you are shopping, your bank balance is depleting. Every time you use a credit card, it is getting debited. Every time you purchase something, it's getting debited. Likewise, when we enjoy, the currency which is getting debited from us is the Punya currency. And when we suffer, when we undertake some misery, then the Papa currency is getting depleted. So that's why it is said, because of Punya we enjoy and because of Papa we suffer. So, Abhramma bhuvana loko punar avritino, when that pious credits gets depleted, then punar avritino, we again come back to this fallen condition. We again glide back. Just like you have a bank balance, again it gets depleted. Who knows, it becomes nil. And if the company gives an overdraft, it can become negative also.

---
#### detachment, heaven and hell, material world, prison house
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9k_j_1KSDYg?t=1198
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Even if we may get promoted to heavenly planets, they are like, they are still a prison house. Still they are dukha alayam ash ashwatam. It's a place of misery."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that knowing the temporary nature of heaven and hell, a devotee does not aspire for elevation to heavenly planets. He compares the material world to a prison house with different classes of confinement.

So therefore, knowing that Papa, Punya, Heaven and Hell are part of this material world, even if the standard of enjoyment is very high, because still we are caught up in this cycle of birth and death, punar api janma, punar api marna, a devotee who is in knowledge, a learned person, does not make elevation of himself to a heavenly planet as his goal. Like in India, for many people, going to America is their goal, their aspiration. It's number one thing in their wish list. They think success in life is if I can somehow get a green card and get citizenship of US. So our scriptures say that even if we may get promoted to heavenly planets, they are like, they are still a prison house. Still they are dukha alayam ash ashwatam. It's a place of misery. And just like in a prison house, you know, you have different classes of prison, because you have different types of prisoners. Sometimes you have a prison cell which is, you know, dark, damp, practically no amenities, shared bathroom, you have to sleep on a bench made of stone. So there are prisoners, hardcore prisoners we are made to stay in. There are prison houses where, you know, just the food is dropped from the top. It's practically like a ditch. No light, no ventilation. I don't know presently such, but in olden times, yes, there were prisoners who were made to stay in dungeons, pits, dark. And there are prisoners who are, you know, the VIPs. Recently Lalu, Prasad, Yadav got bail. He was also behind bars, but I'm sure the kind of prison what he was in is different from what a normal prisoner is put into. I'm sure he must be getting his meals on time, the newspaper, I'm sure must have all the amenities, must have got an attached toilet, all kind of, you know, maybe a television for him to get updated about the latest happenings. It's also prison house, but it's A class prison house. So whether it's A class prison house, B class prison house or C class prison house, it's a prison house. Your freedom is checked. So the freedom of the living entity who does not want to die, who does not want to become old, who does not want to become diseased, is choked, whether it is the heavenly planet or it is the hellish planets.

---
#### detachment, heaven and hell, material world, maya
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9k_j_1KSDYg?t=1374
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Why do we come in this material world? We forget our real position, who we actually are. He instead, instead of serving the Lord, he becomes servant of Maya."
**Summary:** The speaker summarizes that all scriptures discuss heaven and hell as part of the material world, and even promotion there is not a great achievement. He then quotes Srila Prabhupada on how living entities forget their position as servants of the Lord and become servants of Maya.

So all scriptures talk about heaven and hell, heaven and hell are part of this material world. Even if we get promoted there, in one sense, it is not a great achievement because we are still not absolved of the problems of birth, death, old age and disease. I'll just read out some quotes from Srila Prabhupada's teachings. In this way, the living entity forgets his position as an eternal servant of the Lord. Why do we come in this material world? We forget our real position, who we actually are. He instead, instead of serving the Lord, he becomes servant of Maya. Maya means that which is not. So if we analyze, although we may say that I should not serve anyone, I'm not a servant. Why should I serve God? We may rebel, we may put forward this kind of argument, but the fact remains that we are servant. We are serving our, the dictates of our senses, dictates of our mind. It is his misfortune that by forgetting his real contact with the Supreme Lord, he becomes a servant of Maya. As servant of Maya, he sometimes becomes a king, heavenly planets, and sometimes an ordinary citizen, sometimes a Brahmana, sometimes a Shudra and so on. Sometimes he is a happy man, sometimes a prosperous man. We see all this flux in this material world. Somebody who is very prosperous, he becomes a pauper. Somebody who is very famous becomes suddenly infamous. Vijay Mallya, you know, people were so, such a wealthy businessman. He had so many industries. Especially in Bangalore, he was so famous. There is a separate, you know, city called UB City. And he got implicated in all these scandalous things. Sometimes he is a happy man, sometimes a prosperous man, sometimes a small insect. Sometimes he is in heaven. We are talking about heaven and hell. And sometimes he is in hell. Sometimes he is a demigod. Sometimes the living entity wanders all over the universe. Brahmanda brahmite kono bhagyavanji. We are Brahmanda brahmite. We are wandering from one planet to another in this material world, in this material universe. Only when he comes in contact with a bona fide spiritual master, he can understand his real position. Otherwise, for all of us, the goal of life is to enjoy life. To enjoy and dabble in sense objects. Eat, drink and be merry. Without knowing why are we in this material world, why there is death, why there is old age.

---
#### Krishna consciousness, maya, detachment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9k_j_1KSDYg?t=1575
**Verse References:** BG 7.14
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "A devotee who is in knowledge, a learned person, does not make elevation of himself to a heavenly planet as his goal."
**Summary:** The speaker continues quoting Prabhupada, explaining that after many lives of wandering, one awakens to Krishna consciousness and regrets past experiences. He emphasizes that even in heavenly planets, one is still in the clutches of Maya.

He thus becomes disgusted with material existence. Bahunam janmanam ante. After many lives of wandering here, there, being kicked by maya, like a football. Sometime as a king, sometime as a beggar, sometime in heaven, sometime in hell. At that time, he awakens to his Krishna consciousness. Bahunam janmanam ante Jnanvan mam prapadyante Krishna. Krishna says, will come to me. Vasudevam sarvam iti sa mahatma durlabha. That atma, which has come to the understanding that I should surrender to Vasudeva and he is all in all, he is the absolute truth. Such an atma is very rare. At that time, when he becomes Krishna consciousness, he comes to Krishna consciousness, he regrets his past experiences in material world. He regrets because he has come to his senses. This regret is very beneficial because it purifies the living entity. What was I chasing? What kind of wrong goals I had? What kind of aspirations I had? What kind of wrong understanding about life I had? A devotee comes to his senses. He then prays to the Lord to engage in his service. And at that time, Krishna grants liberation from the clutches of Maya. So even if one is in the heavenly planet, still he is in clutches of Maya. In fact, because the standard of happiness is much higher, the possibility of a person forgetting his real position, just like we had example of the Govardhan Leela, Indra actually forgot that all his powers are coming from Krishna. And all that Leela was enacted to bring Lord Indra to his senses that all his powers, we all know that Leela, how Krishna lifted the Govardhan hill and Indra sent all the Samvartaka clouds and all kinds of things to destroy Vrindavan. And eventually Krishna protected all the Vrajavasis. And finally Indra was defeated and he comes to his senses. Falls at the lotus feet of Krishna.

#### detachment, heavenly pleasures, service to Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9k_j_1KSDYg?t=1773
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This world is so designed that we might want to get promoted to heavenly planets, we might want to enjoy heavenly pleasures, but every such pleasure independent of Krishna will have a reaction."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the world is designed to tempt us with heavenly pleasures, but any enjoyment independent of Krishna will have a reaction. He uses the analogy of a servant who tries to take the master's position to illustrate the consequences of trying to enjoy independently of the Lord.

So this world is so designed that we might want to get promoted to heavenly planets, we might want to enjoy heavenly pleasures, but every such pleasure independent of Krishna will have a reaction. Yehi samsparsha jaabhoga dukhayonahi evate will result in misery. Nothing comes free in this world. Whatever enjoyment we might aspire for minus the Lord forgetting our real position as servant of the Lord, we will suffer sooner or later. So devotee comes just like a servant in the house. If he takes the position of the master, he cooks the food and before even serving the master he eats it. Starts living in the main house. Starts living in the main bedroom where the master is supposed to be using. And the master gets to know he will be very upset with him. This is a crude example. So we have taken the position of trying to take the position of the Lord, try to be the enjoyer, to be the Lord of all we survey, to be the controller, to be the master, to be the enjoyer, to be famous, to be glorified, and we suffer the consequences. So therefore, it's mentioned here the devotee regrets that and when he comes to Krishna Consciousness, comes to his senses, the Lord frees him from Maya. Krishna says this divine energy of Maya is very very difficult to surmount. This divine energy of Maya is very very difficult to surmount. So a devotee should actually not aspire for heavenly pleasures or heavenly planets getting promoted there. He should actually aspire, a devotee actually prays to the Lord, Oh Lord, does not matter where you keep me, heaven or hell, all what I want is an opportunity to be engaged in your service. Shri Prabhupada says, Shri Prabhupada gives a very nice story Shri Prabhupada says, how people are so maddened by materialistic pleasures by materialistic pleasures with no understanding of what is spiritual world, why we are suffering, etc. why we are suffering, etc. So one time there were some Christian preachers So one time there were some Christian preachers who went to London who went to London and they were preaching in a place where there were coal mines. So there were miners there, they were working in coal mines. So there were miners there, they were working in dark, damp coal mines. We all know coal mines below the earth, it's very dark, damp and sometimes we heard of some accidents also. So the preachers were telling all the coal miners in the sermon, saying that if you do not surrender to Jesus, then you will go to hell. Sometimes the preachers are fanatics. So they said if you do not surrender to Christ you will go to hell. So some of the miners, they were quite nape. One of them asked, what is hell? How does it look like? So they had no clue about what is hell. So the preacher told them, hell is a place which is dark, damp, a lot of suffering. So that's what they were doing whole day, morning to night. That was their workplace. So it did not enthuse them. What's the difference? So that's what they are, you know, morning to night. They got accustomed to. That's their life. Dark, damp.

#### detachment, accountability, sinful deeds, pious activities
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9k_j_1KSDYg?t=1984
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "His goal is not to get promoted to heavenly planets. His goal is not to enjoy heavenly pleasures. That's the goal for majority, 99.9999% people, including so-called religious and pious people."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes the story of the coal miners, explaining that people need to understand they are accountable for their actions. He emphasizes that a devotee's goal is not heavenly pleasures, but service to Krishna.

So it doesn't really inspire them, motivate them to surrender to Jesus because the status quo remains the same. Even if they go to hell, it's dark, damp. So is the world. So this is the coal mine where they are working. So then the Christian preacher told them, you know, hell is a place where you will not get newspapers, where you will not get alcohol, drinking. And then all of them came to this. Oh, that's very miserable. No, no, we don't want that place. And then they got inspired. Yes, we want to surrender to Jesus. So people have no idea that for all the activities we are doing, we are accountable. If we are engaged in sinful deeds, we will have to suffer the consequences. We may degrade ourselves to lower species of life, to hellish planets. And if we engage in pious activities, we will elevate to heavenly planets. But still, we are not absolved of, we are not freed from the cycle of birth and death. And hence, the devotee chooses his goals very wisely. His goal is not to get promoted to heavenly planets. His goal is not to enjoy heavenly pleasures. That's the goal for majority, 99.9999% people, including so-called religious and pious people. Their goal is somehow, with the blessings of the Lord, enjoy life. I was hearing a lecture in which it was mentioned that for many of us, the main meal, just like you have a main meal. And along with the main meal, you know, there's a lemon served. And on the main meal, you can put some lemon, squeeze some lemon, and it enhances the taste of the main meal. So for many of us, so-called religious people also, the main meal is materialistic pleasures, sensual pleasures. And yes, we all, 5-10 minutes in the morning, we do some religious activities. Some pious activities, it's like squeezing lemon to enhance the flavor, so that we can enjoy our material life nicely. We can have more materialistic pleasures, enhanced materialistic happiness. But that's not what a devotee aspires for. So this is the first point which I want to say. In all scriptures, the mention is there of heaven and hell. And as I mentioned, it is... Heaven is part of this material world, and a devotee does not aspire to go to heaven, or he is worried about hell. He is always concerned, oh Lord, wherever you want to keep me, I don't mind, so long as you engage me in your service.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### WHAT'S WRONG WITH SENSE ENJOYMENT? | HG AMITASANA DAS | GITA LIFE Live
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### introduction, sense enjoyment, happiness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7V6VaGltgPY?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The topic for the day is what's wrong with sense enjoyment."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa begins the Gita Life session by introducing the topic of sense enjoyment and questioning the common understanding of happiness through sensory experiences.

A warm welcome to all of you for today's Gita Live session. The topic for the day is what's wrong with sense enjoyment. The typical understanding is that if we have to be happy, and that is what all of us are seeking, Anandamayo Bhyasat, to be joyful, to be blissful, to be happy, the typical understanding is let the senses come in contact with the sense objects, dabble with the sense objects, and there is a sensation of pleasure. The more we get, the more the senses come in contact with the sense objects, in other words, the more we eat nice palatable foodstuffs, the more we see beautiful forms which are very, very pleasing to the eyes, the more we will be happy. So today's session is what's wrong with that understanding? So before we get into this discussion, let us start the session with prayers seeking the blessings of Prabhupad, Lord Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, Lord Sri Krishna, so that we can have a nice discussion, a meaningful discussion today with a lot of important insights which we can go back and apply in the coming days, our lives.

---
#### prayers, invocation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7V6VaGltgPY?t=117
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Namo Mahavadanyaya Krishna Prema Pradayate Krishnaya Krishna Chaitanya Namne Gauratuse Namaha"
**Summary:** The speaker leads the audience in chanting prayers, invoking the blessings of Prabhupada, Lord Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, and Lord Krishna.

So please fold your hands if possible and chant these prayers along with me, maybe you can repeat after me. Nama Om Vishnupadaya Krishna Prishtaya Bhutale Srimate Bhaktivedanta Swamin Nityanamine Namaste Saraswate Deve Gauravani Pracharine Nirvishesha Shunyavadi Pashchatyade Shatarine. Jai Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nithyananda Shri Advaita Gadadhara Shiva Sadhi Gaurabhakta Vrinda. Namo Mahavadanyaya Krishna Prema Pradayate Krishnaya Krishna Chaitanya Namne Gauratuse Namaha. Namo Brahma Nithyananda Shri Advaita Gadadhara Shri Advaita Gadadhara Shri Vasadi Gaurabhakta Vrinda. Brahma Nadevaya Go Brahma Nehitaaya Cha Jagadhitaaya Krishnaya Govindaya Namo Namah. He Krishna Karunasindho Dhinabandho Jagatpate Gopesha Gopika Kanta Radha Kanta Kanta Namostu Te. Tapta Kanchan Gaurangi Radhe Vrindavaneshwari Vrishabhanu Sute Devi Pranamami Hari Priye. Vansha Kalpat Rubhyascha Kripa Sindhu Bhya Evachha Patita Nam Pavne Bhyo Vaishnave Bhyo Namo Namaha. Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare.

---
#### sense enjoyment, material happiness, suffering
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7V6VaGltgPY?t=272
**Verse References:** BG 3.39
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Material happiness is nectar in the beginning, but it's poison in the end."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the first aspect of the discussion: 'Beginning nectar, ending poison,' explaining that material happiness derived from sense enjoyment ultimately leads to misery, contrasting it with the typical understanding of happiness.

So the topic is what's wrong with sense enjoyment. Like we have been doing in previous sessions, we will cover three aspects. The first aspect as far as today's discussion is concerned, I would give it a heading: beginning nectar, ending poison. I repeat, beginning nectar, ending poison. Krishna in the Gita says, Yehi Samrsparshya Bhogha Dukh Yonahi Evate. The normal typical understanding is that when our senses dabble with sense objects, there is a sensation of pleasure. Like for example, if I see something very beautiful or I hear something very melodious which is very pleasing to my ears, naturally I feel happy, I feel joyful. There is a sensation of pleasure when I eat something which is very, very palatable, very tasty, very good for my taste buds, very pleasing to my taste buds, I feel happy. That's how in today's world, you know, fast-food restaurants and different kinds of eatables, even though it may not be very good for the body, so long as it is pleasing for my tongue and gives me pleasure, pleasing sensations, I go for it. So that's the typical understanding. So more and more I bring my senses come in contact with the sense objects, the more happy I will become. And that's how with this understanding, the entire advancement which is happening in this world is all about how we can titillate our senses more and more, how we can enjoy our senses more and more in much more gorgeous, much more elaborate way. And such elaborate arrangements for goodness and pleasure and pleasure and pleasure and pleasure and pleasure and gratifying our senses, enjoying our senses is called as advancement of civilization. Now what's wrong so long as it is giving me pleasure? If I eat something very nice, what's wrong? Why should I restrain? After all, it's giving me happiness. Is it something wrong to be happy? So Krishna in the Gita says, yehi samsparsha jaab hoga, this very happiness which a person derives by senses coming in contact with the sense objects, this very happiness is dukha yonahi evate, is the breeding ground, yoni means the womb in which the embryo grows, dukha yonahi evate, it is a breeding ground for dukha, for misery.

---
#### dharma, sense control, spiritual pleasure
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7V6VaGltgPY?t=488
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Material happiness, material sense enjoyment is nectar in the beginning, it's poison in the end."
**Summary:** The speaker clarifies that Krishna is not asking us to shut down our senses, but to use them within the framework of dharma, distinguishing between base pleasure and uncontrolled indulgence, and contrasting material happiness with spiritual pleasure.

Now am I trying to convey that we should keep our senses shut? We should not see anything beautiful? We should not eat anything which is palatable? We should not hear anything which is melodious? Is that what Krishna is asking us to do? Just quietly in one place and practically don't do anything with your senses? No. All of these senses have a proper function or a role to play. Krishna in the Gita says, dharma aviruddho kamo asmi. Even kama, even sexual pleasure has a place. But if it is against dharmic principles, then it becomes illicit sex. And then such pleasure brings misery in our lives. There is something called as base pleasure. To maintain my body, for the upkeep of the body and for serving the Lord, my senses have to dabble with sense objects. Like we have to eat something. Like we have to see something. Such base pleasures which are within the framework of dharma are allowed. There is nothing wrong in enjoying such pleasures. And when we enjoy such pleasures as per dharma, like for example a man is enjoying a company of a woman in a proper marriage framework, there is nothing wrong about it. So such pleasures, if it is not within the framework, if we dabble the senses with the sense objects, thinking that the more I dabble, the more I indulge, the more happier I will become, that becomes the philosophy of my life, or the philosophy of my source of enjoyment, I am heading for trouble. That's why Krishna says, yehi samsvarsya jabhoga dukh yonahi evate. So material happiness is nectar in the beginning. For example, a man sees something. Let's say an adult sight. It's nectar. It's very pleasing to the eyes momentarily. It's nectar in the beginning, but it's poison in the end.

---
#### spiritual pleasure, renunciation, higher taste
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7V6VaGltgPY?t=695
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Spiritual pleasure is apparently poison in the beginning, but nectar in the end."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts material happiness with spiritual pleasure, describing spiritual practices as seemingly difficult at first but ultimately leading to real and lasting pleasure.

We all know how many many scandals happen because of rapes, because of abortions, because of illicit affairs, live-in relationships, so many complications which a person gets into because of uncontrolled mind, uncontrolled senses. So material happiness, material sense enjoyment is nectar in the beginning, it's poison in the end. Whereas spiritual pleasure, which in human form of life, we are entitled to experience, we can experience such higher pleasures, are apparently poison in the beginning. For example, getting up early in the morning, it's not very pleasing to the mind and body. For example, chanting the names of the Lord, you're feeling sleepy, but somehow you're pushing and you're doing it. Because you're following the order of the Guru, who has asked us to chant. It may not be very pleasing to the mind, maybe in the initial stages. But if we pursue that path, we enjoy real pleasure or nectar in the end.

#### detachment, illusion, standards of happiness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7V6VaGltgPY?t=742
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Although he is living in filth, but he seemed to be quite happy."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates the story of Indra being cursed to become a pig, illustrating how even in a filthy state, one can find a standard of happiness, highlighting the illusory nature of material pleasure.

One time, Indra was cursed by Brahaspati to become a demon, to become a pig, a hog. So, Indra took birth as a pig and he had a she-pig as a wife and he got children, piglets, from his wife. And he was dabbling in family affairs, taking care of the family, the kids and enjoying pleasure. You know, typically pigs, how do they enjoy? It's a filthy place, rolling all over, the body is fully covered with filth. And they seem to be enjoying such pleasures, eating fresh stool. So, Indra was dabbling in such pleasures. And in the meantime, there was some challenge in Indraloka, some management issues, some administrative issues. And Brahma came down to invite Indra back to Indraloka, saying that, your services are required there, why don't you come over? But Indra, who was in the form of a pig, was reluctant to go. He says, no, I have my family, I have responsibility, I have my kids. And I am quite happy out here. Although he is living in filth, but he seemed to be quite happy.

---
#### consciousness, standards of happiness, lower pleasures
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7V6VaGltgPY?t=842
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "When a person's consciousness is covered, he cannot realize that he is in a miserable state."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of people living in a slum to illustrate how one's standard of happiness is relative to their consciousness, and how a covered consciousness prevents one from realizing a higher standard of enjoyment.

Sushila Prabhupada explains that there are many standards of happiness. For example, when you go to a slum, you will see some people who have not taken bath for 3 days, 4 days, tattered clothes, very dirty looking, dirt all over the body. You feel so uneasy. How can they live in such an unclean state? Not take bath for days together. They are comfortable with that. For them, that is happiness. They don't feel anything bad about it. But for a person who has got used to, who has experienced a clean environment, having taken bath daily, for him it is obnoxious. It is difficult for him to believe that a person can be without bath for days together. Because his standard of cleanliness, his standard of pure enjoyment is different from that of a person living in a slum. So when a person's consciousness is covered, he cannot realize that he is in a miserable state. He cannot realize that there is a much higher standard of enjoyment. For example, you throw halwa to a pig, it will not be able to relish that halwa. You throw pearls and diamonds to that pig, it has no sense. Because it is happy dabbling in the pleasures which according to us are abominable. Accordingly, the pleasures which we are dabbling in, sensual pleasures, which according to us is the highest achievement in life, according to self-realized souls, they are lower pleasures.

---
#### sensual pleasure, human intelligence, eternal pleasure
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7V6VaGltgPY?t=969
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Sensual pleasures are temporary. They are nectar in the beginning, they are poison in the end."
**Summary:** The speaker quotes Prabhupada, stating that sensual pleasures are available in all life forms and are temporary, contrasting them with the eternal and ever-increasing nature of real, genuine pleasures.

In fact, Prabhupada says, such sensual pleasures, the pleasures which a person atma experiences when the senses come in contact with the sense objects, is available in plenty in all other life forms also. For example, dog in a street chasing a she-dog is also experiencing pleasure, the sensual pleasure. Prabhupada was quite blunt. He made the statement, a dog may have an intercourse in the street and experience that pleasure. A man can indulge in similar pleasure and intercourse in an air-conditioned building, in a multi-storied apartment, in a very, very cozy environment, in a very soft bed. But the intrinsic quality of the pleasure is the same. What dog is experiencing in a street, in open, a man may experience maybe in a very, very exalted surroundings, in a very nice environment, but the standard of pleasure is the same. In human form of life, with our developed intelligence, with discretion, with discrimination, we are entitled to experience a much higher standard of pleasure. Ramante yogino anante. And the standard of that pleasure is, it is ananta. Ananta means it is not temporary. Ant means it ends. Sensual pleasures are temporary. They are nectar in the beginning, they are poison in the end.

#### spiritual pleasure, sense gratification, lust
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7V6VaGltgPY?t=1088
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "It is lust only which is the greatest enemy of the living entity."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the nature of spiritual pleasure, highlighting its ever-increasing quality and contrasting it with the conflicts and miseries arising from the mistaken understanding that sense gratification leads to happiness, identifying lust as the greatest enemy.

Real genuine pleasures are eternal in nature, are ever increasing. Law of diminishing returns doesn't set in. In fact, the more you dabble, the more you feel happy. Anandam budhi vardhanam tatipadam purnam rita svadhanam Anandam budhi vardhanam. The anand, the happiness, vardhanam, vardhanam means increases, pratipadam, every step, purnam rita svadhanam. And when one dabbles in such pleasures, he feels completely satisfied and contented. As I mentioned earlier, in our present society, everyone is moving ahead in life with the understanding that the more we gratify our senses, the more we will be happy, the more enjoyment we will experience. And there is fundamental mistake in this understanding. And that's why we see lot of conflicts, lot of misery, lot of pain, lot of entanglement, lot of marriages ending up in divorces, lot of abortions happening, lot of rape cases, all because of uncontrolled mind and uncontrolled senses. That's why Krishna in the Gita says, it is lust only which is the greatest enemy of the living entity.

#### sense control, regulation, bodily maintenance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7V6VaGltgPY?t=1171
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "From the very beginning, regulate your senses."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of regulating the senses from the beginning, using the example of a tortoise retracting its limbs, and allowing base pleasures only for bodily maintenance and achieving the goal of life.

And then Krishna, being very kind upon us, is giving us a hint. If you have to attack that enemy, if you have to kill that enemy, where can you find that enemy? And then Krishna says, senses, mind and intelligence are the sitting places of lust. So this karma, which is the greatest enemy of the living entity, where it is sitting? It is sitting on our senses. We fulfill our karma by dabbling with sense objects. Therefore, Krishna in the next verse says, from the very beginning, regulate your senses, restrain your senses. He gives example like a tortoise. Tortoise, whenever it has use of its limb, it takes it out, uses the limb and whenever it wants, it can retract back. So we also, we cannot completely shut our senses. That's not what Krishna is advising. But we should restrain and use it only for our bodily maintenance. The pleasures, what we experience, the base pleasure for maintaining this body, for achieving our goal of life, the human form of life.

#### sense pleasure, wrong direction, chariot analogy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7V6VaGltgPY?t=1389
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Sense pleasure is wrongly directed."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the second aspect, 'Sense pleasure is wrongly directed,' using the analogy of a chariot driven by five horses to represent the senses, mind, intelligence, and soul, explaining the proper flow of command.

The second aspect, what I would like to cover today, is sense pleasure is wrongly directed. We can just remember two words, wrongly directed. Now what do I mean by wrongly directed? Our scriptures give us a wonderful example of a chariot driven by five horses. In this example, the five horses represent the five senses: eyes, ears, nose, tongue and touch, a skin. The horses are controlled by the reins. The reins in this example represents our mind. The chariot driver who is controlling the reins, controlling the horses using the reins, represents our intelligence. And the passenger in the chariot represents the atma, the soul. So typically in a chariot, we see that the flow of command, the direction of command is coming from the passenger to the driver, driver to the horses through the reins.

#### uncontrolled senses, panic, anxiety
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7V6VaGltgPY?t=1477
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The senses is directing the soul."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that in our present life, the flow of command is reversed, with the senses directing the soul, leading to a chaotic and panic-stricken state, similar to being in a chariot with unruly horses and a weak driver.

And that's how the chariot moves in the direction in which the passenger wants, the ultimate destination of the passenger. And all along the route, the passenger is happy because very soon he will be reaching the desired destination. Now in this example of chariot where this chariot is driven by five horses, our mind being the reins, the driver being the intelligence, and the atma being the passenger in the chariot, in our present life, with uncontrolled mind and uncontrolled senses, the flow of command, the direction of command is opposite. Instead of atma saying and directing the intelligence, and intelligence directing the mind, and mind directing the senses, in our present civilization, with no proper training, the senses is directing the soul. Based on what we want to eat, based on the demands of the senses, the mind starts making plans. The origin of idea comes from the senses. I want to eat this, I want to see this, I want to hear this. The desire, sensual desires. And based on that the mind starts making plans. And then the intelligence becomes a slave of the senses. Instead of using spiritual intelligence, instead of having that spiritual intellect, what is in my self-interest, the intelligence starts making plans to fulfill the desires of the senses. And the atma, the passenger is helplessly sitting, watching the whole show. Now visualize, you are in a chariot. Although these days we don't see chariot quite often, but assuming that you are in a chariot driven by five horses, there is a chariot driver, and he has that lagam, the reins in his hand. And all the horses are unruly, untamed, unchecked. Each one trying to move in different directions. Each one trying to move the chariot, pull the chariot in different directions. And the chariot driver, he is actually not properly trained, he is weak. He is trying to move the chariot. He is trying to control the horses, but each one is moving in different directions. And the chariot is wobbling. Any moment it can skid off the road and can meet with an accident. And the passenger in the chariot is completely in a panic stricken state, completely in anxiety, not knowing where the chariot is heading, not knowing when it will meet with an accident. And he is in a panic state of affairs. Actually this is what people get into when they have panic attacks, when they have hypertension, blood pressure, heart attacks, not able to control the mind, not able to control the senses, not able to control their emotions, and get into mess.

**Summary:** The speaker explains that Vedic civilization emphasizes training in controlling the mind and senses, and that accidents in life, such as rapes, murders, and suicides, are a result of an untamed mind and senses.

So Vedic civilization, the first training which a person would get is Pramacharya training, how to control the mind, how to control the senses, how to tame the horses. If you have to have a smooth ride, we have no option but to have the horses which are well trained and obedient. The horses have to listen to the chariot driver. The chariot driver has to be skilled. Chariot driver should know how to control the horses. And then and only then, if you are in such a chariot, then you will have a smooth ride and you can expect to reach your destination safely. So all of us are moving fast in life. Unfortunate state is most of us do not know the destination. We do not know the destination. And we are moving in a chariot. Unfortunately all the horses in the chariot are unruly, untamed, unchecked. The intelligence is weak. The spiritual intellect is weak. And we are meeting with innumerable accidents. Accidents could be in the form of rapes. Accident could be in the form of murders. Accident could be in the form of abortions. Accident could be in the form of illicit relationships which messes up somebody's family, somebody's life, leads one to suicide. All that is the result of untamed mind and untamed senses.

#### senses, death, moth
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7V6VaGltgPY?t=2612
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Because of one weakness, because of his eyes being uncontrolled and drawn towards that fire, towards that light, it loses its life."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the third aspect, 'Gone astray,' and begins with the example of a moth being drawn to a flame, illustrating how one uncontrolled sense can lead to death.

There's a very nice verse in our scriptures, which says, Patanga, matanga, kuranga, bhringa, meenahata. I'll go one by one. Patanga. Patanga means moth. Just a week back, it was raining very heavily. We stay in the fourth floor of a building. So I was watching. I got up early in the morning. It's around 3.30 in the morning when I was looking down. So there was a street lamp light and it was raining very heavily, pouring very heavily. And around that street light, I saw a lot of moths buzzing around that light. In spite of that heavy rain pouring on them, still they were determined to hover around that light. And I was like, oh my God. And I'm sure I didn't go in the morning, but in the morning, all of them, all those patangas, all those moths would have been lying dead below that light. It seems that when the moth come closer to that light, that heat, that warmth is very, very intoxicating for them. Although it may start burning them, burning their bodies, but they cannot resist that. They cannot resist the attraction to that flame of light, and they're helplessly drawn towards it, and then they're burned to death, and they'll be lying all dead below that light. So our scriptures say that patanga, because of one weakness, because of his eyes being uncontrolled and drawn towards that fire, towards that light, it loses its life.

#### senses, bondage, elephant
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7V6VaGltgPY?t=2737
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Just one sense, in case of elephant, the sense of touch, becomes the cause of its bondage."
**Summary:** The speaker continues with the example of an elephant being captured through the sense of touch, illustrating how a single uncontrolled sense can lead to bondage.

Patanga. The next, matanga. Matanga refers to, or means the elephant. You know, elephant, huge, untamed elephant, few tons. Now, to tame such an elephant is a Herculean task. Imagine a human being, 50 kg, 60 kg, 70 kg, wants to train a wild elephant, which is few tons. So what they do, they have trained she-elephants, and the trained she-elephant passes by this male elephant, untamed, unruly, wild elephant, male elephant, and touches at a particular spot behind the back of the elephant. Moment the elephant is touched there, it gets irritated, intoxicated, and starts chasing that she-elephant, and there is whole plan laid out. The she-elephant has been trained, the male elephant chases that she-elephant, the she-elephant passes by a pit, a pit which is very deep, very big in size, but covered by bamboo and green grass. So very expertly, the female elephant moves by the side of that pit, and the male elephant, the wild elephant, chasing that she-elephant, comes to that spot, and drops down in that pit. And this man, who is just 6 feet in height, weighing 50 kg, 60 kg, starts taming that elephant. For days together, starving that elephant, beating that elephant, and after a few days, the elephant gives up, becomes tamed, starts obeying the order given by the man. So in this case, elephant, the touch, is the weak link, because of which, wild elephant, weighing few tons, gets captured, and eventually gets tamed. So just like that. So just one sense, in case of elephant, the sense of touch, becomes the cause of its bondage.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### WHO IS A GREAT SOUL? | Bhagavad Gita Verses 9.14 - 9.15 | HG Amitasana Das | HKM Mumbai
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### great soul, spirituality, cheating
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oem-P51ETjo?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "In this world, unfortunately, many people are getting royally cheated in the name of spirituality."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the topic of the great soul (Mahatma) as described in the Bhagavad Gita, highlighting the importance of understanding the characteristics of a genuine spiritual person to avoid being deceived by false God-men.

So, today we will be discussing the 14th and 15th verse from the 9th chapter of the Gita. Here Krishna describes the characteristic of a Mahatma, or a great soul. Who is a great soul? Before we get into this discussion, Srila Prabhupada in the purport of this particular verse explains some very, very important and crucial points, more so in present times. In present times, we see a lot of people claiming to be God, and we see a plethora of God-men around. And we also see a lot of people following different God-men, worshipping them, surrendering to them, sometimes bordering towards blind faith, and at times we see even scandals slamming, and people feeling cheated and left broken-hearted. So, Srila Prabhupada was once asked, have you seen God? As I mentioned in one of our earlier discussions, Srila Prabhupada on different occasions, because this was a very, very commonly asked question, frequently asked question, Srila Prabhupada gave different answers. But one answer which he used to give is that actually it's not a very intelligent question because if I say, yes, I have seen God, in one sense you have no way to verify it. I can always say, yes, I have seen God, I am God, and there's no way of verifying it.

---
#### symptoms, pure gold, spirituality
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oem-P51ETjo?t=404
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Rather than asking, is this gold? If you were to ask me a question, what are the characteristics of pure gold?"
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of gold to explain that instead of asking if someone has seen God, a more intelligent question would be to ask about the symptoms of a person who has seen God, similar to asking about the characteristics of pure gold to avoid being cheated.

Srila Prabhupada corrected that person and said a more intelligent question would be, can anyone help me? What is a more relevant and intelligent question if you want to ask the same very question? How can I see God? Okay, anybody else? How can we reach God? So he said, rather a more intelligent question would be, what are the symptoms of a person who has seen God? Isn't it? It makes sense. Rather than asking, is this gold? And if I am very dead sure that you don't know anything about gold, I can say, yes, this is gold. This is 24 karat gold. Pay me 50 lakh rupees for this. And imagine you don't know anything, and somehow you trust me. There is some element of faith. Who knows, you are wealthy, you may even pay. I can say, yes, this is gold. But the probability of getting cheated is much lesser or blindly believing anything if you were to ask me a question, what are the characteristics of pure gold? How does it look? How can we find out that a particular object is gold? So in this particular verse, Prabhupada explains, or rather Krishna explains, that's how Gita is foolproof in case we have read Gita very thoroughly and understood it. Then we will see that in this world, unfortunately, many people are getting royally cheated in the name of spirituality. People are being given fake gold in the name of gold and people are willing to pay the price.

#### bhakti, sadhana, attachment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oem-P51ETjo?t=543
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Sadhana Bhakti is when we do Bhakti or do some devotional activities without having attachment to the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the two types of Bhakti: Sadhana Bhakti, which is devotional service performed without attachment to the Lord, driven by obedience to higher authorities, and Raganuga Bhakti, which develops from Sadhana Bhakti through the blessings of higher authorities, leading to genuine attraction and attachment to the Lord.

And they are very very attached to the Supreme Lord. Last class, we talked about two kinds of Bhakti. Anyone remembers what are the two types? Sadhana Bhakti and Raganuga Bhakti. Ninth chapter is Krishna talks about Bhakti Yoga. Sadhana Bhakti is, when we do Bhakti or do some devotional activities, devotional activities without having attachment to the Lord. How many of us have genuine attachment to the Lord? Genuine. The right answer would be, we are trying to revive that attachment. Right now, our attachment is, as I mentioned, to so many things. Lord is one of the many things I am attached to. So, in the Sadhana Bhakti stage, our attachment is to so many things, so many people, but still we are doing some devotional activities. It's mainly not driven by attachment to the Lord. It is driven by obedience to superior authorities. I may not relish those activities in the present state. For example, getting up early in the morning, attending the Mangal arti. It may not be very relishable. I may feel like to lie back in the bed and be there in the bed for additional one hour is more relishable, relatively. On a fasting day, to fast on Ekadarshi and not eat anything is not relishable, it's not very pleasing. So initially when we start off, there are many rules and regulations which may not be very palatable, very pleasing to us, but we do it because we have been asked by higher authorities to do it. Just like the doctor, you may give a medicine which is bitter, may not be very tasty, we may not like taking it. I mentioned when I was diagnosed with some deficiency on B12, the doctor said you should take 7 days injection. So I didn't like going to the hospital and taking an injection every day, but the doctor said it's important and I did that. Injection is not going all the way to the hospital, spending time, spending money and then taking the medicine. It's taking a jab, it's painful. Doctor said do it and I have done it. It is for my good. So we have examples how many times we do when somebody who is having a higher understanding on a particular subject, asks us to do a particular thing, we have faith and we do it. So likewise initially when we do devotional activities, it is driven not by attraction to the Lord, but it is driven by obedience to higher authorities. And that bhakti is called Sādhanabhakti. And when we do Sādhanabhakti, because of the obedience to the higher authorities, we get their blessings. In fact, not because of our efforts, because of their blessings, their mercy, because we have endeavored to follow them, follow their instructions in spite of some inconvenience is, by their blessings, what do we get? We get Raga. What is Raga? Attraction and attachment to the Lord. And that Bhakti which starts with Sadhana Bhakti, graduates to Raga Nuga Bhakti.

**Summary:** The speaker continues discussing Sadhana Bhakti, emphasizing the importance of following rules and regulations, practicing Tapasya (voluntarily accepting inconveniences), and striving to reconnect with the Lord through Yoga, acknowledging that our current state is one of separation due to Maya and wrong choices.

Then there is no force. Then it is done with taste. One relishes that. Then we are not in a mood that when will my chanting get completed. Then we want to do more and more. So, right now all of us are in a state of Sadhana Bhakti. It's all about rules and regulations. It is about Tapasya. Tapasya means voluntarily accepting some inconveniences for a higher cause. Getting up early in the morning, not eating particular things. It may be very relishable for the tongue. But it's said don't eat it. So, when we do Sadhana Bhakti, we are blessed because we are following the superior authorities, Acharyas, just like we are following a doctor. When the doctor told me to take injections for seven days, I was blessed in one sense. What way? The deficiency that I had and the symptoms I had, I recovered from that. I regained my health. So, determined to…now, another quality of Sadhana Bhakti… Mahatma is determined to achieve the ultimate end, the association of Supreme Lord, always in association. Right now, in one sense, we are de-linked. Our relationship although is eternal, but we have forgotten that relationship. That's why Yoga means…what is the meaning of Yoga? To link, to connect, to reunite. Bhakti Yoga. Karma Yoga. Jnana Yoga. So, that relationship which is forgotten, which is now severed, who has severed that relationship? Because of whom that relationship has got forgotten? Because of? Because of Maya. We should blame Maya. Whom we should blame? Our senses. Anybody else whom to blame? Our ego. Our ego. Our ego. What else? Myself. Yes. My wrong exercise of free will. Made wrong choices and I have forgotten the Lord. Janiya suniyya vishya khainu. Knowingly I have…knowingly, unknowingly I have drunk poison. One of the Acharyas prays, knowingly, unknowingly I have drunk poison. So we will come to that, what poison he is referring to. He engages all activities, mental, bodily and vocal, everything in the service of the Lord, Shri Krishna, Maam.

Maam means, who is telling this, Lord Krishna is telling. So Mahatma is cent percent, he doesn't have any other part-time business. He is completely cent percent engaged in the service of the Lord. His body, his mind, his senses, his abilities, his talent, understanding that, I am the Lord. Mahatma's great souls strictly observe all the rules and regulations, although they may be great souls, but still, sometimes we have seen examples of so-called God-men, themselves not following some of the rules. And when they were asked, they said that no, this is required in the beginning stages, and once you cross a particular threshold level, then it's all not mandatory. Could be dabbling with women, could be following some particular rules. You can relax on all those things. Initially it is required as stepping stone. Once you reach that level of transcendence, then you can all be liberal. Whereas Mahatmas, although they may be transcendentally situated, example to lead people on the right path they walk the talk in other words their character is pristine and it is not just a show many times people put up a show just like in film we see actors they put up a show they act like a old man they act like a rich man they act like a this thing so you can also put up a show and a gullible man if you know the show is good if a person acts well if he's dressed well it matches with the quality of sadhu it's easy to to for people to believe for example fasting

#### soul, chanting, experience

One person strictly follows all the rules. Now I have a very fundamental question. We were talking about soul, great soul, Mahatma. Bhagavad Gita, Krishna many many places talks about soul. In fact the second chapter starts with the understanding of soul in reply to Arjuna's question, dilemma, confusion. Krishna introduces the concept of a soul. What is a soul? And how many of us have seen a soul? Seen the soul? Felt the soul? Experienced the soul? Seen the soul? Okay, so let me share you on a lighter note. Shila Prabhupada was once asked, Prabhupada when I start chanting, when I chant very feelingly, I can see some you know green light, white light and that you know sparkling some kind of stars and what is this experience is there some kind of a spiritual experience? So Shila Prabhupada being a great acharya he said you continue chanting in the beginning stages you will see all the stars slowly as you continue it will all go away, so in other words let's what is a soul. We have been talking about it, all the sessions have some understanding of soul.

#### death, soul, experience
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oem-P51ETjo?t=1251
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "First time in my life it was a very close encounter with death."
**Summary:** The speaker shares a personal story about the sudden death of a close friend in college, describing the events leading up to his death and the impact it had on him, marking his first close encounter with death.

This reminds me, I briefly covered today morning also, when I was in the final year in my college, the 8th semester, I was I think maybe 21 years that time, I had a very close friend of mine, we were staying together, we studied together, same batch, staying in the same hostel, his room was in the vicinity, close to my room and we used to spend lot of time together. Not one of my closest friend but yes, very close. So one night, generally after having dinner, we used to go for a walk and then we had dinner together. And I asked him, let's go for a walk. He said, no, today I am not feeling well, I am feeling feverish, let me take rest, I am feeling very tired, feverish. So I said okay. So his room was on the way, so I left him in the room, I said, need any help, please call me and then he lied down on the bed and then I continued, I went for a walk, we had some other friends also. And when we came back, one of the friends told me that he has, looks like, his health is not good, he is having lot of fever. So then we quickly arranged for a thermometer, went and checked and by that time he had, you know started some kind of shivering, so we could make out that no, temperature is there, maybe fear has risen and then as usual when somebody become sick, so people start, you know, okay, let's get this, let's get that, let's try to do this, should we call a doctor and then we said, no, it's normal fever. So one of them suggested. So let's give him some medicine. some crocin, paracetamol and it will subside. Let him take rest. Maybe he is tired, maybe he is sick or whatever. So we left him at that and then we gave him crocin and one of them who was attending to him in fact realized that the fever is not subsiding. When they checked it was around 103 degrees. So then one of them suggested let's get some ice pads and let's see if we can help him with that. So they got that also. And then it was later late in the night, about 10.30 in the night and then some of us went back to our rooms and some of them were there with him. His roommates were there. And then we got a news around 12.30, 1 in the night that he has died. Imagine 8 o'clock you had dinner with a person, he was all okay. Feels feverish, complains of some kind of uneasiness. 9.30, 10 we saw him you know shivering. Gave him paracetamol, gave him few things and 1 o'clock he leaves his body. So we were all called. In fact the entire hostel woke up, the word spread and then everyone gathered. So first time in my life, although I have heard about death, first time in my life it was a very close encounter with death. And somebody whom you have met, you know conversed with just 2 hours, couple of hours before. So then quickly we called the ambulance and then we rushed him to the hospital. Many of us were not sure even because it was first encounter with death, whether he is unconscious, he is in coma. Somebody was saying he is declared dead and somebody is saying that no let's take him to the hospital. We took him to the hospital and it was confirmed that he is dead, he has died.

#### fear, soul, death
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oem-P51ETjo?t=1482
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "What has happened to him that is arising fear?"
**Summary:** The speaker continues the story, describing the aftermath of his friend's death, the fear he experienced being near the body, and reflecting on the question of what had changed in the person that caused such fear, leading to the realization that it was the absence of the soul.

So then I was there at 1.30, 2 o'clock in the night and then there was utter commotion there and then people trying to get hold of his numbers so that parents can be called. So some of them were calling the parents and we were all there. And since it was 1.30, 2 in the night, so nothing can happen at that time, post-mortem and few other things, whatever formalities were there, has to happen the next day. So some of us were to stay back in the hospital. I was there in the hospital. We were supposed to stay in the room next to the room where this body was kept. It was about 1.32 in the night. Some people were told to go back to the college, go back to the hostel, go and sleep and only few of us, 4-5 of us were there in the hospital next morning all the thing and others were busy doing, calling the parents and few like things. And then I was supposed to sleep in the room. to, not sleep, be in the room next to and I was very, very fearful because I had seen some horror movies and had some idea of ghosts and all those things. I was very, very fearful. Can anyone tell me what was the cause of fear? Here was a person, four hours back I was dabbling with him, we have played together, we have conversed together, we have laughed together, we have stayed together, we have enjoyed each other's company. Why is it that the same person lying next to me, I am fearful? Why? One thing was, what if in the middle of night he gets up? You know, the body is lying there. So what is it that? It was a first very close encounter with death. What is it that this person was there with us? What has happened to him that now we are fearful of him? The same person who was so loving, so we used to be, you know, always in his company, now the same person is lying there and we are fearful. What has happened to that person, that same person who was so loving, so nice, now there is some element of fear being in his. Sometimes we say, without you I cannot live. In fact, when a person dies with that person, with that body, we cannot live. What has happened? We say gujar gya wo. He has left the body. The body is there. What has left? The soul. And because of the presence of the soul, that person was alive, was chirpy, was bubbling, was talking, was walking, everything. And moment the soul leaves the body, the body is lying there with no emotions, with no feelings. Next day in the morning, we try as far as possible, call all the relatives and cremate.

---
#### soul, evidence, mind
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oem-P51ETjo?t=1665
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "The person is gone. So, who is gone is soul, the atma, the real self, which Krishna is talking about."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the soul, or atma, is what leaves the body at death, and addresses the question of evidence for the existence of the soul, comparing it to the existence of the mind, which cannot be seen but is understood through its symptoms.

That same body which has been so royally taken care of is burnt. If we analyze chemically, all the chemicals which constitute the body are still there. But we say the person is gone. So, who is gone is soul, the atma, the real self, which Krishna is talking about. Now, somebody may say, we all students of science, we have grown up with scientific temperament. We want evidence, we want proof. We don't want to believe things blindly. We don't want to subscribe to things, something which is just as a matter of belief or faith. So, can someone... Question, what is the evidence? Okay, you are talking about soul. What is the evidence soul exists? Can you show me soul? If you show me soul, then I believe. So, many things in this world we cannot see, but we believe. For example, if I were to ask you, do you have a mind? How many of us have a mind? How many of us have intelligence? If I were to ask you, can you show me your mind? I am not talking about grey substance, the brain, the grey matter, that physical brain. I am not talking about that. So, mind, Krishna says, is sukshma. Sukshma means subtle. Sukshma sharira, manas, buddhi and ahankara, mind, intelligence and false ego. They are sukshma. They are subtle. They are not gross. Gross body is panchabhuta, earth, air, water, fire and ether. When a person dies, what we cremate is the sthula sharira, gross body. So, it is said that mind is subtler than the body. Suttler than the mind is intelligence. Even subtler and finer than the intelligence is ego. And even sukshma, finer than the ego is the soul. Now, as things become more finer and finer and subtler, the body we can see with our gross eyes. Yes, that person we are relating with day to day, we are seeing the gross body. But as it becomes finer and finer, we cannot perceive with our gross senses, but we can perceive the presence of mind, presence of intelligence, presence of ego, presence of soul through the symptoms.

---
#### consciousness, soul, reincarnation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oem-P51ETjo?t=1836
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "The soul can be perceived by its symptom: consciousness."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the presence of the soul is perceived through its symptom, consciousness, which is absent in a dead body, and quotes verses from the Bhagavad Gita to illustrate that the soul is eternal and transmigrates from one body to another like changing clothes.

How do we know the mind is present? Sometime we say face is the index of mind. When a person is frowning, we say why are you so disturbed today? Why your mind is so disturbed? Seeing the face, why are you so anxious? Why are you so anxious? Or somebody is very quick in grasping things, quickly you tell him something and he is able to grasp, he is able to give an answer, he says you are very intelligent, brilliant. So, seeing the symptoms, we can say that intelligence is there, mind is there. Likewise, the soul can be perceived by its symptom. What is the symptom of a soul? Consciousness. A dead body does not have consciousness. Because of consciousness, we perceive pleasure and pain. If there is a pinprick in our finger, we immediately feel pain. We rush for some kind of a first aid. Whereas a dead body, there is no experiencer. You put it on a funeral pyre, light the fire, the body is lying there. The experiencer, the kshetragana, the knower of the field of activities, the soul has gone. And because the soul has gone, the symptom of presence of soul is consciousness. Therefore, there is no consciousness. So, this soul, Krishna is saying, is ajo nitya shashvato ayam purano na hanyate hanyamane sharire. When hanyamane, when the sharira, when the body dies, when a person dies, na hanyate, it does not die. What doesn't die? The soul. Vasamshi jeernani yatha vihaya navani granati naroparani. Just like we give up old dress and accept new dress. Suppose my shirt is torn, next day I wear another shirt. I change this dress and wear another dress. Likewise, vasamshi jeernani, jeernani means old, when the body becomes old and it is not fit for the soul to continue its journey, jeernani yatha vihaya, we give up old garments, accept navani granati, navani means new, granati means to accept. Tatha, tatha, exactly like the sharirani vihaya, sharir means the body, vihaya means to give up. Navani granati naroparani, a soul accepts another body.

#### ego, identity, ignorance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oem-P51ETjo?t=2004
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "False ego means false identity."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the concept of reincarnation, emphasizing that the soul is eternal and the body is like a dress, and that identifying with the body leads to false ego and ignorance, causing attachment, pain, and agony, while a wise person is not bewildered by the change of bodies.

Now let's visualize what is the overall scheme of things. The soul which is eternal, never dying and this body is like a dress which the soul is wearing. Because I am wearing a dress which is male, man, born in India, I call myself Indian and believe that I am Indian. Actually that's not my real identity. Because next life, the same soul accepts another body, let's say in Pakistan and in some other maybe gender and I call myself that. India is my enemy. So that is the meaning of ego. Ego means identity. False ego means false identity. Dehatma buddhi, this buddhi, this understanding, this identity that I am the body is the fundamental ignorance. And that's why to break that ignorance, Krishna introduces that you are a spirit soul. When we are in bodily concept of life, invariably there will be attachment, there will be pain, there will be agony. And when we come out of this bodily concept of life, then Krishna says, dhirastatranam huyati, a sober person, a wise person is not bewildered by such a change. Sometimes we hear of some very near and dear one suffering from some terminal disease, cancer or whatever. It completely rattles a person. No doubt, yes, we have attachments. Attachments have a place. But it completely shatters a person. Krishna says, dhirastatranam huyati, a sober person, a wise person is not bewildered by such a change.

#### reincarnation, sukshma sharira, karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oem-P51ETjo?t=2175
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Soul with the sukshma sharira accepts another body."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that in reincarnation, the soul is covered by the sukshma sharira (mind, intelligence, and ego) and carries it into another body, which accounts for innate abilities, talents, inclinations, phobias, and skills.

We were talking about destiny, what is destined, what is not destined. We will cover that, I think it requires little more detailed explanation. So what happens is, I understood your question. So in reincarnation where a person remembers his past life experiences, what happens is, the soul is covered by sukshma sharira. Sukshma sharira is a concept of manas, buddhi and ahankara. So this soul with the sukshma sharira goes into another body. That's why we have born with some abilities, some talents, some inclinations, some phobias, some skills. Where is it coming from? Some people have artistic skills. Some people have auditory skills. I was telling you about child prodigy. There is a girl. She is 15 years old, plays some 15-20 instruments, musical instruments. Can play tabla, can play harmonium, can play violin, can play so many things. And I can't play even one of them after 25 years. I don't have that sense. Sachin Tendulkar joined the Indian team at the age of 16. Where did he get that skill from? Some phobias, some fears. Where did it come from? So soul with the sukshma sharira accepts another body. With the sukshma sharira. That's where our vasanas are there. That's why imprints are there.

#### karma, human birth, self-realization
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oem-P51ETjo?t=2273
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Now that you have got human birth, try to inquire."
**Summary:** The speaker provides an overview of the cycle of birth and death, explaining that the soul takes many lives in different forms based on karma and desire, and that human birth offers the opportunity to understand the purpose of creation and realize the self.

So now let's give the overall picture. Overall picture is what? Soul is eternal and life after life. We have taken many many lives. Life after life it is accepting a body and then it develops different kinds of attachments, relationships, friends, brother, father, sister, all this kind of relationship. And then again dies. And then again it accepts based on its karma. Again repeats the same process. Again dies. Again accepts another body. Punarapi janma, punarapi maran. And sometime as an animal. Sometime as a plant. Sometime as a bird. Sometime as a reptile. Basis are karma. Basis are desire. If my desire is to eat flesh, I will be awarded a body which is conducive for fulfilment of that desire. And after many many lives, the soul gets an opportunity to get the birth of a human being. And in human birth, the soul has the opportunity to get the birth of a human being. The soul has an opportunity to understand what is all this. Who am I? Who is God? What is the purpose of this creation? Athato brahma jignasa. Now that you have got human birth, try to inquire. If these fundamental questions are not asked and understood, then we have wasted this human birth, which has that ability to inquire and realise the self. And by following this sadhana bhakti, raganuma bhakti and various other processes,

#### mukti, temporary solutions, fear
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oem-P51ETjo?t=2387
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "All the solutions what we are looking for in this world are temporary stopgap solutions."
**Summary:** The speaker states that all worldly solutions are temporary and that the ultimate goal is to break free from the cycle of birth and death through mukti or moksha, and revisits the story of his friend's death, emphasizing the fear that arose from the absence of the soul.

the soul can extricate itself from the cycle of birth and death and that is called mukti or moksha. All the solutions what we are looking for in this world are temporary stopgap solutions. All the advancement we are making, maybe we can prolong. If a person has heart attack, maybe with latest of medical interventions, we can prolong life by another 5 years. But the original problem of repeated birth and death cannot be solved. In fact, at times, with all the advancement, it has accelerated the process. I told you, because what if he wakes up in the night? And it can be unfounded also. I am not saying it was rational. Any dead body, if you have close in the vicinity, you will be fearful. So that made me think, what is it that the same person is lying there, he was very close friend of mine, why I am fearful of him? What has happened to him that is arising fear? It was a question in my mind. Why is it that same person whom we say, I cannot live without you, the same person, I cannot live with him now. I am fearful of living with him, living in the next room, why? Because soul has left. It is a dead body. Yes.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### WHO IS DEAR TO GOD?  | Bhagavad Gita 12.16- 12.20 | HG Amitasana Das | HKM Mumbai
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### devotion, love of God, Bhagavad Gita
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-Zp1jWkwYFo?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Do not try to see the Lord, act in a way that the Lord sees you."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa introduces the topic of who is dear to Krishna, based on the last verses of the twelfth chapter of the Gita, explaining that the lecture will cover verses sixteen to twentieth.

The topic is, who is dear to Krishna? This is the last few verses, last six verses of the twelfth chapter of the Gita where Krishna is saying, who is dear to me? How many of us want to be dear to the Lord? Not that kind of, Bhagwan ko piyare ho gaye ho. So it is used in the connotation of, he has reached God, he has died. So Krishna is saying, they say, do not try to see the Lord, act in a way that the Lord sees you. Now Krishna is giving his opinion, who is very dear to me? What can we do, what kind of person we can become or be, which will be very, very pleasing to Krishna. He will be very happy with us and such kind of person is very dear to him. So this is the discussion which we are going to have today, who is dear to God? We are covering sixteenth to twentieth verse of the twelfth chapter and this ends the twelfth chapter.
