## HG Amitasana Dasa

### What is your next birth? |  BG 8 05 - 8.08 | HG Amitasana Das | HKM Mumbai
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### death, consciousness, spiritual body
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/kuAqgkyAWu8?t=0
**Verse References:** BG 8.5
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The real success is if you could leave this body as a self-realized soul remembering the Supreme Lord."
**Summary:** This segment explains that one's consciousness at the time of death determines their next life, and remembering the Lord leads to a spiritual body and liberation.

So, this is text 5 from the 8th chapter of the Gita. Translation: "And whoever at the time of death quits his body, remembering me alone, at once attains my nature, of this there is no doubt." So, we had discussed in our previous session, we all have an understanding of what it means to be successful, what is real success, when can we say that we have led this human life meaningfully. So here the Lord is saying, the real success is if you could leave this body as a self-realized soul remembering the Supreme Lord. We all are bound and entangled in this material world with various attachments, with different kinds of desires, different attachments. So if we have led a life whereby we have got purified of all those desires, developed right kinds of attachment, then it is possible that at point of death we remember the Lord with a focused mind. Translation: "And whoever at the time of death quits his body, remembering me alone, at once attains my nature, of this there is no doubt." And if we do that, whatever our state of mind, our consciousness at the point of death, that determines our next life. The 84 lakh species of life, plants, birds, aquatics, reptiles, different kinds of people, so based on our desires we get appropriate body. The soul gets appropriate body. So if we have material desires, we get a material body, an appropriate body for the fulfillment of those desires. And if we have spiritual desires, purified desires, we are thinking of the Lord, we get a spiritual body. And we come out of this material existence, this material bondage, this cycle of birth, death, old age and disease, which is also called Mukti or Moksha. So here the Lord is saying, one who quits his body, remembering me at once, attains my nature. My nature means spiritual nature. Of this there is no doubt.

---
#### practice, Krishna consciousness, struggle
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/kuAqgkyAWu8?t=191
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This process of Krishna Consciousness is we have to cultivate the remembrance of the Lord."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the importance of practicing Krishna consciousness to cultivate remembrance of the Lord, comparing it to the practice of athletes and highlighting the struggle involved in pulling the mind back from material attachments.

Whatever state of being one remembers when he quits his body, that state he will attain without fail. So if we are too conscious of, attached to a particular object, or a particular thing, or a particular person, or a particular desire, then accordingly we get another body to fulfill those desires. Therefore, Arjuna, you should always think of me. This process of Krishna Consciousness is we have to cultivate the remembrance of the Lord. Suddenly the remembrance will not come at the point of death. They say practice makes a man perfect. Just like cricketers, we see before a match they are practicing. They are on the nets. Practice is going on. There is a coach who is teaching the people how to bat, how to ball for a normal material thing. So likewise we have to practice this chanting what we do. It's a practice. Mind is not willing to remember the Lord. Mind is absorbed in so many things. But by force we try to pull the mind back, pull the mind back, pull the mind back. Mind is attached to so many things. Not this, this, not this, this. And then if we have sufficiently practiced, if we have got purified, then it will be seamless, effortless to remember the Lord. And that's why you are saying therefore, because that's the end goal, therefore Arjuna you should always think of me in the form of Krishna and at the same time carry out your prescribed duty of fighting. There is a struggle. Remembrance is not easy. If we have struggled to remember the Lord while we are in this material existence, then at the point of death, which will be a painful moment, we have to leave everything and go. There is a good possibility that we will be able to remember the Lord. Sometimes people say, life is a lot of struggle. Yes, while we are struggling, we have to strive. We have to practice remembering the Lord. When it is difficult to remember the Lord, we have to remember.

---
#### sadhana bhakti, taste, service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/kuAqgkyAWu8?t=331
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We have to learn the art, how what we are doing can be done as a service to the Lord."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the concept of sadhana bhakti as a process to cultivate a taste for remembering the Lord, using the analogy of sugarcane juice tasting bitter to someone with jaundice, and emphasizes performing one's duties as a service to the Lord.

So acharyas what they have given, knowing that it is not easy for us to remember the Lord always, they have given us a process of sadhana bhakti. Sadhana bhakti means it is not spontaneous, but involves rules and regulations. Even if you don't like, do this much. When a person is having jaundice, he doesn't like sugar. It looks, it tastes bitter. But the doctor says, please take sugarcane juice. When the jaundice, the person recovers, the same sugarcane juice becomes sweet. So right now when we start, because we have no taste for the Lord's name, we have taste for various material things, this holy name, chanting of the holy name, remembering of the Lord will look very, not normal, tasteless, not easy. It will be painful. There is no maja. There is no taste because we haven't cultivated that taste. So when I was in college, all along, you know, when we were young, so we were Gold Spot, Fanta, those cold drinks used to be there and so typically we knew that, okay, Gold Spot, if it was 5 rupees a bottle and then, you know, this was relatively very expensive. So, as a child, what comes in your mind? You think it's much more sweeter, isn't it? Gold Spot is sweet, so if it is more expensive, it will be much more sweeter, much more tastier. That's why people are paying higher price. So first time, when I was in the final year, somebody just somehow said, so I thought, okay, all along, so long, let me just, and it was so bitter, so bitter. There's no taste. Those people who have developed the taste, they would hanker for it. They want it, they love it, they like it. So at the same time, carry out your prescribed duty of fighting with your activities dedicated to me. We have to learn the art, how what we are doing can be done as a service to the Lord.

---
#### mind, focus, desire
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/kuAqgkyAWu8?t=490
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Something which you should, which is really worthwhile aspiring for is this, while you are doing your prescribed duty, thinking of me, keeping your mind and intelligence fixed on me."
**Summary:** This segment concludes by emphasizing the importance of fixing one's mind and intelligence on the Lord while performing prescribed duties, highlighting that this is a worthwhile aspiration and that material desires can deviate one from this path.

And your mind and intelligence fixed on me. You will attain me, without doubt. So this is what we should be, the Lord is giving us what we should be aspiring for. What is the project for us? We all have some goals, some aspirations, something which we are wanting to do. So here the Lord is saying, something which you should, which is really worthwhile aspiring for is this, while you are doing your prescribed duty, thinking of me, keeping your mind and intelligence fixed on me. Okay, you have to get your work done. And I'm going to tell you the way. Do the last three sentences, and then go somewhere else. And my help should be in a fixing of my mind and focus. Why? Because I believe that your mind is moving on something which makes you keep. You tell me that by focusing on what you are depressed doing… You will income yourself and others, anything and everything from the path, we get deviated because we have a lot of material desires. We come here on Sunday and go somewhere else from Monday to Friday. Yes, because we have different desires.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### What makes Powerful People Powerful? | BG 7.08- 7.12 | HG Amitasana Das
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### Krishna's energies, perception of God, taste of water
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_ZPaXSqTG4c?t=0
**Verse References:** BG 7.08
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Everything we see in this world around us is nothing but the Lord and His energies."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the verse and explains that everything is the Lord's energy, emphasizing the taste of water as a way to perceive Krishna.

So today we are discussing from the seventh chapter, text number eight to twelve, based on the teachings of His Divine Grace AC Bhaktivedanta Swami Shri Prabhupada. Let's chant this verse together. Please repeat after me. This is the eighth verse from the seventh chapter: raso 'ham apsu kaunteya prabhasmi sasi surye pranava sarva vedesho shabdakhe paurushum. Translation: O son of Kunti, I am the taste of water, the light of the Sun and the moon, the syllable Om in the Vedic mantras. I am the sound in ether and ability in man. These are the words spoken to Arjun. So he is saying, O son of Kunti, Arjun, I am the taste of water, the light of the Sun and the moon, the syllable Om in the Vedic mantras. I am the sound in the ether and ability in man. Now we discussed in the previous session, everything we see in this world around us is nothing but the Lord and His energies.

---
#### perception of God, senses, Krishna consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_ZPaXSqTG4c?t=98
**Verse References:** BG 7.08
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "When we drink water, the taste of water, the pure taste of water, the unadulterated water, that is Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on perceiving Krishna through the senses, particularly the taste of water, and how remembering Krishna while drinking water is a form of remembrance.

The energies are broadly speaking two categories: material and spiritual. Now the taste of water is also one of the energies of the Lord. In fact, Prabhupada in one place says we have five knowledge acquiring senses, right? What are the five knowledge acquiring senses? Eyes, ears, nose, tongue, and skin. So a lot of people say, can you show me God? In other words, they want a Pramand and evidence which they can acquire through their sense of seeing, right? Through sense of seeing they want an evidence that God exists. So Prabhupada says, why only the sense of seeing? Sense of seeing is one of the five senses. Just like we are interested in seeing God, why are we not interested in tasting God? Why are we not interested in hearing God? I am sure you would have not heard the statement, a lot of people say, can you show me God? You would have not heard the statement, can I hear God? Can I hear God? You would not hear that statement. Can I taste God? Then I will believe in God. So Prabhupada says, when we drink water, the taste of water, the pure taste of water, the unadulterated water, that is Krishna. In one place Prabhupada says, how do we perceive Krishna in our daily lives? We all drink water. If we remember the taste of water is Krishna and when we drink water and we remember that this taste is Krishna, it is also one form of remembrance of Krishna.

---
#### impersonal vs personal, gratitude, natural resources
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_ZPaXSqTG4c?t=237
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "All the natural resources which we have taken for granted, sunlight, moon, water, air, it's all coming from the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the impersonal and personal conceptions of God, emphasizing gratitude for natural resources provided by the Lord.

So impersonalists who believe that ultimately Lord is energy, they say the taste of water is one of the energies of the Lord, which is true. Prabhupada, when he was asked, can you show me God? He said, why lay so much emphasis on seeing? Why not on hearing God? Why not on tasting? Now the personalists who believe that ultimately God is a person, they glorify the Lord, not only for the taste of water, but for his kindness in supplying water to quench our thirst. Mind you, all these resources, air, water, which we all have taken for granted, is not created by man. In fact, with so much of rains coming, they say that we are 90% we should be able to manage our water requirement. Imagine a case where one year there is not enough rainfall, which is not in the government's hand. Assuming it's 50% of the expected monsoon, it will be chaos. So we should be grateful. All the natural resources which we have taken for granted, sunlight, moon, water, air, it's all coming from the Lord. One in nine people around the world do not have access to safe and clean drinking water. Many times we get vexed up, I don't have this, I don't have that. If we are a devotee, invariably we will be grateful to the Lord and have an attitude of gratitude.

---
#### consciousness, gratitude, impersonal vs personal, chanting
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_ZPaXSqTG4c?t=350
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The more we elevate, we realize how much Lord has given us, how much He has done for us."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the relationship between consciousness, gratitude, and the Lord, then transitions to the topic of chanting Om versus Hare Krishna.

A devotee always considers that, Lord, you have given me so much. Only when we have turned away from God, when we have dropped in our consciousness, how many levels of consciousness are there in human form? Four lakh. The more we drop in our consciousness, the more we become ungrateful. The more we elevate, we realize how much Lord has given us, how much He has done for us. And we feel that we are so ungrateful. One who knows God knows that the impersonal conception and personal conception are simultaneously present. The example is given, just like Sun. Krishna in the Gita talks about Sun God. What is the name of Sun God? Vivaswan. So there is Sun God, there is Sun globe, the Sun planet, and there is Sunshine. Sunshine is the energy of the Sun. It's emanating from the Sun. You cannot have Sunshine without Sun. Am I right? So likewise, impersonal energy is emanating from God, who ultimately is a person, which Krishna in the Gita is saying, it is me. So one school of thought called impersonal is to say that ultimately there is some supreme power. Ultimately there is supreme energy. But Krishna here in the Gita says, that is me. It is all emanating from me. I am the taste of water. I am the Pranava Omkara. A lot of people chant Om. Is there anything wrong in chanting Om? Yes? No? Loudly? No.

---
#### chanting, impersonal vs personal, Om, Hare Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_ZPaXSqTG4c?t=471
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna says, I am Omkara. I am Pranava Omkara."
**Summary:** The speaker addresses the question of chanting Om versus Hare Krishna, explaining that Krishna identifies with Om but that there are different aspects of the Lord.

So somebody may say, Swamiji, I will chant Om. I feel much better chanting Om. Somehow Hare Krishna Mantra is too complex. It has become very lengthy. Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna. Why not just chant Om? And Krishna says, I am Omkara. I am Pranava Omkara. So what is the difference? He will say, this is what I wanted to ask you. You are asking me. It is an impersonal form. So what is impersonal form? Okay. So what is wrong with... Nothing wrong. In fact, Pranava Omkara we can chant. Nothing wrong with chanting of Om. But let us understand what is the difference between Hare Krishna Mantra and the chanting of Om. So Lord is all pervading everywhere. We say He is omnipresent in the form of Om. He is omnipresent in the form of His energy, which is also called Brahman. In the example of sun, it is the sunshine. When the sunshine enters our house, we say the sun has come. Yes or no?

---
#### impersonal vs personal, Brahman, Paramatma, Bhagavan, chanting
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_ZPaXSqTG4c?t=593
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Absolute Truth or Lord has three features: the impersonal feature which is Brahman, the localized feature which is present in everyone's heart, which Krishna says Sarvasya Chaham Hrithi Sanyushta, Paramatma, and the Supreme Personality of Godhead, Bhagwan."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the three features of the Absolute Truth: Brahman, Paramatma, and Bhagavan, and how Hare Krishna mantra represents the personal form of the Lord.

If sun actually comes inside your house, we will get Nirvana. So sunshine comes. So in that sense, it is coming from the sun. It is energy of the sun. Sunshine. We say the sun has come. And then we have sun planet, which is equivalent to the localized aspect of Lord which is called Paramatma feature. So impersonal feature of the Lord is called Brahman. The localized aspect of the Lord which is present in the heart of the Lord, which is present in the heart of everyone is called Paramatma, which is like the sun planet, the localized. And the ultimate aspect of the Supreme Lord is called Bhagwan, the Supreme Personality of Godhead. Just like in case of sun, it is Vivaswan. So the Absolute Truth or Lord has three features. Kindly hear carefully. What are the three features? The impersonal feature which is Brahman. The localized feature which is present in everyone's heart, which Krishna says Sarvasya Chaham Hrithi Sanyushta. What is that feature called? Everyone loudly. Paramatma. And the Supreme Personality of Godhead, Bhagwan. All three are representation of the Lord only. Just like sunshine, sun globe and sun planet and the sun God. They all are same thing. There is no question of sunshine without sun planet. But it is an energy emanating from the Lord. So likewise Pranava Omkara is the sound representation of the Lord, impersonal sound representation of the Lord. Whereas Hare Krishna Mantra is representing the personal form of the Lord. Sakshat Dharitvena. It is Lord himself incarnated in the form of sound, especially in this age of Kali. Krishna, Rama. So nothing wrong in chanting the holy names of... Sorry chanting Pranava Omkara which is an impersonal representation of the Lord.

---
#### Krishna's energies, life, divine intervention
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_ZPaXSqTG4c?t=724
**Verse References:** BG 7.09
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "You may have the best of doctors, the best of medicines but the Lord if he is not ready to protect that person he cannot be saved."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses Krishna as the original fragrance, heat, and life, and how the Lord can alter the duration of one's life, even with the best medical care.

Text 9. I am the original fragrance of the earth and I am the heat in fire. I am the light. I am the life of all the lives and I am the penances of all aesthetics. Everything original has some smell, some fragrance and some taste. And Krishna is saying I am that. The heat in the fire is Krishna. In our bellies there is fire, Agni which digests the food products. Food we eat metabolizes this thing. It is also energy of the Lord. The durations of man's life is also due to Krishna. Here in the verse he says the life. No doubt we say that when we take birth our duration of life is fixed. The number of breaths which we will take is fixed. But the Lord can change that. Mare Krishna Rakheke. Rakhe Krishna Mareke. We have examples of... In fact Prahlad Maharaj says in the Bhagavatam, you may have the best of doctors, the best of medicines but the Lord if he is not ready to protect that person he cannot be saved. Whereas we have examples of people who are in a very precarious situation but they have survived. I gave example of a building which collapsed. After six days they found two brothers surviving.

---
#### intelligence, power, karma, surrender
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_ZPaXSqTG4c?t=826
**Verse References:** BG 7.10
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Whatever we think is my ability, today if I am able to speak well, or if I have some power, it is given to me by the Lord and one day it will be taken away."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that intelligence and power come from the Lord according to our karma, and the best use of these abilities is to offer them back to the Lord.

Text 10. O son of Pritha, know that I am the original seed of all existences, the intelligence of the intelligent and the prowess of powerful men. We see lot of powerful men, lot of intelligent men in this world. This intelligence, prowess, power is bestowed by the Lord. According to what? Why everyone has different intelligence, different power, different prowess, different abilities? Why? According to our karma. As you sow, so shall you reap. Krishna says I am equal to everyone. Samoham surve bhuteshu naame dveshi stina priyaha. So each one of us have attracted some abilities, some talents, some power, some wealth, some fame, some position. It's a cumulative effect of the actions we have done in this life and over many many lifetimes. But this ability is coming from the Lord. Whatever we think is my ability, today if I am able to speak well, or if I have some power, it is given to me by the Lord and one day it will be taken away. If I am beautiful today, one day that beauty will go. I am just a Kshetrajna. Kshetrajna means knower of the field of these activities. Kshetra and Kshetrajna. Kshetra is this body. And Kshetrajna is the knower of the field of the body. I am a spirit soul, in one sense you can call it. It possesses nothing. Material nature under the direction of the Lord provides us this body with certain abilities, certain talents, certain skills for a while, according to our karma and under the influence of Kala, it is withdrawn back. So Acharya says, so long as we have some ability, some power, we should recognize that it is coming from the Lord and the best use of that power, that ability, that wealth, that strength, whatever we have, is to offer it back to the Lord, to serve the Lord with that.

---
#### detachment, surrender, spiritual asset, intelligence, power
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_ZPaXSqTG4c?t=977
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When I give that money, when I serve the Lord with that money, that money no longer is material, it becomes a spiritual asset, which becomes a permanent credit of the soul."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of surrendering to the Lord, as everything we have will eventually be taken away, but offering it to the Lord creates a permanent spiritual asset, and that even the most powerful people lose their power over time.

In Hindi you say, Khaali aat aaye the, khaali aat jaayenge. We all know this sentence, but to realize it, realize it. Whatever I have, it belongs to the Lord. Why Acharya says we have to give, we have to surrender, we have to offer? Because whatever we have, it will go away. But when I give it to the Lord, it becomes my permanent credit. It becomes a spiritual asset. If today, if I have some money in the bank, any way it has to go, I will be separated. Either the money will go or you will go away from the one. But when I give that money, when I serve the Lord with that money, that money no longer is material, it becomes a spiritual asset, which becomes a permanent credit of the soul. So Krishna is saying, I am the seed-giving father. Sarve yoni shukaunteya aham bija pradapita. We have 84 lakhs yonia, species, plants, birds, aquatics. Krishna is saying, I am the seed-giving father. I am the intelligence of the intelligent. The intelligence which the intelligent people have is large. Sometimes we get very, he is so intelligent, he is so brilliant. You know, he has done this. Actually, Krishna says, it is just a spark of my splendour. Prowess of all powerful men. We are talking about power, position, fame, beauty. We all have been blessed with. It doesn't last for long. This slide gives us an indication that whatever we have with Kala, it will go away. Very, very influential people who have lot of money, lot of influence, lot of power, lot of fame. With Kala, it deteriorates. And these are all not ordinary people. They are stars. Stars.

---
#### detachment, humility, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_ZPaXSqTG4c?t=1131
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Humility is a natural derivative when a person becomes a devotee of the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that our abilities and talents are not truly ours, as they are subject to time and the Lord's will, and that true humility comes from devotion to the Lord.

So this should give us an idea that such powerful people also. Why do we say it is not ours? Because if it was ours, if it was mine, it should be at my beck and call whenever I want. If you say it is my house, your house, your permanent house, you can stay wherever you want. You can do some kind of alterations in the house because it is your house. If you are a tenant, you cannot do that. It is at your beck and call. You can do whatever you want with your house. You can change the flooring. You can maybe, whatever is permissible. You can build additional wall. You can change the fittings. Then you say it is my house. But if you see our abilities, our talent, it is not at our beck and call. We want to remain young. We want our hairs to remain black. But time, Kala, will change everything. It will change everything. So this has been given to us by the Lord for a while, according to our karma. When we recognize that I am just an insignificant Jeeva, a Kshetra Gna, the Kshetra belongs to the Lord, ability belongs to the Lord, the power I have belongs to the Lord, then we don't become proud. Lot of people say humility counts. We should be humble. Humility is a natural derivative. You don't have to psych yourself up. It is a natural derivative of when a person becomes a devotee of the Lord.

---
#### strength, desire, religious principles, procreation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_ZPaXSqTG4c?t=1230
**Verse References:** BG 7.11
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The strong man's strength should be applied to protect the weak, not for personal aggression."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the proper use of strength and the role of sex life within religious principles, emphasizing procreation as its primary purpose.

In fact, true humility comes only when a person becomes a devotee. Otherwise, it is at a mental level. Text 11. I am strength of the strong, devoid of passion and desire. I am sex which is not contrary to religious principles. O Lord of Bharata, Arjuna. The strong man's strength should be applied to protect the weak, not for personal aggression. We have some strength. We have some ability. We should use that, not for selfish aggrandizement, for serving God, serving mankind. Abilities to be used in service of God and mankind because your life has a purpose. Similarly, sex life according to religious principles, Dharma should be for procreation of children and not otherwise. We have examples of great sages who practice celibacy. Lot of people sometimes ask, is sex wrong? Does it not have a place in the society? Why do we restrain ourselves from it? Why not be a grahastha, get married and let it be a part of life? Yes, actually it's part of life. In fact, there is an ashram called Grahastha Ashram wherein a bona fide way, as per religious principle, for procreation it should be practiced. It is called illicit when this purpose, procreation, propagation is just for the sake of enjoyment. And Krishna says, dharma aviruddho, which is not against religious principles, I am that sex life.

Now here we have, 90% of children aged 8 to 16 have viewed porn. Porn sites comprise 12% of internet users. So it's all agitation of mind, the senses. Infidelity has increased over the years. And that's because the dharmic principles have gradually got eroded. The understanding of dharma has got eroded. Life has just become eat, drink, be merry, enjoy life. Who has seen the next life? The understanding of dharma, understanding of soul, understanding of how the whole thing is, what is the ultimate purpose, it's all completely lost. And all these things become prevalent in the society.

#### modes of nature, nirguna, transcendence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_ZPaXSqTG4c?t=1406
**Verse References:** BG 7.12
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna here is saying, in one sense, I am everything."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Krishna is independent of the modes of material nature (sattva, rajas, tamas), and that He is nirguna, unaffected by these modes.

Text 12. Know that all states of being, be they of goodness, passion or ignorance, are manifested by my energy. I am in one sense everything, but I am independent. I am not under the modes of material nature, for they, on the contrary, are within me. What are the three modes of material nature? Sattva, rajas and tamas. This is also called three modes of material nature. So Krishna here is saying, in one sense, everything, I am in one sense everything, which we also discussed. Everything is nothing but the Lord and His energies. What is the meaning of nirguna? Which means that these gunas or modes, although coming from Him, do not affect Him. You would have heard this word nirgun brahman. Have you heard this word nirgun brahman? That means the Lord is not affected by the gunas. We are affected by gunas. Sometimes we are in raja guna, sometimes we are in tamo guna, sometimes we are in sattva guna. And these modes influence us, influence our behaviour, our thinking, our feeling, our willing. But this Lord is transcendental, is above all these modes. He is situated...

#### modes of nature, transcendence, spiritual form
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_ZPaXSqTG4c?t=1537
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Although he descended in the human-like form, but his form is not like us. He has transcendental form."
**Summary:** The speaker continues discussing the modes of nature and how to transcend them, emphasizing that Krishna's form is transcendental, not material.

Traigunya vishaya veda, nisthraigunya bhavarjuna. These Vedas are dealing with three gunas. Arjuna, rise above these three gunas. So from tamo guna, we rise to raja guna. From raja guna, we rise to sattva guna. And sattva guna is a springboard, is a board where we can leap into transcendence. That's why when we practice spirituality, all these rules and regulations are there so that we can elevate to sattva guna. Eat sattvic aahar. Get up early in the morning, which is predominantly sattvic. Sattva guna is predominant so that our lifestyle becomes sattvic. And in sattva guna, when we practice spiritual activities, we can transcend the modes and reach shuddha sattva. In one sense, Krishna is saying, I am everything. Fools deride me when I descend in the human form. They do not know my transcendental nature as the supreme lord of all that be. So when the lord descends in this human form, like form, he is not like us. His form is not material. What is his form made of? It is all spiritual. It is transcendental. It is not affected by the modes. That's why Krishna says, When I come in manusya form, a human-like form, like Krishna appeared 5000 years back, fools deride me that he is also historical personality like you and me, but he was very powerful. Krishna says, They don't know when I descend in a human-like form. Although he descended in the human-like form, but his form is not like us. He has transcendental form.

#### free will, devotion, choice
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_ZPaXSqTG4c?t=1668
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Bhakti comes when it comes voluntary. When you have a choice not to love, and you choose to love a person."
**Summary:** The speaker answers a question about why people do wrong things if God is with them, explaining that God has given us free will and that devotion must be voluntary.

Any questions? We will today catch up with lot of things. Any question on what we discussed so far? Only one question. Only one question. Only one question. Oh, great. Come again. Why do we do wrong things, if God is with us? Another question which we can ask, similar question would be, If God is all-powerful, then why wrong things are happening in this world? Can he not force us to do the right things? So, we will not get into details. We have answered this in the previous session. Maybe we can meet after the session. The answer is, God has given us a free will. Even after speaking the Gita, Krishna says, Yatha Ichhasi Tatha Kuru. I have told you that you should fight. Now you decide whether you have to fight or not fight. There is no question of Bhakti, if there is no free will. If I put a revolver on your head and say, better love me, then that is not real love. I am forcing you to love me. Bhakti comes when it comes voluntary. When you have a choice not to love, and you choose to love a person. And that's why the Lord has given us a free will. As you sow, so shall you reap. You want to remember me? Krishna says, I will give you remembrance. You want to forgive me? Forget me? I will give you forgetfulness. The choice is ours. He will not force. That is the meaning of free will and the Lord has given us.

**Verse References:** —

**Summary:** The speaker clarifies that chanting Om is not wrong, but that chanting Hare Krishna is preferable because it represents the personal form of the Lord.

Are there any other Vedic mantras? No, no. Hare Krishna mantras. So when I am saying, Om, become impersonal, this does not mean that it is something low. It does not mean that something we should not do. For example, sunshine. All I am saying, these are aspects, three aspects of the same truth. Just like you have sunshine, the sun globe, and the sun, God. Exactly like this, Omkara is an impersonal representation of the Lord and we have the Lord Himself. So when we have an opportunity to touch the Lord in the vibration form in which He has personally descended, it is recommended that we prefer that form to Pranava Omkara. Whereas it is a fact that all Vedic mantras start with Om. And also nothing wrong about if somebody chants Om. Nothing wrong about it. So that is the whole thing.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### When does real purification happen? | HG Amitasana Dasa | SB 6.1.18 | 06-12-2019
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### purification, devotion, karma-khanda, jnana-khanda
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RBJEQq12_F8?t=0
**Verse References:** SB 6.1.18
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Such a person cannot be purified without at least a slight touch of devotional service."
**Summary:** This segment introduces Srimad Bhagavatam 6.1.18, emphasizing that atonement processes are ineffective without a touch of devotional service, contrasting karma-khanda and jnana-khanda with devotion.

Reading from Srimad Bhagavatam, Canto No. 6, Chapter No. 1, Text No. 18: PRAYAS CHITTANI CHIRNANI NARAYANA PARAN MUKHAM, NANESHKUNANTI RAJENDRA RAKUMBHAM IVAPGAH. Translation and Purport by His Divine Grace A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Shri Prabhupada. Translation: My dear King, as a pot containing liquor cannot be purified even if washed in the waters of many rivers, non-devotees cannot be purified by processes of atonement even if they perform them very well. Purport: To take advantage of the methods of atonement, one must be at least somewhat devoted, otherwise there is no chance of one's being purified. It is clear from this verse that even those who take advantage of karma-khanda and jnana-khanda, but are not at least slightly devoted, cannot be purified simply by following these other paths. The word PRAYAS CHITTANI is plural in number to indicate that both karma-khanda and jnana-khanda. Narottam Das Thakura therefore says karma-khanda, jnana-khanda, kevala, vishera, bhanda. This Narottam Das Thakura compares the path of karma-khanda and jnana-khanda to pots of poison. Liquor and poison are in the same category. According to this verse from Srimad Bhagavatam, a person who has heard a good deal about the path of devotional service, but who is not attached to it, who is not Krishna conscious, is like a pot of liquor. Such a person cannot be purified without at least a slight touch of devotional service. Thus ends the Bhaktivedanta.

---
#### devotion, materialism, ethics, purification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RBJEQq12_F8?t=111
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "It is very important for us to understand why it is important to be a devotee of the Lord."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the importance of devotion over mere ethical behavior, addressing common arguments that being a good, honest person is sufficient, and questioning the value of devotion when devotees exhibit negative qualities.

So here in this verse it is mentioned that non-devotees cannot be purified by processes of atonement, even if they perform them very well. So Prabhupada mentions about karma-khanda and jnana-khanda. So there are many ritualistic ceremonies, so-called religious performances mentioned in the Vedas. But Prabhupada mentions here that there is no chance of one's getting purified without at least a slight touch of devotional service. So this is a very important point. If one performs these processes diligently, very sincerely, like here it says, processes of atonement, even if they perform them very well, unless there is a tinge of devotion or surrender to the Supreme Lord, unless there is a slight touch of devotional service. We have come across many people, when we preach, more so in present times, there are a lot of people who say that I am spiritual but I am not religious. Or we may come across people who say that for me, work is worship. Or we may come across people who may say that I don't go to temples, I don't worship, but I am honest. I am a good human being. And very sarcastically, basically they are referring to so called devotees, so called religious people, who may be chanting, who may be doing all kind of so called devotional activities, but may be corrupt, may be indulging in bribery, may be speaking lies. So referring to such devotees, they say that better I am, I may not go to temples, I may not worship, I may not chant the names of Lord, but I am honest, I am truthful, I am ethical, I don't hurt anyone, I try within my means to help others. And they actually believe that that position is superior.

#### devotion, materialism, goodness, supremacy of the Lord
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RBJEQq12_F8?t=265
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "But the most important thing is a person should recognize the supremacy of Supreme Lord and recognize one's subordination, that I am subordinate, I am Lord."
**Summary:** This segment explains why being a devotee is essential, emphasizing the recognition of the Lord's supremacy and our subordination to Him, contrasting this with material goodness, which is considered mental speculation from an absolute perspective, using the analogy of dry and wet stool.

So it is very important for us to understand why it is important to be a devotee of the Lord. Why just being a good human being is not enough? Why, although superficially it appears that yes, what is the use of so called devotion, a person is telling lies, he is getting angry. The other person is calm, composed, a good human being. Sometimes we can also get bewildered. So many years he is chanting, but his behavior is like this, but other person, you know, he doesn't chant, he doesn't go to the temple, he doesn't do any so called, but he is very calm, he doesn't get angry, he is very loving, very kind, very gentle. So sometimes we may even doubt, you know, is the process right? Is it really working? Am I following the right process? So let's first understand why it is important, why a devotee, why even a so called, just like here it says, the so called process of attunement, even if they perform them very well, even if a person is assuming not a devotee of the Lord, but he is a very nice human being, even though no matter how good he may be, ornamented with some so called good qualities in the material mundane sense, he is honest, he is loving, he is kind, he is gentle, he is soft spoken, he is well mannered, he doesn't hurt anyone, he is helpful. Although these qualities are considered very highly in this world, which is no doubt has to be there, these qualities are good, we are not, so to say, denying the fact that these qualities are very much wanted. But the most important thing is a person should recognize the supremacy of Supreme Lord and recognize one's subordination, that I am subordinate, I am Lord. We are not talking about being a pure devotee where a person realizes that I am Krishna's. Whatever strength I have, whatever ability I have, whatever talent I have, whatever I have, my possessions, all belong to Krishna. That's a very elevated stage of consciousness. So being devoted means to understand the supremacy of Supreme Lord, in some sense, may not be in a pure form, I belong to the Lord, I have to serve the Lord. So here it says, unless one has a tinge of devotional service, even the so called atonement done very nicely has of no use. Even the same question Parikshit Maharaj asks, what is the use of Kunjara Sochavata, elephant taking bath in the river getting cleansed, and then it comes out and again sprays dust on the body.

#### materialism, goodness, absolute perspective, analogy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RBJEQq12_F8?t=473
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "There is nothing good in that sense, from absolute perspective, in the material world, so called goodness is also, Prabhupada gives example of dry stool and wet stool."
**Summary:** This segment uses the analogy of shopping in a mall to illustrate the importance of recognizing the Lord's ownership, comparing a devotee who acknowledges this to a customer who pays for their items, and a non-devotee to a thief who tries to take without paying.

So Prabhupada explains, when one is on a material platform, ae bhalo, ae manda, this is good, this is bad, is all mental speculation. There is nothing good in that sense, from absolute perspective, in the material world, so called goodness is also, Prabhupada gives example of dry stool and wet stool. It appears that dry stool, one side is dry, but both are stool. Why this element of recognition of the supremacy of the Lord and we subordinate to the Lord is important? We see people go for shopping in malls, go to a shop. In malls, we have unlike traditional shop where you stand across the counter and you tell the shopkeeper he picks the items. In mall, when you enter, you have trolly, basket and then you keep walking in the mall freely and pick up whatever items you want. In mall, you have, key is a full boxed choice of food. You come and go to a shop, we analyze, one common aim is to drink. Inside a shop helping the rich to cook, one should also watch from face to dedication in order to survive the non Conscious and Self-25. You can't touch items. Tell the shopkeeper, he will come and give it to you. But in this case, you freely walk around in a shop, in a mall and pick up items. And in case your trolley gets filled, another sales person will come up with another trolley. You want something more? So in other words, he wants you to pick up items. And after you have picked up all the items, whatever you want, at the end, it is expected that you recognize that these items belong to that mall. And because you want to take it with you, you have to pay the price. So at the billing counter, they quickly bill whatever items you have taken and you pay the price and you walk out. And with that, with great respect and actually these days they are trained to treat customer like a king, customer loyalty. They are very happy that you have picked lot of items. And they will also say thank you sir and they may even, again depends, they may even want you to come back again to give them more business. But imagine a person who walks into that mall. He also picks up items. But not a full trolley or a basket. Just two or three items. And he tries to come out of the mall without paying for it. In every mall there is a terminology they use in accounting. It is called shrinkage.

#### analogy, devotion, materialism, ownership
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RBJEQq12_F8?t=671
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "What differentiates between a good customer and a thief is a person recognizes that it is his property malls property. And since I am taking, I have to pay for it."
**Summary:** Continuing the mall analogy, this segment contrasts a paying customer with a thief, highlighting that the key difference is the recognition of ownership and the obligation to pay, relating this to recognizing the Lord's ownership of our abilities and possessions.

Shrinkage means, you know, just like clothes when you wash it shrinks. So in every mall it is understandable that there will be some shrinkage. Shrinkage is because of people who go and flick some items. When we were in college, so there were some friends from very rich family. Very rich family. And not that they were dearth of scarcity of money. But it was adventure for them to go into a shop and pick up some greeting card. Archie's greeting card. That love becomes even more, you know, you have picked up a greeting card for your lover. And with great adventure, with lot of risk, you know, it really adds more rasa to the whole. Valentine day or whatever. So lot of this to do that, flick the items. Now you see, and if a person is caught, there was one Indigo pilot. It was in the news. It was in a very senior position, a pilot. And he, you know, flights layover happens when they go to a particular, they have flown for 16 hours, 20 hours. So they overnight stay there in the destination. So this person in a duty free shop in the airport while coming, he picked up some item and did not pay for it. Which later on CCTV cameras captured that. And finally, he was removed from job. He was suspended. Probably he went to the court or something like that. That suspension was revoked. And finally, net net was he lost his job. A pilot, very reputed airline, international flights. And he lost his job. So what's the difference between a person who takes items, these two different individuals. Both of them are picking up items from the shop. A person, trolley load or many, many baskets of item picks up and he walks out. Whereas a person just one or two items, one or two items, he's also picked up and is trying to walk out without paying for it. He's called a thief. You see, many things are common. Both are walking in the mall. Both are looking at items. Both are wanting some items. Both are picking some items. Both are coming out of the mall. Many things are common. But what differentiates between a good customer and a thief is a person recognizes that it is his property, mall's property. And since I am taking, I have to pay for it. Whereas a thief wants to enjoy that without recognizing that it is mall's and I have to pay for it. That's the essential difference.

#### devotion, materialism, humility, knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RBJEQq12_F8?t=880
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "That is the essential difference between a materialist and a devotee."
**Summary:** This segment defines devotion as recognizing the Lord and acknowledging that our abilities come from Him, contrasting this with the materialist's belief in their own self-derived abilities, which can lead to pride and atheism, and reiterating that without devotion, atonement and other practices are useless.

So likewise, without a tinge of devotion. Devotion means being a devotee. Devotion means to recognize the Lord. If I have some ability, if I have some talent, if I have this body. If I am able to do something, if I am able to see something, if I am able to lift something. That ability is coming from the Lord. Yes, when a person is a pure devotee, he recognizes that I am not doing anything. The Lord is doing everything. I am just as insignificant Jeeva puppet in the hands of the Lord. Just like Prabhupada when he is going to the West. He is writing, I am an insignificant beggar. I have no power to speak. I don't know how to convince them of your message. That is the consciousness of a pure devotee. But a materialist so-called who is morally, ethically upright. He believes, he believes that his ability is his. If he is very intelligent, he believes that he is intelligent. In fact, if he is too intelligent, he becomes arrogant. He becomes proud. So-called intelligent people at times even become atheists. Because they see that they are able to control things. They are able to comprehend things. They are able to analyze things. They are able to understand things. Without recognizing that this intelligence is coming from the Lord. Some people have lot of abilities, some skills, some talents. And people glorify them for that. And they really believe that yes, they have done it. They have made it. You see any newspaper when some sports people, they won some trophy or some match or something like that. Invariably there is some interview. Man of the match. How did you do that? So they will invariably end up glorifying. I thought I should be very patient and I should stick on to it. And they glorify themselves. I thought like this. I did like this. And that's how it happened. But so many people think. So many people plan. But then things don't unfold necessarily based on your planning, your thinking. So here Prabhupada mentions that unless that tinge of devotion is there, the understanding is there that I am Lord's. Whatever I have. That is the essential difference between a materialist and a devotee. Now devotee, there is a big spectrum. A pure devotee and a mixed devotee and a Kanishadikari and so many other things are there. So here it says, unless one has that tinge of devotional service, recognition of the Lord, all is so-called atonement, so-called karma khanda, so-called jnana khanda. It's all kevala vishara bhanda. It's all pot of poison. E bhalo e mando sakalara. This all is mental. At a mental level. This is good. This is bad. It is from absolute perspective, unless we bring Lord into picture, it is all bad.

#### Krishna consciousness, dormant knowledge, revival, hearing
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RBJEQq12_F8?t=1084
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "This Krishna consciousness, recognition that I am Lords. I belong to the Lord. Everything what I have is coming from the Lord, This recognition, this consciousness is already there. It's dormant in all of us."
**Summary:** This segment explains that our job is to revive the dormant Krishna consciousness within us, which is covered by material consciousness, comparing it to dormant sexual desire in children, and emphasizing the importance of hearing about Krishna from a pure devotee to revive this consciousness.

So our job, if we analyze what we are up to in after having joined Prabhupada's mission, our job is. Prabhupada also mentioned that this consciousness. This recognition of the Lord is not an artificial imposition. This Krishna consciousness, recognition that I am Lords. I belong to the Lord. Everything what I have is coming from the Lord, This recognition, this consciousness is already there. It's dormant in all of us. And the reason why in all of us, you're the Lord, you're the mana, you're the crystal, you're the invite to the Lord. This recognition, this consciousness. This Lord, this Krishna consciousness. And the reason why today we feel, we see a lot of people who are atheists, who don't believe in God, who are very hardcore materialists, because this consciousness is now covered. Prabhupada gives another example that just like sexual desire is dormant in everyone, in every child. You see children, boy, girl, they're playing, they're completely oblivious, they're not very, even so-called gender conscious, because that desires have not got yet fructified. That's why that age is called adolescent age, 12 years, 13 years, 14 years, then it starts dawning on them. Again, some hormonal changes happen in the body. Adolescence. At the biological level. So how that these desires are dormant, and then you don't have to necessarily go to college to learn about how to get attracted. It's dormant. Just like at the right age, in the right circumstances, at the right time, they start manifesting. Likewise, this dependence on the Lord. This recognition of the Lord, this recognition that Krishna is the Supreme Controller, He is the Supreme Lord. This recognition also starts manifesting in the consciousness when the circumstances, the situation is right. And that's why it is said, Shunvata, Sukhatha, Krishna, Punya, Shavana, Krishna, Kirtana, Hrida, Antas, Toya, Bhadras, all that Abhadras, all the dirty things, which is absolutely wrong. Actually covering our real knowledge. If we have to uncover, if we have to revive our pure consciousness, Krishna consciousness, recognition of the Lord, then the process which Bhagavatam is recommending is Shunvata, Sukhatha, Krishna. We have to hear about Krishna from a pure devotee of the Lord.

#### chanting, attentiveness, Prabhupada, example
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RBJEQq12_F8?t=1379
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** This segment recounts an anecdote about a devotee assisting Prabhupada and chanting outside his room, where Prabhupada emphasized the importance of listening while chanting, followed by a story about a devotee who didn't chant the full mantra.

There was one time a devotee who was assisting Prabhupada. Prabhupada used to travel extensively so there were devotees who would take turn to you know there were some secretaries and he also devotees of the temple he would go and Prabhupada at times would be late in the night early mornings would be transcribing correspondence writing letters so one time early morning about 2-2.30 one devotee was waiting for Srila Prabhupada just outside his room Prabhupada was busy writing letters responding to the letters and there was very dimly and there was a light in the room dimly and this devotee was waiting outside 2.30 in the morning and the normal thing is this Prabhupada wants something he would the devotee can assist so for the time being So, while he was waiting and it was early morning, so no noise, no sound, pin drop silence, Prabhupada dictating something on the dictaphone and this devotee was outside the room chanting Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna and it was so silent that Prabhupada could hear what the devotee was chanting. So then at one point Prabhupada called the devotee and said, good that you are chanting, I hope you are also listening. So momentary if somebody tells us, you know, that are you listening, so immediately we get into defensive mode, yes, yes, yes, I listen, I listen, I chant. It happened with one devotee in. I was speaking to him. So another devotee came and told me about him that Prabhu, I have gone with him in the vehicle. I have also heard him that he does not chant full mantra. Now this is very interesting, the conversation we had. So he said, does not chant full mantra. I have heard him many times and he also says that his chant, one round gets over in six and a half minutes. And this is very interesting. So he said, does not chant full mantra. I have heard him many times. And he also says that his chant, one round gets over in six and a half minutes.

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We all are here to purify. If someone points out our improvement area or mistake, it is good for us. Otherwise how will we improve?"
**Summary:** Continuing the story, the speaker recounts how he addressed the devotee about his chanting, emphasizing the importance of being open to feedback and striving for improvement.

And this devotee who told me was relatively a younger devotee, junior devotee. So he did not have the courage to go and tell him, Prabhu, your chanting is not, you are missing some words. So in one conversation, I called this devotee, not specifically for this, I was having a conversation. So I told Prabhujit, little bit be conscious. This is what this devotee has told, please do not feel hurt about this thing. We all are here to purify. If someone points out our improvement area or mistake, it is good for us. Otherwise how will we improve? So this devotee has told me that you do not chant, you are missing some words. So first response was, no Prabhu. In fact I have, I very attentively chant. I am very conscious that I hear each word. So I said, okay. So how much time does it take? He says, I said, he said six and a half minutes. And he says, if I chant slowly, then my mind wanders. Therefore I chant fast and I finish it in six and a half minutes. So then I said, let us do one thing. I am not here to prove that, in fact it is very good if you are chanting very attentively. Let us do one thing, just a small test. So I said, okay. Let us stop watch. Chant nine mantras full at the speed what you are chanting and let us measure. Again my mood was not to prove that he is wrong, just to help him. Sometimes we all have blind spots and because of our ego, we do not want to accept our mistakes or our shortcomings. Sometimes somebody points out, we feel angry, we get upset. Why did he complain? So I said, okay. So we wrote, I said every time you complete one mantra, one, two, three, I will write down and stopwatch is there in milliseconds, we will be able to measure. So I said you chant at the fast pace, whatever you are chanting. So he started chanting one mantra, two mantra. I was noting down at nine mantra, we stopped the stopwatch.

This is the most important thing."

This is the most important thing. #### chanting, purification, Kali Yuga, attentiveness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RBJEQq12_F8?t=1833
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Of all the devotional activities, the most important is chanting the holy names of the Lord."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes chanting as the most important devotional activity in Kali Yuga, highlighting the need for attentiveness and proper execution of the process for effective purification.

The process in this Kali Yuga is to chant. Sarvabhauma Bhattacharya, when he went to Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, after he was touched by, he was transformed in Chaitanya Charita, I had mentioned. So he goes and asks Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, of all the devotional items or activities which is the most important? Chaitanya Mahaprabhu says, of all the devotional activities, the most important is chanting the holy names of the Lord. No doubt, Shravanam, Kirtanam, Vishnu Smaranam, all these are important. Prabhupada emphasizes that anyone for that matter. We have examples of devotees, just by listening, just by chanting. Just by praying, just by offering service, they got purified. But in Kali Yuga, specifically, Kirtanadeva Krishnasya Mukta Sangha Param Rajed. So, very, very important for us, many times, we may be, there are two different things actually. We may be chanting, but may not be listening. We may be chanting, but we may be skipping the beads. We may be chanting, but may not be chanting full mantra. And, many a times, if you are not chanting attentively, if you are not listening, because that is a process, if that process is not properly followed, then the process is to help purify our consciousness. Adipa Kriya by Shri Ramakrishna. That whole process will take a longer time. Nama Bhasa and Shuddha Nama, so we will be in a stage where we are chanting offenselessly and it will take time, purification will take time.

#### chanting, progress, mind, concurrent chanting
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RBJEQq12_F8?t=1962
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "That's why Prabhupada says when you chant, listen very carefully."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the challenges of maintaining attentiveness while chanting, addressing feelings of stagnation and the importance of concurrent chanting and hearing to focus the mind and serve the Lord through the holy name.

And if you are not conscious at times, because the progress is very slow, we may feel that I am not progressing or lusty desires are still haunting me or so many years I have been chanting, still I am like that only, I don't see much difference. All these kind of thoughts can start cropping up in the mind. So in chanting, so MPP has been talking about in his seminars on japa workshop, so many of us have also heard and trying to implement few of his, realization whatever he is sharing with the devotees. So one may be chanting but he is not listening, this is possible. That's why Prabhupada says when you chant, listen very carefully. So in one of the seminar he talks about concurrent chanting and hearing, concurrent. Concurrent means it is not that we are chanting and once in a while we are hearing. Hare Krishna, Hare Krishna and once in a while we are hearing. Hare Krishna, Hare Krishna and once in a while we are hearing. Concurrent means our mind should be, it is meditation. Meditation means you are focusing on something. Just like typical meditation means it is candle light or focusing on some point. It is all about focusing. But in our case chanting is much more than just focusing. It is serving the Supreme Lord who is descended in the form of holy name. How do we receive that Supreme Lord by giving our attention? How do we give attention by hearing distinctly, clearly each and every word? With me it used to happen that sometimes I used to measure. Even now I measure. I chant in groups of four rounds. So sometimes it generally takes 34 minutes, 35 minutes, 36 minutes. I have noticed some days it will finish in 29 minutes. So what has happened? It is not that suddenly on that particular day you chanted fast. Quite possible. Because we are not listening. Suppose mind goes here and there you may skip the beats. Before the mantra is over you have already moved to the next beat. So and it is a very big challenge. More so because mind is lot of thoughts keep coming. So we invariably ask when chanting time we are thinking of our service. We are thinking of our service because we are not listening. Mind cannot have 10 thoughts simultaneously at one time. Just like multiprocessing they say multitasking. Even multiprocessors in the computer parlance. Although they may process millions of instructions per second. But actually at one time one action is happening. So mind also cannot be thinking of something and listening the Hare Krishna mantra. Cannot. That means if we are getting some thoughts and we are thinking about our service. That means at that moment we are not listening. That mantra or that word we are not listening. So chanting is. Is a very very powerful process. It is the recommended process. It is

Weekly once he is allowed to make a phone call to his family members, after five minutes the call cuts. He wants to speak more. He wants to meet them. He wants to go home. He wants to have home food but there are restrictions. Not everything what he wants is fulfilled. So if you analyze from that perspective, even this material world imposes a lot of restrictions on us. We wish that we don't become old. We wish that we don't die. We wish that our near ones remains with us for all time to come. Its not fulfilled. Uncalled from nowhere miseries land up in our lives. In spite of all our efforts to be happy, to live, to live, to live, to live, to live, we do not find any hope. We do not find any hope. We do not find any life. happy, blissful, peaceful, things keep happening. Again, it's not a pessimism. We are just trying

#### prison, Durga Devi, Maya, misery
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Nms25Z1eY5w?t=823
**Verse References:** BG 7.14
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Durga Devi is like the jail superintendent."
**Summary:** The speaker continues the prison analogy, comparing Durga Devi to the jail superintendent who enforces restrictions in the material world, also called Durga Dham or Devi Dhama. He reiterates that Maya imposes misery and that it is very difficult to surmount.

to objectively look at what the world is. Just like prison house, to say that prison house, the prisoner is meant to undergo some kind of punishment is not pessimism. It's objective reality of a prison house. So, just like in a prison house, there is a jail superintendent who is the government servant. The same government which is supposed to serve the citizens, the same government constructs a prison house and appoints a jail supervisor whose duty to ensure that all the prisoners are kept within captivity and they are not allowed to go out. Moment somebody tries to get out of the prison house, in fact, the jail superintendent will become even more cautious and watchful. So, without getting more into further discussion, otherwise the whole session can go on. So, just like you have a prison house, you have a jail superintendent, likewise this material world which is also called a prison house in one sense, this Durga Devi is like the jail superintendent. Durga means what? Fort, Killa. Fort. Just like to get inside the fort or outside, huge, tall walls or thick walls are there so that enemies can't enter unauthorized. So, likewise this world, material world is called Durga Dham, Devi Dhama. And Krishna says mamma maa yaad dhurad deyaay, it imposes lot of misery. Lot of people say no, God does not exist. Yes, because you are not under be Prakriti, be under the influence of Maya. And Maya's duty is to impose all kinds of misery on the living entity. And Krishna says, it is my energy. Mamamaya duratya, it is very difficult to surmount.

#### directions, surrender, reformation, freedom
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Nms25Z1eY5w?t=948
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If you surrender to Me, I will be able to do it only if you surrender to Me."
**Summary:** The speaker describes Durga Devi's ten hands representing the ten directions, symbolizing captivity. He quotes Krishna, stating that surrendering to Him is the only way to be freed from Maya, similar to reforming oneself to be eligible for release from prison.

It is said Durga Devi has ten hands. Each hand represents ten directions. What are the ten directions? Up, down, left, right, four corners and front and back. So, from all sides you are you are held captive, you are being watched. Then Krishna says, mam evaye prapadyante mayam etam tarantite, if you surrender to Me, I will be able to do it only if you surrender to Me. If you become a law-abiding citizen, if you reform yourself, just like we have in a normal prison house, you go into a prison house because you have violated the state laws, you have indulged in some criminal activity. When you get reformed, then you are eligible to come out of the prison house. Therefore Krishna says, maam eva ye prapadyante, if you surrender to me, mayam etam taranti te, I will free you from maya.

#### maya, mahamaya, yogamaya, desire

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker distinguishes between two types of Maya: mahamaya, which we experience in the material world, and yogamaya, or Daiviprakriti, the Lord's internal potency. He states that Mahatmas are under the influence of Daiviprakriti, not mahamaya, and asks why we are entangled in the material world.

Now there are two types of maya. One is called mahamaya. Mahamaya is what we experience in this world and one is yogamaya. Yogamaya is Daiviprakriti, Lord's internal potency. So right now we are under the influence of mahamaya. The Mahatmas, great souls, are under the influence of Daiviprakriti. They are not under the influence of mahamaya. Now one of the requirements, why we are entangled in this material world, can anyone tell me why we are, kyun maya jaal mein phasay hoon hai? **Key Quote:** "Instead of serving the Lord, when we desire that yes I want to be the Lord, then instead of becoming so called Lords, we become servants of our senses."
**Summary:** The speaker identifies the root cause of our entanglement as Ichcha (desire) and Dvesha (envy), specifically the desire to enjoy independently of the Lord and envy of the Lord. This leads to becoming servants of our senses instead of Lords.

Our desires. Our desires. Hum log kyun maya jaal mein phas gaye hain? Why? What was the beginning point? What was the starting point? Because we have been through a lot of experience. So God let us free into a world of creation. The answer is Ichcha. Ichcha means desire and Dvesha. Dvesha means envy. Ichcha, desire to enjoy independent of the Lord and Dvesha means envious of Lord. Ichcha, dvesha, samuthena, dvandamohena bharata. When one develops Ichcha, desire to enjoy independently. How do we desire? Instead of serving the Lord, when we desire that yes I want to be the Lord, then instead of becoming so called Lords, we become servants of our senses. Senses, our mind, our desires.

Then matanga. Matanga means elephant. You know in especially wild forest, how the elephants are caught, wild elephants? They make a pit. But how does the elephant get into that pit? Covered it with, but why will that elephant go there? So there are trained female elephants. And especially behind this head, there is a spot which is a very, very sensitive spot for that elephant. The female elephant is trained to pass by that elephant, a wild elephant, completely untamed and brushes that elephant on that particular spot. So there are trained three elements. And the moment that elephant is touched at that spot, it gets completely untamed. But it gets completely aroused, agitated. And this she-elephant is then further trained to lead him to a particular path. And on that path, they have a pit, a huge pit covered with a green grass. So this elephant who has got aroused, who has got agitated, he just madly follows that she-elephant. And then that she-elephant circumvents that pit. And this elephant passes through that pit which is covered by green grass and falls into it. Then small little mehut, 8 feet, 6 feet, goes and captures him, tames him. So which sense of that elephant led to its downfall? Touch. Skin.

In fact, all the Vedic literatures, who is the compiler? Anyone can help me with an answer? Who is the compiler of all the Vedic literatures? Vedavyas. Vedavyas has compiled many Vedic literatures. And can anyone help me with what is the final, the final work of Vedavyas? After having written different Vedic literatures, the final work, the last book what he wrote, which is more of the essence of all the Vedas, what is that? It is Srimad Bhagavatam. Srimad Bhagavatam. #### karma kanda, material desires, Vedas
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jgSvxU7s64I?t=373
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "God is a means to an end. What is the end? Your desire's fulfillment."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the karma kanda portion of the Vedas, which offers material benefits in exchange for worship, and how Vedavyasa compiled it to elevate materialistic people towards God consciousness.

The other side, we have completely perfect, pristine, pure Paramahamsas, pure devotees. So Vedas have some instructions which are very important, which are meant for these base people to move them to the next level of their consciousness. So that's why there is a karma kanda portion in the Vedas, which says, you do this sacrifice, you do this yagya, you will get this material benefit. You worship Uma, you will get good wife, you worship Saraswati, you will get learning, goddess of learning, you worship Lakshmi, you will get lot of prosperity and wealth. Nothing to do with God. God is a means to where there is an end. What is the end? Your desire's fulfillment. And there is a place for this instruction, because not everyone is ready to accept the highest thing. There are people who are completely materialistic, now to bring them to God consciousness, all allurement is given. You worship, you worship, you will get this. Worship, get it. So that portion of the Vedas is called karma kanda. And Vedavyasa has compiled that, because he wanted to elevate everyone. Vedas means knowledge, meant for everyone. So after having compiled all the Vedic literatures, Vedavyasa was despondent, he was very sad, he was not satisfied, he was not feeling contented, for having done this compilation.
