#### Srimad Bhagavatam, Narada Muni, uncompromising devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jgSvxU7s64I?t=463
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "You have not spoken directly, in an uncompromising way, about the Supreme Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker recounts how Narada Muni, Vedavyasa's spiritual master, recognized his despondency and advised him to compile a literature focusing directly on the Supreme Lord without compromise.

So Narada Muni, who is his spiritual master, when he happened to meet Vedavyasa, he could immediately understand the cause of his despondency. And he said, you have created, you have compiled all these Vedic literatures, you have told so many things, but it is like beating around the bush. You have not spoken directly, in an uncompromising way, about the Supreme Lord. And it is quite possible that people whom you are trying to convince, that people whom you are trying to elevate, by giving them such instructions, karma kanda portion, they can go tangent, they can get misled, they can think that this is the ultimate purpose of the Vedas, to do the sacrifices and have material prosperity and enjoy life comfortably. That is not the purpose. So then Narada Muni said that they can get distracted, they can feel that this is the end. Ultimate objective of religion is to do yagnas, to do the sacrifice for your material prosperity. So then Narada Muni told Vedavyasa, now you compile a literature where you speak about the Supreme Lord in an uncompromising way. Do not beat around the bush. This is not the words of Narada Muni, but the essence was that. Now speak about the Supreme Lord. So Srimad Bhagavatam, which is the final work of Vedavyasa.

---
#### Srimad Bhagavatam, cheating religions, Krishna's pastimes
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jgSvxU7s64I?t=557
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "All the religions in one sense, they call cheating religion, because there is a promise of material prosperity, material well-being, and God is a means to that end."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Srimad Bhagavatam rejects religions that promise material prosperity, emphasizing that loving God should be the ultimate goal, and highlights the Bhagavatam's focus on the pastimes of Krishna.

Starts with the verse that this Srimad Bhagavatam will reject all cheating religions. dharma projita kaitavatra paramo nirmatsranam. Now all the religions in one sense, they call cheating religion, because there is a promise of material prosperity, material well-being, and God is a means to that end. Service to God is not the end. Loving God is not the end. God is a means to an end. What is that? The end is your own fulfillment of desires. That's what generally religiosity is all about. People do, when there is a house warming ceremony, they do some pooja, so that there is peace in the house. When there is some exam is there, people go to the temple and worship. Some elections are there, politicians throng to the temple. It's all about blessings I need of God for my desires to get fulfilled. So in that sense, it is not pure religion. It is dharma projito kaitava. Kaitava means cheating religion. So in the Srimad Bhagavatam, Shukadeva Goswami now starts delineating directly about who the Supreme Lord is and what is the ultimate purpose of religion. And then he talks about different incarnations, incarnations of the Lord, and in the first canto, there are twelve cantos, in the first canto, Vedavyasa says, iti chamsa kalapumsa krsnastu bhagvan svayam, indrari vyakulam lokam hridayanti yuge yuge. Although many many incarnations come from time to time, there are many many incarnations, krsnastu bhagvan svayam, and the entire Srimad Bhagavatam is dedicated to describing the pastimes, the leelas of the Supreme Lord Shri Krishna.

---
#### Krishna, supreme controller, source of Brahma and Shiva
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jgSvxU7s64I?t=682
**Verse References:** BG 10.2, BG 10.8
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna is the source of Brahma, Shiva, and all the demigods."
**Summary:** The speaker addresses potential misunderstandings about Krishna's pastimes, emphasizing that Krishna is the source of Brahma, Shiva, and all demigods, and that He effortlessly manages the material and spiritual worlds through His energies.

The Dasma Skanda, the tenth canto, is about Krishna's esoteric pastimes with the gopis, with Radharani, which unfortunately, if heard without proper context, proper understanding of who the Lord is, we can get mistaken. Sometimes people say, you know, okay, I understand Krishna is there, supreme, why did he dance with the gopis? Why did he indulge in all these so-called frivolous activities? Why did he steal butter? Stealing is not considered something very good. How can such a person be God? So, the Vedic leaders agree that Krishna is the source of Brahma, Shiva, and all the demigods. In fact, in the Gita itself, Krishna says, aham adir hi devanam. Aham means I, adir means the source, the creator, the creator of devanam. In the Moksha Dharma, Krishna also says, prajapati cha rudra capi aham eva srijami. Srijami means I create. Prajapati means, whose prajapati is? Brahma. Rudra, whose rudra? Lord Shiva. Capi aham eva srijami, I am the creator. Aham sarvasya prabhavo matta, matta means from me, sarvam pravartate emanates, comes about. Now, one who understands Krishna in such a way, having understood that he is a samambonam, the source of everything, including me, including my ability, including what I have, is coming from him.

All my energies. When a person gets a paralytic stroke, he has a desire to move his hand, cannot move. We have been given an ability, it's there with us for a certain period of time, and when the time comes, that ability is withdrawn. So one who understands that, that everything what I have is coming from the Lord, he is the ultimate source, he with that understanding, a wise man, engages all what he has in the service of the Lord. And how does he engage? Buddha, bhava samanvitaha, with love, with devotion, with feelings, with the right understanding that he is the one whom I have to serve. Now somebody may say, you have been talking about serving the Lord, all what we have, we have to use in the service of the Lord, why? It is said that we cannot live without serving. If we don't serve the Lord, we serve, we serve maya. So Buddha, a wise man, voluntarily engages in the service of the Lord. Now how he is engaged? With bhava samanvitaha, with feeling, with love, with devotion.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### Why Our Senses Fail to Reveal Truth | Sri Amitasana Dasa | ISKCON Bangalore Sanga
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### limitations of senses, spiritual knowledge, surrender
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Lxx7DF7xBbM?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Our scriptures are saying that we cannot perceive the reality with our senses because our senses, we all have four inherent defects."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the limitations of our senses in perceiving reality, citing scientific theories about extra dimensions and emphasizing the need for spiritual knowledge and surrender to overcome these limitations.

I'll share with you a quote from a very eminent scientist. He says, all the scientific theories do not dictate that there has to be only three dimensions. Although we can't see with our eyes or feel with our fingers, additional dimensions of space are a logical possibility. Our senses register only three dimensions. So, extra dimension might sound incredible. Even the dimension in the world we are living, the earth planet we are living, in Hyderabad city what we are living, what we are seeing is not necessarily complete reality. Forget about where we have not gone. But our scriptures are saying that we cannot perceive the reality with our senses because our senses, we all have four inherent defects. So, with this limitation, inherent defects in our senses will never be able to perceive the reality. So, when we get spiritual knowledge, we understand how small, insignificant we are coupled with humility. I am a small, insignificant Jeeva. I have limited sense perception. Can't even control myself. How can I control things? When that living entity develops that humility, surrenders to the Supreme Lord, surrenders to a bona fide spiritual master, OM AGYANATI MIRAN DASYA GYANANJANA SHALAKE, we open our eyes with torch of knowledge, we are able to sense some bit of the reality.

**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Our desire for that life is getting frustrated at every step."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses how our desire for a life of permanence, bliss, and knowledge is constantly frustrated, leading to suffering, and how a wise person questions the source of this suffering.

Our desire is to live a life of permanence, to live a life which is full of bliss, enjoyment, full of knowledge. And that's why Krishna says, our desire for that life is getting frustrated at every step. And we are getting frustrated after many, many births. A person in knowledge, a person who is little bit wise, he will say, why am I suffering? Why this happiness which I want to achieve is elusive? Why there is so much of struggle in this world? Actually, animals cannot figure out, neither they can ask these questions.

---
#### suffering, knowledge, surrender
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ufB-4WxO0oM?t=93
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Real knowledge means to understand, why am I suffering?"
**Summary:** The speaker explains that questioning suffering is not pessimism but a sign of seeking real knowledge, which leads to understanding one's identity and ultimately surrendering to Krishna.

Somebody, when we ask this question, why am I suffering? He is a pessimist. He is not strong. He is scared of life. He is a pessimist. Actually, it is not. Real knowledge means to understand, why am I suffering? Who am I? When a person starts, Bahu naam, Janma naam ante, Gyan van, a person who is in knowledge, who is sufficiently experienced, Maam prapadyaante. Krishna says, will come to me, will surrender to me. Now, this is not a binary logic. Zero or one. Surrender to means, we suddenly become a pure devotee. No. We pass through a stage where we become little awakened. We realize that yes, the Lord is there. Lord has to be glorified. Lord has to be served. I am not the Lord. The lord, I am the servant of the lord, we realize that but we are filled with material desires.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### Why so many miseries? | BG 8.15- 8.19 | HG Amitasana Das | HKM Mumbai
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### suffering, God, world
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7pmBvTZ8SxE?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "So sometimes you might wonder, where is God?"
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the topic of why there is so much misery in the world, questioning God's presence amidst suffering.

The topic is, why so many miseries in this world? We have discussed this earlier also. On one side we see that God is our loving father. He is very compassionate, he is very kind, he is very magnanimous. On one side we see he is our father, he is loving, he is kind. On the other side we see a lot of misery in this world. Am I right? Yes or no? People dying of starvation and then floods, tsunamis, earthquakes, natural calamities, accidents, innocent children getting crushed in accidents. People not having enough food to eat. Lot of anomalies in this world. So sometimes you might wonder, where is God? It's all chaos. No one is in control.

#### world, perception, positive thinking
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7pmBvTZ8SxE?t=75
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Some of us saying that it's a wonderful place, beautiful place, lovely place, beautiful flowers, wonderful landscape, sun rising in the morning, very scenic, very beautiful."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the teachings of Srila Prabhupada and discusses the first category of people who view the world as a wonderful place, advocating for positive thinking.

So today this discussion will be based on the teachings of His Divine Grace A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Srila Prabhupada. We will start with text 15 of the 8th chapter. Please repeat after me. Dukhalayam asashvatam naapnuvanti mahatmanah samsiddhim paramam gatah. This discussion we have had many many times, but this is the verse today which I was referring to in many many of our previous sessions. If you remember we had discussed, I asked some of you what is the nature of this world and then we had three kinds of answers. Some of us saying that it's a wonderful place, beautiful place, lovely place, beautiful flowers, wonderful landscape, sun rising in the morning, very scenic, very beautiful. Some of us have had very good experiences in life so far, so we might opine that this world is a wonderful place. It's a good place, great place. And some of us also suggested that it's a good place. Subscribe to positive thinking. Focus on the positives. Yes, even though there is misery, but yes, the world is wonderful. Let's make the best use of whatever things, whatever unfolds in our life.

#### suffering, faith, duality
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7pmBvTZ8SxE?t=167
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "There is a lot of misery in this."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the second category of people who view the world as miserable due to tragedies and problems, potentially leading to a loss of faith.

So that's one category of people who says yes, the world is great. There's another category of people who unfortunately have not met with very pleasant experiences in life, a lot of tragedies, may not be having good upbringing, financial problems, health problems, family problems. And they might get frustrated and out of frustration say this world is bad. There is a lot of misery in this. They might even lose faith in the Lord and say there is no God here. A person may be even pious, he may be God fearing, but still he may feel that his life is in shambles. So there is another category who says this world is very miserable. And what is the third category? First category, it's a wonderful place. Second category, it's a miserable place. What is the third category? What is the third category of people? One category says it's a wonderful place. Another category says it's a miserable place. What is the third category? It's a mix of all. It's a mix of both. Kabhi khushi kabhi gam. Happiness and distress are two sides of the same coin.

#### perception, change, creator
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7pmBvTZ8SxE?t=250
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Now what's the opinion of the Lord who is the creator of this world?"
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that opinions change with time and transitions to discussing the Lord's perspective as the creator of the world.

Yes, we'll cover that as we move forward in the session. So now let's understand what's the opinion of the Lord who has created this world, the Creator Himself. Our opinions change with time. You might have an opinion that the world is a wonderful place. And suddenly in an accident, your near and dear one, you lose him or her. Your opinion may change. Or some series of tragedies unfold in your life. Your opinion may change. I know a person, very senior person, government official. He is at the rank of municipal commissioner. Recently met him. He was driving a car that was about ten years back with his wife, with his two children. And he was driving. He was in the driver's seat. Met with an accident. Lost all three of them. And till date he has not been able to come to terms with this incident. So the moment I entered his office, he fell at my feet. And then he said, Swamiji, do you have some time? So I said, yes. So he took me to his inner chambers. So then with tears in his eyes, he says, Why these things are happening? He says, they were very nice. We were very loving family. We all had good understanding. The kids were so cute. Why did it happen to me? So anyway, I explained to him to the best whatever I could answer. But like this, things happen in lives of people. So opinions may change. We talked about three categories. It's not a constant thing. Somebody is in category one will suddenly shift to category two. Depends on the experiences what he goes through. So now all of us have opinions. Now what's the opinion of the Lord who is the creator of this world?

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker quotes the Lord stating that the world is full of miseries (Dukkhalayam) and that great souls who attain Him never return, emphasizing objectivity over personal feelings.

So he is saying, After attaining me, great souls who are yogis in devotion never return to this temporary world. This temporary world which is full of miseries. Lord is saying this world is full of miseries. Dukkhalayam. Just like you have Anathalaya. Dukkhalaya. It's a place of misery. Again, mind you, as I repeated earlier, it's not about being pessimistic or optimistic. It's being trying to understand the world objectively without mixing emotions and feelings. What's the nature of this world? Objectively, intrinsically. Not based on our opinion. I think so in my understanding. Or I feel so. No. How it is constructed. How it is designed. Just like the prison house. It is designed for a certain outcome. It is not designed to be a comfortable place. It's not designed to give a certain outcome. It's not designed to give a comfortable feeling to the inmates in the prison house. It is designed to inflict some kind of restrictions, pain, punishment to the inmates. So Krishna is saying, It's full of miseries. But great souls who attain me, Who are yogis in devotion, Never return to this world. So two points we have. One is, Why this world is a miserable place? How this world is a miserable place? And the third, Another associated point is, Why are we here in this world?

we are in this material world which is dukkhalayam ashashvatam. What are the wrong deeds we have done? We ate masala dosa without asking Krishna. What are the wrong deeds we did? **Summary:** The speaker discusses the vast timescales of Brahma's life, including kalpas and yugas, but emphasizes that even Brahma is not free from birth, death, old age, and disease, highlighting the cyclical nature of the material world.

By human calculation, a thousand ages taken together is the duration of Brahma's one day and such also is the duration of his night. So first created being in this universe, in this material world, in this material universe is Lord Brahma. In one sense he is called creator God, secondary creator. The universe is created by the Lord and in the universe we have Garbhodakshaya Vishnu, from the navel of Garbhodakshaya Vishnu sprouts a lotus stem and in that we have different planetary systems and then on the lotus comes the Brahma. So now a day of Brahma is also called Kalpa. And what is the duration of one Kalpa? Anyone knows what is the duration of one Kalpa? What is the duration? Yes? Four million years? Okay. So one Kalpa is the duration of one Kalpa. One Kalpa is one thousand cycles of four yugas. What are the four yugas? Satya yuga, Treta yuga, Dwapa yuga and Kali yuga. So the four yugas combined together, thousand cycles of these four yugas is one Kalpa and that's one day of Brahma. And the night of Brahma is also thousand cycles of four yugas. Now hold your seats tightly, I am going to tell you something very interesting. Hold your seats tightly, you shouldn't fall off. One Kalpa lasts for 17,28,000 years. Now how long should Treta yuga last? 12,96,000 years. Dwapa yuga lasts for 6,64,000 years and Kali yuga for 4,32,000 years. And in Kali yuga, how many years? 5,000 years. So these many years you add up, this is one cycle. Like that thousand cycle is one day of Brahma. Like that is one night of Brahma. Like that Brahma lives for 100 years. From the viewpoint of eternity, this is very minuscule time. I will explain how. Now, even Brahma, although is having such an extend life span, one kalpa, which is cycled, thousand cycles of four yugas. And that is the night and like that you have a year, like that you have 100 years. Even Brahma is not free from birth, death, old age and disease. Abhrahma bhuvana loke punaravarthino arjuna. When Brahma is not freed from old age, disease and ó One life, past and future life is created. Abhrahma bhuvana loke punaravartino arjuna. When Brahma is free from this condition, then you are free from this problem of Adivajshana

#### Brahma, annihilation, cycle of birth and death
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7pmBvTZ8SxE?t=2416
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "During the day time of Brahma they exhibit their activities and the coming of Brahma's night."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the cycle of creation and annihilation in the material world, explaining how living entities are reactivated during Brahma's day and dissolved during his night, emphasizing that only great souls who are yogis in devotion can escape this cycle.

Yeah, I'll continue. So, this is text 16. Sorry, text 18. When Brahma's day is manifest, this multitude of living entities come into being and at the arrival of Brahma's night they are all annihilated. During the day time of Brahma they exhibit their activities and the coming of Brahma's night. In fact there are three kinds of pralaya. Pralaya you understand? Pralaya means annihilation. What are the three kinds of pralaya? One pralaya happens when at the end of the cycle of four yugas. When the Kali Yuga ends. So that's one kind of pralaya. Another pralaya is at the time of end of the day of Brahma which is thousand cycles of four yugas. And another pralaya happens at the end of life of Brahma. So when the night comes or when the change comes, the living entities they reside in the body of Vishnu and next time when the day comes just like in the night time we sleep, we are not conscious and then when the day comes we again awaken and resume our activities. Likewise Jivas who are in the material world at the time of night of Brahma, they are in the body of Vishnu and when the Brahma day comes, they again get reactivated. Like that day, night, day, night, day, night even at the time of the death of Brahma. So they remain in this material world in the cycle of birth and death and great souls who are yogis in devotion they cross this Dukkhalema Shashvatam and go to the spiritual world. In this way the Jivas are captivated in the material world. Again and again the day comes and this host of being is active and again the night falls. Opartha and they are helplessly dissolved. Dissolved means they lose the material body. The soul does not get killed. The soul is eternal but materially body what we have, we lose that body and we are dormant in the body of Lord.

So today we'll be discussing from the seventh chapter of the Gita, where Krishna talks about the Devatas. And the topic for today's discussion is, why too many gods? Especially in our religion, we call ourselves as Hindus, we have how many gods and goddesses? 33 crore Devi and Devatas. So sometimes we wonder, what is all this mess? Everyone is worshipping different gods, different rituals, different superstitious beliefs, at least when we look at Christianity, we see at least they have some common understanding. Islam, they talk about Prophet Muhammad. We have somebody worshipping Shiva, we have somebody worshipping Hanuman, we have somebody worshipping Durga, somebody worshipping Kali. And then some people say, you have to have something to worship, some form to worship. These are all means. Whatever suits you, whatever is resonating with you, you can take that help. Ultimately, God is formless. It's a means to an end. The ultimate end is impersonal.

So likewise there are several categories. There are some empowered entities, living entities or Jeevas who have been given the position of Deva or Devatas. there is element of selfishness, we do business with God. Whether you embrace me or you make me broken hearted by not being present before me, you are my Lord unconditionally. Pure devotion is unconditional. Now in case of normal scenario, when we are not devotees of the Lord, the Lord is witnesser and sanctioner. Based on our karma we get what is due to us. But when we become devotees of the Lord, when we start surrendering to the Lord, which is not a binary logic, 0 or 1, then the Lord is not just the witnesser and sanctioner. He also becomes a director…. He starts involving in our lives, directing us.

#### Demigods, Limited Fruits, Spiritual World
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oXKNozaR4is?t=1441
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Men of small intelligence worship the demigods and their fruits are limited and temporary"
**Summary:** The speaker explains that worshiping demigods leads to temporary material benefits and heavenly planets within the material world, while worshiping the Lord leads to the spiritual world.

I am there in everyone's heart, from me comes knowledge, remembrance and forgetfulness… So, devotee of the Lord is always thinking, how do I can please the Lord, how I can act in a way, is pleasing to the lord and the the lord in turn is serving his devotee which is exemplified in the case of Arjuna and Krishna the Lord has agreed to become a chariot driver of Arjuna, Partha Sarti. Text 23. Men of small intelligence worship the demigods and their fruits are limited and temporary. Those who worship the demigods go to the planets of demigod just like we have Indraloka, we have Brahmaloka, these are all material planets in the material world just like you have different countries in this planet likewise we have different planets in this universe, Swargalokas we have. So those who worship demigods go to the planets of demigods but who worship my devotees ultimately reach my supreme planet that spiritual world is beyond this material world which is manifest and unmanifest so when we worship devatas we can go to heavenly planets but those heavenly planets also are within within material world just like somebody can go to U.S it's much more prosperous but those heavenly planets are also in the material world the standard of living may be much higher but still it is material world so worship of devatas can elevate us can give us some material benedictions can fulfill our material desires can make us prosperous but it is temporary and it is not eternal whereas worship of the lord ultimately takes us out of this material world and changes our life completely.

It is not the highest thing which we can be asking. Again story, we have shared this earlier. There was a lady working, she was very impoverished, very old and her son had deserted her and she was working as a laborer in a construction company, in the hot sun would be carrying bricks to the construction site and she was quite devoted. And every day, you know, in the hot sun she would be carrying the bricks and at the end of the day she would get some part to some which can manage her living. So one day while she was carrying, somehow she stumbled and all the bricks fell. And you know, because she was devoted, says Govinda, Govinda, please help me. So Govinda actually came seeing that a devoted lady. So she says, what do you want? What do you want? He says, I need your help to carry the bricks so that I can carry it strongly. I don't fumble. I don't stumble. Can you please help me give the strength so that I can carry the bricks nicely? What's wrong in this ask? Can anyone tell me? The Lord himself has come. And the lady is saying, can you please help me with the strength so that I can carry the bricks better? What's.

wrong in this asking? What's wrong in this ask? She didn't have the intelligence. There are much higher things. The Lord can give much more. The Lord can give much more. Fear, I think a lot of thing emerges from ignorance. A lot of superstitious beliefs emerge from ignorance. And because you are ignorant, there is an element of fear. Kya ho jayega? And then there are all kind of superstitions. Yes, Deepakji? What is wrong with the part of the lady who asks only for what occurs to her? If the Lord is so magnanimous, he can also give much more than what is being asked. As we discussed in previous slide, Lord being magnanimous to everyone actually can give us without even we asking it. Just like a father to his son. Not necessarily the son always asks. The father is always ready to do something for the betterment of the child. Much more than what he is asking for. But specifically when it comes to this thing, we have come with the intent that I am asking for. I am asking for this. I am asking for this. I am asking for this. I am asking for this. I am asking for this. I am asking for this. I am asking for this. I am asking for this. I don't want you. Leave me alone. I will make my independent plans for enjoyment. Ichadw esadha samuthena. So, Lord has given us a free will. He does not intervene. You want this material. God has given you this, and he says I want it. So you sit, you are material desires, okay, take it. You don't want me, okay, take it. You want to forgive me, take it. Although that's not the highest thing. The Lord knows that a person will unnecessarily suffer. But that's what we want. That's why Krishna says, bahu naam janma naam ante, after many many lives of trial and error, this way, that way, this way, that way, gyan vaan maam prapadyanti, a person in knowledge will come to me. Vasudevam sarvam iti, that Vasudeva is everything. I have to approach him, I have to surrender to him. Samahatma durlaba, that atma is a real person. We don't want him, that's why, why he should force upon us the highest thing. We still want material desires to be fulfilled. We are not interested in anything spiritual. We are not interested in surrendering to him. We are not interested in obeying his commands. We are not interested in obeying his instructions. We are interested in my desires getting fulfilled by hook or crook. You tell me that way, I'll do it. So, Lord doesn't want to thrust upon anything.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### With Our Polluted Mind How To Bring About Purification Of Consciousness
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### detachment, purification of consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0IK0mZBO3kA?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So with polluted consciousness, how to practice this thing, how to bring this element of detachment?"
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the problem of practicing detachment with a polluted mind and highlights the inbuilt purification system.

Some fruitive, my mind is acting in that direction only, some owner, something I want out of it. So with polluted consciousness, how to practice this thing, how to bring this element of detachment? So this purification of consciousness is inbuilt in the system.

---
#### karma, false ego, enjoyment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0IK0mZBO3kA?t=12
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "After many times getting hit, finally I said, and now I realize I am not the enjoyer."
**Summary:** The speaker explains how the world is designed to backfire when one acts as an enjoyer or proprietor, leading to the realization of not being the enjoyer.

One thing is the world has been designed so beautifully by the Lord, when you act in a wrong way, it will backfire. When you want to act as an enjoyer, act as a proprietor, it will hit you back, even as devotees. That's why Krishna said, after many times getting hit, finally I said, and now I realize I am not the enjoyer.

---
#### devotional service, false ego, purification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0IK0mZBO3kA?t=52
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So we will start doing devotional service with that same consciousness, I am the enjoyer."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses how devotional service often begins with the desire for personal enjoyment and control, which is a natural starting point but not the ultimate goal.

So we will start doing devotional service with that same consciousness, I am the enjoyer. I want to enjoy peace of mind. I want to enjoy lording over bhaktas. We start with that. Cannot help it. Theoretically it will not help. And today onwards I will not enjoy. And today onwards I will not think I am the proprietor. It doesn't happen. It will be at a mental level.

---
#### devotional service, purification, humility
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0IK0mZBO3kA?t=82
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Then you realize, my God, it's a fact, I am not the convincing."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of rubbing a diamond to explain how engaging in devotional service gradually purifies consciousness through repeated experiences of success and failure.

So we engage in devotional service, just like a diamond gets rubbed. So this rubbing will go on here also. So we will collect, we will think that I am a big collector. Finally you will get a hit. No, I am not the collector. You go for book distribution, one day you will distribute a lot of books. You get a feeling, I am good, I am very expert, very nice. See I convinced him. Next day you do all your convincing and nobody will buy a book. Then you realize, my God, it's a fact, I am not the convincing. This happens over a period of time.

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

And while we are engaged in devotional service, slowly, dhire dhire, dhire dhire, that purification happens. One comes to our senses. Unfortunately, to some extent you can fast track the process, otherwise it will take time. That's why it's a dhairiya, patience. You start with potency. You start with polluted consciousness. As we engage, the purification of consciousness is inbuilt in the system.

#### hearing, chanting, purification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0IK0mZBO3kA?t=144
**Verse References:** SB 1.2.6
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When we do that, all this abhadras, dirty things which is clinging to us, clinging to our consciousness, which is overpowered our consciousness, completely gets subsided."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that hearing and chanting (shravanam and kirtanam) are essential processes for removing impurities (abhadras) from the consciousness, using the analogy of polishing a diamond to illustrate the transformative effect.

For that, what we have to do, we have to, the process, shravanam, hearing, shunvata, swakataha, krishna, punya, shravana, kirtana, nashta, praya, shobhadreshu, nityam, bhagavata, sevaya. When we do that, all this abhadras, dirty things which is clinging to us, clinging to our consciousness, which has overpowered our consciousness, completely gets subsided. Just like a diamond, it's actually a very precious stone, but when you see original, this thing, it is unpolished, uncut, it is not shiny, but because of that polish, because of rubbing, because of different treatment it goes through, it starts shining. Same is the case what happens with us.

**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### worship, diversity, understanding

**Summary:** Introduction to the lecture topic: the diversity of worship in India and the need for a fundamental understanding of the reasons behind it.

The topic is three kinds of worship. In India, we specifically notice that there are many types of temples, people worshipping different gods, different deities, different devatas, different festivals being observed. Every state has their own religious festivals. So in today's session, we will be discussing some of the fundamental concepts with respect to why so many gods, why so many religions. Although we have covered it at different times, nevertheless, it's very important for us to have a very good understanding of this subject, dedicated to Srila Prabhupada, our founder and beloved spiritual master.

#### demigods, supreme Lord, empowerment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hrQx-SpeVPU?t=52
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** Explanation of the difference between demigods (Devatas) and the Supreme Personality of Godhead (Bhagwan), emphasizing that demigods are empowered representatives of the Lord.

Text 4th from the 17th chapter. Men in the mode of goodness worship the demigods. Who are the demigods? Devatas. Actually, in India, we have a concept that there are many gods and you can worship any one of them. Basically, you need something to worship. You need something as an object of your faith and whatever resonates with you, whatever you're comfortable with, you can worship and it's all one and the same. So in the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna very clearly demarcates or distinguishes between the Devatas and the supreme personality of Godhead, Bhagwan.

Who are the Devi and Devatas? Devi and Devatas are empowered representatives of the Lord. Just like we all are Indians and some Indians by following a democratic process become cabinet ministers, some become state ministers, some become chief ministers, some become prime ministers. So they are Indians only like us, potentially we all can become prime minister of the country, we can become chief minister of the country, more so in present situation, deadlock. So different Devi and Devatas are devotees of the Lord who have been empowered by the Lord to take care of different administrative affairs in this universe. Like we have Sun God, we have Varuna, we have Chandra, we have all different Devi and Devatas. So they are also living entities.

**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers

They are also Jeevatattva and worship of the Devatas is not equivalent to worship of the Supreme Lord. Devatas cannot give us liberation from this material existence. They can give us material benedictions. They can give us some, we have some desires to be fulfilled by worshipping different Devatas, they can be fulfilled. Material desires can be fulfilled because they are very materially empowered. Just like chief minister, if you are very close to him, if you want to, he can give you lot of blessings, lot of your work can happen because he is in an empowered position.

#### modes of nature, worship, ignorance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hrQx-SpeVPU?t=251
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Those in the mode of passion worship the demons. And those in the mode of ignorance worship ghosts and spirits."
**Summary:** Explanation of how the three modes of nature (goodness, passion, and ignorance) influence the type of worship people engage in, with examples of each.

So men in mode of goodness worship the Devatas. Those in the mode of passion worship the demons. And that's how you see different kinds of worship. Sometimes have you seen people kind of worship which is very, very, people pierce through their tongue or through their face. How many of you have seen that? At least photographs, if not live. And then covered, smeared face with different kind and you know especially in Kumbh Mela, you see all kinds of Babas with all kinds of Vibhuthis, all kinds of ash, matted hairs. And those in the mode of ignorance worship ghosts and spirits. In India, there are places people go to remote villages and there are some trees which are said to be places where the ghosts are living and they worship them. Different kinds of black magic, this tantric kind of worship is practiced. So all kinds of situations are. So what motivates different people, it's the modes influencing them. If a person is predominantly in Satva Guna, his worship will be of one kind. If he is in Raja Guna, his worship will be of one type. In Tama Guna, another type.

#### modes of nature, transcendence, contamination
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hrQx-SpeVPU?t=337
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** Discussion on the limitations of even the mode of goodness (Satva Guna), explaining that transcendence (Shuddha Satva) is the ultimate goal.

Is Satva Guna worship the highest? So Krishna in the Gita talks about three modes: Satva Guna, Raja Guna, and Tama Guna. And over and above, these three modes are the highest. One of the highest modes is Shuddha Satva. That's why he says Traigunya Vishaya Veda, Nishtraigunya Bhavarjuna. You rise above the flowery language of the Vedas. Even mode of goodness is better than passion, better than ignorance, but it's not the highest. It's a springboard by which we can jump to or move to transcendence. Transcendence means you transcend the mode. You cross over.

They'll do their pujas, but materially motivated. Predominant thing which is driving them is Sukh Shanti. Prosperity. And materially well situation, which is not the ultimate goal of religion. What is the ultimate goal of religion? Anyone? Ultimate goal of religion is to come out of this material existence, cycle of birth and death, and go back to our original home, Kingdom of God. All bona fide religions and scriptures, being word of that one God teach the same truth in essence. The ultimate objective of all religions is the same. The essence is the same. The principles are the same. What they are meant for is the same. The objective is the same. The purpose for which it is being propagated is the same. What is that purpose? What is the purpose of all religions? Irrespective of the details, details may vary from one religion to another. What is the purpose of all religions? Bring us closer to God. Connect us with God. Now everyone is away from Lord or separated from Lord or distant from the Lord to different degrees, is forgetful of God to different degree. I am also forgetful of God, you are also forgetful of God, there are many others who are forgetful of God. The degree may vary.

#### degree, essence, laws

Every one of us knows maths, but some people have done PhD in maths, some people have done elementary school, somebody else. Everyone knows 2 plus 2 is 4. You can say confidently, I know maths, but the degree varies. All religions are doing the same. There is the same work which is taking people closer to God, but the fact is all of us are away from God to different degrees and hence the instructions vary. So the essence, what is the underlying essence of all religions? God is one, develop love for God, obey God's laws. Every religion talks about, there are some laws. Sin no more. Everyone talks about. Be serious, be truthful, be honest, do not sin. Glorify the name of Lord. This is also in different religions, in different forms, it's there, Allahu Akbar. Render service to God, seva. Every religion has that. Because all these things, whatever we talked about, sin no more, chant the names of God, render service to God is to bring us closer to God. Return to the kingdom of God. So that ultimately we come out of this cycle of birth and death, this material existence and return back to our original home. That's the objective. Why Gita life? There's an objective, there's a purpose to spread the message of Gita. Likewise religions have an objective which is that to bring people closer to God. However, God gives his message. He gives his message. Directly or through his representatives, according to time, place, circumstances and according to the capacity of individuals to receive it. This brings the difference. God is one, the message is one, essential principles are the same.

#### instruction, understanding, circumstances
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hrQx-SpeVPU?t=1147
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Teacher instructs according to level of understanding of students. Likewise, different religions teach the same principles, but according to time, place and circumstances."
**Summary:** Using the analogy of a math teacher instructing students at different levels to explain how religions adapt their teachings to the understanding and circumstances of their followers.

So, there is some instruction for them. So, Prabhupada gives a very good example. In elementary school, a child is taught very basic addition, subtraction, multiplication. Prabhupada gives a very good example. In elementary school, a child is taught very basic addition, subtraction, multiplication. 2 plus 2 is 4. 2 plus 3 is 5. 3 minus 2 is 1. Now, if the child asks the teacher while being taught some subtraction, 3 minus 2 is 1. 5 minus 2 is 3. What is 2 minus 5? The child asks. What will the teacher say? Teacher will say, it is not possible. In elementary school, teacher who has done B.Ed, who is quite well versed, knowing that the child does not know negative numbers, time, place, circumstances and the level of understanding, teacher says it's not possible. And the teacher will tell also, if you have 5 apples, how can you give 8 apples to someone? So it is not possible. The same teacher in high school will say it is minus 3. And the same teacher when it addresses students in, let's say at graduation level, will talk about even advanced concepts of mathematics. So all levels are being taught Maths by a Maths teacher. But the level at which the Maths is being administered, the lessons are being administered is different. Teacher instructs according to level of understanding of students. Likewise, different religions teach the same principles, but according to time, place and circumstances.

**Summary:** Explaining that while typical worship is for material gain, Kunti Maharani prayed for calamities to remember Krishna, highlighting a higher level of devotion.

Why I am saying this, while I am saying this, I am aware of the fact it may sound fanatical, just like people say Bible is the ultimate thing, Jesus is the only way, it may appear like that. Now to substantiate what I am saying, I'll give you couple of examples. In the Bhagavatam, typically when we go to God, when we pray, when we go to the temples, why do we go to temples generally? When we pray, typically, a typical mentality for praying, going to God, worshipping is what? Why do we do that? What's the objective? For some material gain. For some fulfillment of our desires. For peace. Am I right? Is there any other reason why we worship? Before exams, before elections, some problem has come, your flight is about to, you are stuck in traffic and your flight is about to miss. That time we worship God. So, Kunti Maharani is praying, you see the level of instruction in the Srimad Bhagavatam, that's how I am saying that the level is different. Kunti Maharani is telling Krishna, when Krishna, they got the kingdom back and Krishna is living for Dwaraka, sorry Krishna is leaving, and she says, I wish that those calamities would happen again and again, for we can see you again and again. Who worships God for calamities? Who worships God that yes, I may get problems? Kunti Maharani, knowing that ultimate goal is to get freed from the cycle of birth and death, for which we have to remember the Lord. He says better let the miseries be there because miseries are an impetus for us to remember you. When we have good time, everything is comfortable, we are in the kingdom, now there is no troubles, no demons attacking us, we will forget you. What is the use of that kind of prosperity? So better let there be more and more calamities so that we can remember you. You see typical all so called religious people are worshipping so that our calamities go away. So that we become peaceful and we enjoy life. When there is a problem in our enjoyment, we bring God, worship a little and put him aside. Isn't it? It's a use and throw kind of a thing. Our plans for enjoyment is independent. When some intervention is required, we call him, use him and put him in the side.

Lord Chaitanya, he is saying, O Lord, I have no desire to accumulate wealth, nor to enjoy beautiful women, nor do I want any number of followers. Na dhanam na janam na sundarim kavitam ba jagadishya kaam aye. O Jagadisha, kaam aye means I do not desire, that's not my objective, that's not what I am worshipping you for. And then what I want is your causeless mercy of devotional service in my life, birth after birth. It doesn't matter where I take birth. All I want is opportunity to serve you, opportunity to remember you. See how these instructions are different from typical religious understanding you have in the society. Typical religious this thing is, I want money, I want wealth, I want name, I want fame, I want followers. He is saying, I don't want all this. All I want is your bhakti. Let my Lord trample me or break my heart by not being visible to me. He can do whatever he likes, but still he alone is my worshipable Lord unconditionally.

#### habits, habit cycle, reward
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/B5ZGqlvV19Y?t=1816
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "In this book, he talks about the habit cycle, how the habit gets formed."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the concept of the habit cycle (cue, routine, reward) from Charles Duhigg's 'The Power of Habit,' using brushing teeth as an example.

Facebook is an addiction. Some of us may not be aware of it. There is a book written by Charles Dweck. The name of the book is Power of Habits. In this book, he talks about the habit cycle, how the habit gets formed. So, we have a cue or a trigger that triggers us to take some action. And that action, let's say we call it routine. For example, when we get up in the morning, we go to the bathroom, there is a trigger. We put up a toothbrush, put the paste. We are not conscious of it and we do the routine of brushing our teeth. And in the process, we get a reward. What is the reward we get when we brush our teeth in the morning? Freshness. Gush of freshness. Therefore, it has become a habit. We get up in the morning. We go to the bathroom. It's a cue. It's a trigger. The moment we step into the bathroom, even if we are very sleepy, even if we are very tired, we will pick up a toothbrush. We will pick up a toothpaste. Even if we are sleepy, we will put it there and start brushing our teeth. That's a routine. And the reward what we get is the freshness. And because we get this reward, we look for this trigger that is next day in the morning. And because we do this repeatedly, it becomes a habit.

**Summary:** The speaker introduces the concept of dopamine as a pleasure neurotransmitter and explains how drugs stimulate dopamine release.

Now, why we are doing it? We have an answer. Hi guys, Dr. Berg here. In this video, I want to talk about this very interesting thing called dopamine. Dopamine is a neurotransmitter which is a hormone-like communication or message that travels through the nervous system versus hormones are communications that travel through the blood. And this neurotransmitter has the effect of giving you pleasure. So it's a pleasure neurotransmitter. It does other things that are involved with reward and even motion. So if you have a problem with dopamine deficiencies, you might have resting tremors and Parkinson's, which is a different thing. But it's mainly pleasure. It's a feel-good hormone. It also gives you that vitality. So if you could imagine, not that you ever took cocaine, but the sensation of taking cocaine, what you would feel like, this heightened pleasure, you know, ability to conquer everything. It's only because cocaine and other drugs stimulate dopamine. So the actual physical feeling that you have is coming from dopamine, not really the drug. The drug is only the trigger that creates more dopamine in the body. So my thought is, why can't we just increase, make sure this is high naturally, right?

---

So why a person is checking 2,617 times in a day? Why? Of a pleasure. Suppose somebody has sent a message. What has he sent? Something I have posted. How many people have viewed it? How many likes I have got? When I see that, I get a pleasure. Actually, it's not the social media. It's the dopamine which gets released. And I get habituated to that release of dopamine. I crave for that happiness chemical. It becomes a craving. Digital distraction works in a similar way. That's why I played the video of trapped. Are we in the process of getting happiness somehow unknowingly, unintentionally getting trapped? It's very scary. So we have a cue. We have a routine. A mail comes in a mailbox or a message comes. We want to check it. There's a reward. There's a feel-good factor. Somebody has liked my post. And there's a craving for distraction

Now there's a craving for distraction. If I don't check my phone, I feel something is missing. It's a trigger. And because we have done it many, many times, what has happened? It has become a habit. A notification came. We know that we should not be checking it. We know that I have scheduled time for it. But it has become a habit. Habitual response. Habitual response is I pick up my phone, swipe it and check what it is. We didn't take a broad enough view of our responsibility, and that was a big mistake. And it was my mistake. And I'm sorry. I started Facebook. I run it. And I'm responsible for what happens here. If the thought process that went into building these applications, Facebook being the first of them, to really understand it, that thought process was all about how do we consume as much of your time and conscious attention as possible? And that means that we need to sort of give you a little dopamine hit every once in a while because someone liked or commented on a photo or a post or whatever. And that's going to get you to contribute more content, and that's going to get you more likes and comments. It's a social validation feedback loop. Turn it off. That's what I would say. It's hard for young people now because they're hooked. They're addicted. If you don't think you're addicted, and I'm talking about anyone from the highest to the lowest, if you don't think you're addicted, then see if you can turn it off for a week. It got quiet in here, didn't it? Didn't it get real quiet? It's a tool, so we should use it. God has blessed us with free will. Now it's free will magnified, free will on steroids. You're free to go in any direction you want. It will allow you, and it's not the enemy. It's just a reflection of our own free will. And we all want to be liked,

If we are too involved in the digital world, seeking that pleasure, which we know is a chemical called dopamine, every time we have a validation feedback, somebody liked our post, somebody commented on what we posted, or liked our picture, it's a validation, and it gives us pleasure. #### brain function, neuroplasticity, habits
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/B5ZGqlvV19Y?t=2574
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** scientific
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "The brain can and does change throughout our lives. It is adaptable, like plastic."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the concept of neuroplasticity, explaining how the brain can change throughout life and how habits are formed through well-traveled neural pathways.

Now this is how the brain works before we understand how to go about handling this challenge. Not so long ago, many scientists believed that the brain did not change after childhood. That it was hardwired and fixed by the time we became adults. But recent advances in only the last decade, now tell us that this is simply not true. The brain can and does change throughout our lives. It is adaptable, like plastic. Hence neuroscientists call this neuroplasticity.

---
#### neuroplasticity, rewiring, change
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/B5ZGqlvV19Y?t=2616
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** scientific
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "This process of rewiring your brain by forming new connections and weakening old ones, is neuroplasticity in action."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on neuroplasticity, explaining how new pathways are formed and old ones weakened through repeated actions and thoughts.

How does neuroplasticity work? If you think of your brain as a dynamic, connected power grid, there are billions of pathways or roads lighting up every time you think, feel or do something. Some of these roads are well travelled. These are our habits. Our established ways of thinking, feeling and doing. Every time we think in a certain way, practice a particular task, or feel a specific emotion, we strengthen this road. It becomes easier for our brains to travel this pathway. Say we think about something differently. Learn a new task or choose a different emotion. We start carving out a new road. If we keep travelling that road, our brains begin to use that road, and use this pathway more, and this new way of thinking, feeling or doing becomes second nature. The old pathway gets used less and less and weakens. This process of rewiring your brain by forming new connections and weakening old ones, is neuroplasticity in action.

---
#### neuroplasticity, habits, dopamine
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/B5ZGqlvV19Y?t=2687
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "With repeated and directed attention towards your desired change, you can rewire your brain."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the ability to change habits through neuroplasticity, highlighting the importance of persistence and replacing old habits with new ones that produce dopamine naturally.

The good news is that we all have the ability to learn and change by rewiring our brains. If you have ever changed a bad habit, or thought about something differently, you have carved a new pathway in your brain, and experienced neuroplasticity first hand. With repeated and directed attention towards your desired change, you can rewire your brain. When we do something repeatedly, it creates neural pathways in our brain. The same action which was very difficult earlier, becomes easy to do. It's because of the pathways. That's why they say practice makes a man perfect. We have examples of eminent personalities who play well, who sing well. It's just that they have, if not in this life, previous life, as a result those pathways are there. So, when we check our phones quite often, it can be an addiction. We can have the neural pathway formed in our brains, and it can become difficult to change. But the good news is, with persistence, with conscious effort, we can form new pathways, and change our habits. By constant practice of replacing the current habits with new ones that produces dopamine naturally.

Meditation for de-addiction and natural happiness. We all are pleasure seeking being, nothing wrong about it. We all are seeking pleasure. Anandamayo Bhyasat. Basically the mind which is chanchala, which is completely out of our control, which is just jumping like a monkey from one thought to another. We have to focus that mind, on a particular thought, on a particular object, on a particular thing, so that it starts getting tamed. That's the basic generic understanding of meditation. ## HG Atma Tattva Dasa

### Sri Tattva Darshan Dasa: A Disciple’s Journey from age 12 to 70 | Sri Gaura Mandala Parikrama
**Speaker:** HG Atma Tattva Dasa

#### renunciation, absolute truth, spiritual seeking
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZW4CAbe8Xuw?t=0
**Verse References:** BG 7.16
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "At the age of 12, somehow or another, I rebelled against the system which I was brought up into."
**Summary:** The speaker describes his early life, starting at age 12, his rebellion against the material world, his search for the absolute truth, and his renunciation of wealth.

I guess it began when I was 12 years old. Now I'm 70, and without knowing in this life, without the knowledge of what is Krishna consciousness, what was Srila Prabhupada, at the age of 12, somehow or another, I rebelled against the system which I was brought up into. I became a vegetarian. I started searching for the absolute truth. The Bhagavad Gita tells us that three types of persons come to Krishna: those who are seeking money, those who are in distress, and those who are seeking the absolute truth. So I don't think I fell into the category of those who were seeking money, because I had a lot of that, and I gave it all up. It was self-made, but some help from the parents and the family. And distress, yes, we were all in distress in this material world. We didn't know which way to go, what to do, our seemingly happiness with our material life, but actually seeking the absolute truth. I was looking in the mountains, I was looking in the forests, I was looking in the swamps, I was living in the ghettos with the black people, I was prompting with kings, I was living rich, I was living poor, I was traveling from place to place, with vows of poverty, trying to understand. And I met what I could call my Vartma Pradaksha guru.

---
#### initiation, devotee association, Krishna's arrangement
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZW4CAbe8Xuw?t=124
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So they said, when you come to Krishna, you should bring an offering."
**Summary:** The speaker recounts meeting Baba Ramdas, being introduced to the Hare Krishna mantra, meeting Vishnu John Swami, reading Back to Godhead magazine, and eventually meeting devotees in Phoenix and offering fruits and flowers to Radha Damodar.

He was the one, Baba Ramdas. He wrote a famous book called, Be Here Now. He had journeyed to India. Anyways, he introduced me to the Hare Krishna Maha Mantra indirectly. And I did that, and we traveled as we did, many people did in that time, among the hippie communes and things. And probably first heard of Prabhupada way back in 1968, when he was in San Francisco. And like this, life went on and on. And the travels led me to a bus that was in a house that I was staying with. We were all, a lot of people staying in a house in the house. The bus had Vishnu John Swami in it. And he's camped out in our house in Fort Lauderdale. And we did some park, we did some kirtan with him. And he was only there a night, so there wasn't much, but what is this, right? And they gave me a book, a Back to Godhead magazine, which I threw on the bed and it went underneath the water bed, later it came out, and I dried it and I read it. And it was a great book. And, of course, chanting Hare Krishna. Then again I met him by the will of providence, meeting devotees in different places, not really understanding, in rock festivals and in downtowns and book distribution, but already chanting Hare Krishna, but not really knowing anything about the Krishna consciousness movement. Then again it really began. I met Vishnu John Swami in 2012. I met him on Krishna Goswami in a bus, in a park in Phoenix, Arizona. So they said, when you come to Krishna, you should bring an offering. So that day we went out with a station wagon, big cars with the back of it. We went all over the fruit orchards and the flowers and the dates, and we picked them from everywhere around Phoenix, which is a famous growing area, and we filled up the whole car. And we brought it to the park. That's it. The park where Vishnu John Swami was. And we offered it to the deities of Radha Damodar there. So then Mr. John said, you've made this offering, you have to come now, back to the temple with us. So we went back to the temple and I stayed a couple of days with them. They were leaving for India, early days. This was probably the beginning of 73 by that time.

---
#### surrender, transformation, early devotee life
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZW4CAbe8Xuw?t=286
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "I'm from the temple of the world."
**Summary:** The speaker describes how Krishna took him from New Orleans to Miami, his transformation into a devotee, his initial struggles with chanting, and his commitment to stay in the temple.

Yeah. And, uh, so that began the journey very shortly, Krishna took me, he took me out of the house of the rising sun, which is a famous place in New Orleans, we won't get in any details. And he transferred me to Miami by airplane with a few bodyguards. And I got to Miami and I had an intimate relationship with it. My first devotee there, his name was Maha Sringa, he was Bhakta Mike then. He looked at me. By this time I had transformed from a long haired hippie with hair down to here. And I had on a dhoti, which was made out of a sheet. I had some beads which were tied up into knots by a rope. I had a sika that was funny shaped, it came down to my back, later became matted. Yeah. And, uh, I had some toothpaste which I had used to make tea lock. And I came from a jailhouse where I was only for 24 hours and the devotees were coming in, they had been picked up for distributing books. And they were coming out, they were getting immediately bonded. And they looked at me, I looked kind of funny, you know, I didn't really look like your regular Hare Krishna. And they said, what temple are you from? I said, I'm from the temple of the world. And they said, where are you going? And my brother was there with some of my friends and they said, he's coming with us. We have a party for him, he's been gone for some time. And I said, no, I'm going with them. And they said, yes, we'll be out in 10 minutes, you come with us. So then Maha Sringa's Bhakta Mike is there. He looks me over. Hmm. What kind of animal is this, you know? And he gives me a dhoti, he gives me a kurta, he gives me some toothbrush, toothpaste. That's all I was. That was it. There was nothing else. And he gave me a mat, it was in the Brahmachari ashram. And he goes, that way's east. It was 10 o'clock at night. Everybody was sleeping. I had no idea which way was east. That's what it meant. That's where you put your head when you sleep. So that began. And then I said, okay, I'll stay here 30 days and give it a try. Never left. That was in 73. So still to this day, trying to be a devotee, I haven't yet become a devotee, but I'm trying. Right? So they asked, when did you become a devotee? I haven't become one yet.

---
#### chanting, book distribution, service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZW4CAbe8Xuw?t=471
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Prabhu, you're cheating."
**Summary:** The speaker shares his initial misunderstanding of chanting rounds, his experience with book distribution, and his natural inclination towards farming and gardening.

So during the years, many things, like was funny thing in the very beginning, I was, I thought that one round was once and back again. I thought that was one round. So for the first month I was chanting 32 rounds, right? And I thought this is hard and everybody was finishing before me. Then one day I'm watching this Brahman, he's chanting and he gets to one round and he pulls down the bead. I watched him again. He did it again. He had his beads out too. I said to him, Prabhu, you're cheating. He said, what do you mean? I says, you're counting half a round as one round. So then he explained to me and then chanting 16 rounds became a little easier. And then after a couple of weeks they said, you should go out and try book distribution. And, uh, I said, okay. Okay. And, uh, some of our, some of the first devotees that are still with me in Krishna consciousness to this day, like Abhirama, Adridharana, Mahashringa, they were all there. We were a big band in Miami. So they said, you got to go out and try book distribution and you got to put on this wig, put on a hat, a wig, put on these Garmi clothes, this thing, and you got to go out and distribute books. I said, I'll go out, but I'm not going to do all this stuff. I said, I've changed my life now. So I went out with my Sankirtan leader and four or five other devotees and they were all out there and our friend Adridharana was there, but he was dressed up a little bit and he would point to people and he would say, we're distributing for India and collecting money for people in India. And here's one of the people from there that was so grateful to us. He came back to help us out because I was wearing a dhoti. So the very first day I went out, I collected more than my Sankirtan leader. I distributed more books and collected more money. So they told me, Prabhu, this is your propensity. This is what you have to do. But I also had an inclination toward farms and gardens and cows.

---
#### guru, initiation, obedience
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZW4CAbe8Xuw?t=603
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "You must protect the cows."
**Summary:** The speaker recounts his desire for a personal initiation from Srila Prabhupada, his efforts to please Prabhupada, and his first direct instruction from Prabhupada to offer obeisances.

So I said, this isn't all I can do, right? And Srila Prabhupada had given me three personal instructions that I consider personal instructions. One was to offer obeisances. That was the first one. That happened in Philadelphia in the airport in 1976. I went there to New Vrindavan for initiation three years after I joined, two and a half years after I joined, because I didn't want a rittvik initiation. I didn't want it. I wanted Prabhupada to hand me my beads. Prabhupada was doing rittvik from the early 70s, right? And I kept asking my temple. The temple president thinks I want to go where Prabhupada is and I want to take initiation. We'll do by letter. We'll do this, that. You have to stay here and distribute books. That's what pleases Prabhupada. And we were developing gardens for some of the temples and I was sending Prabhupada coconuts and nim twigs and everything every day when he was touring around America. We'd climb up the trees and get them in Miami. So Prabhupada was coming. I went to New Vrindavan. I had a Sankirtan party. We had our own party. I was the leader. And I went to New Vrindavan and I told the Sankirtan party, you know, you guys go back where you came from. I'm staying here until Prabhupada comes. You can't. You're leaving the temple. I said, I'm leaving. I'm waiting here for Prabhupada. I'm not going to go for this anymore, you know? So they went back and I waited and Prabhupada came and he gave me initiation personally. That went really well. So when I first saw Srila Prabhupada, the very first time was when he came into the airport in Pittsburgh. You got to imagine the airport's a big circle like this. And on the top, the stairs come out and people come out from the different planes from the different side, right? So for months before Prabhupada's coming, Prabhupada's coming, Prabhupada's coming, he even asked, why is it every new temple, every temple I go to smells like fresh paint? Everything would always be ready. And that was the mantra, Prabhupada's coming, Prabhupada's coming. And we were just totally absorbed in it. Everything we did was. We're going to meet Prabhupada, Prabhupada's coming here. So there was a tumultuous kirtan in the airport. We could do kirtans at that time in the airport. And the Radha Damodar parties were there, the traveling parties, a lot of people because it was new Vrindavan. It was a big gathering. And of course there were some other passengers and airport officials around. So finally Prabhupada walks out. We're right down there in the middle of the kirtan, right in the center. Prabhupada walks out. And. Yeah. When he walked out, the whole kirtan stopped. Everybody hit the ground and did obeisances. But I didn't. I'm looking, Prabhupada, oh my God, he's come. And he goes like this. Looked straight at me and I was the only one, like this. So that was his first instruction was to offer obeisances.

---
#### cow protection, guru's instruction, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZW4CAbe8Xuw?t=790
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "You must protect the cows."
**Summary:** The speaker describes his second personal instruction from Srila Prabhupada during a morning walk in New Vrindavan, emphasizing the importance of cow protection.

And the second instruction I got with him was on a morning walk. I was tending the cows. I was tending the cows in New Vrindavan and helping to build A-frames and do different things. And we were living up in the old Vrindavan farm. And Prabhupada came up. I went on many morning walks in Darshan. But in this morning walk, I'm standing, there's a lot of pictures, I'm right next to Prabhupada. And I'm herding the cows, so I got some boots on and got a t-shirt on instead of a dhoti and everything. I got a dhoti, but that, so I'm herding the cows. And this one cow kept hitting Prabhupada like this. She was a Friesian, she was a Friesian cow, black and white. She kept nudging Prabhupada. And Prabhupada was affectionate, pat her on the head and he would smile. And then Prabhupada turns to, and he says, how old is this cow? Hari Sori says, she's 10 or 12 years old, Prabhupada. I said no. I said, Prabhupada, I'm her cow herd. She's only four years old and her name is Kalindi. And she's just started giving milk. And Prabhupada looked at me directly in the eyes and he says, you must protect, he didn't say we should protect, he said, you must protect the cows.

#### dancing, devotional service, guru's pleasure
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZW4CAbe8Xuw?t=875
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Dance."
**Summary:** The speaker recounts his third personal instruction from Srila Prabhupada, where Prabhupada encouraged him to dance despite another devotee's attempt to stop him.

Second instruction. Third instruction, I like to dance. And I thought maybe Prabhupada would give me a name, something to do with dancing. And the day before initiation, we were in a Ratha Yatra in Cleveland, but Prabhupada couldn't go there. So it came in the front page of the paper, the Rathkart with Lord Jagannath there and everything. And somehow in the paper, in the front of the paper, I was dancing in front of the Rathkart, both feet off the ground, my hands up in the air and my Chatur flying. That came on the front of the Cleveland Press. And I was told later by Kulik Shekhar, Prabhupada's disciple, he said, oh, that devotee dances like Lord Chaitanya. Right? So I thought that Prabhupada maybe would give me some Nataraja, I didn't know, right? So we were outside the barn going into Radha Vrindavan Chandra's temple and we were dancing like everything. And a couple of times, Prabhupada looked at me and smiled, right? It was in Brahmachari cloth, there's pictures of that too. And you come into Radha Vrindavan Chandra's temple and there's a hallway and two devotees can be on each side. And then in the middle, Prabhupada would walk. So I was jumping up and down, I was right there as Prabhupada would come in just a few feet and I was dancing like anything and Prabhupada was ready to come in. And this devotee Charu, he pushes me down, he says, no, you may hit Prabhupada, you can't dance here. I said, I have to dance. And he said, no, you can't dance. And he pushed me the second row behind. And he said, no, you can't dance. And he pushed me the second row behind. And I said, Charu was his name. He later had some different qualities. So Prabhupada walked in and all the devotees were like this. They were a small kirtan. Nobody was really dancing when he walked in the hallway, right? Prabhupada walked over, right next to me, he stopped, he turned, he looked me in the eyes and he raised both hands, one had a bead bag and one had a cane. He said, dance. And he wasn't there when the devotee pushed me behind, but he was there outside. And he had seen that picture of Cleveland Ratha Yatra. So that was the third instruction I got from Srila Prabhupada.

Those were direct instructions. And then one instruction I thought I got from Prabhupada was we were in a garden with Prabhupada. He was giving a darshan, and he had a rose in his hand. He had just finished telling us that sense gratification is like salt. You have to have a little bit or you'll die. And he described the six Goswamis, how they had to take a pat of butter, a little bit of salt, or otherwise they would die too. And how they had to sleep one hour or two hours. They had to eat with a pat of butter. And he said, and for defense, they were always dependent on Krishna. And for mating, he said, there was no question. Then he had this rose. And there was one rose with smell, too. So Prabhupada had this rose. And he said, Krishna consciousness is like this rose. He says, if I have this beautiful rose, and I think, let me give it to Radha Vrindavan Chandra, Prabhupada smiled. He says, I'm going to Vaikuntha. Then he put down again. He says, but I have the same rose. And I think, let me give it to my girlfriend he goes i'm going to hell and he throws the rose and i was right there and the rose came right in front of me but this other devotee grabbed it right i didn't get the rose he took it and i didn't know whether papa was telling me that if i offer roses to robert johnson i'm going to vaikunth or i'm going to hell i didn't know which one and i'm still wondering so that was another indirect instruction.
