#### spirit soul, matter, energy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9k4VXR5266c?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "And what is it? It is the spirit soul."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the concept of the spirit soul as a superior energy exploiting matter, referencing the law of conservation of energy to support the idea of existence after life.

And Krishna says in the next sloka that there is a superior substance and matter, a superior entity. And what is it? It is the spirit soul. So the superior energy is one which is exploiting this matter. And it is, the name given by the Lord is Jitabhutam. And if the soul is energy, then the law of conservation of energy should hold good for the soul too. So that explains that yes, we do exist after life.

---
#### reincarnation, body, soul
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9k4VXR5266c?t=66
**Verse References:** BG 2.13
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna is saying yes, you exist after life, after old age, after death. You exist. And you go, you pass into another body."
**Summary:** This segment explains the concept of reincarnation based on Bhagavad Gita, highlighting the transformation of the soul into different bodies and contrasting it with the limitations of scientific understanding.

And Krishna makes it more clear in the second chapter. In fact, the Bhagavad Gita starts from here. Second chapter, thirteenth sloka. Dehino asliyatha dehe ghaumaryam yoganam jara. Krishna is saying yes, there is a matter. Deha is the matter. And there is a Dehi, which is energy, superior energy. And this energy goes under transformations in different phases. Ghaumaryam, yoganam, jara. What happens next? What happens after jara, after old age? Ghaumaryam said the end. He didn't say the end. He says Dehantara. There is a transformation. Next transformation is a new body. Tatha Dehantara praptir. Krishna is saying yes, you exist after life, after old age, after death. You exist. And you go, you pass into another body. Scientists got stuck here. They understood till here whatever Krishna was saying. Yeah, there is a matter, there is energy. This whole world is a combination of matter and energy. This part they cannot see. Tatha Dehantara praptir. They have no idea how it happens. Only Dheeras they understand and they don't get stuck. Dheeras tattva namuhyati. Not everybody will understand or everybody will appreciate.

#### ignorance, soul, perception
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9k4VXR5266c?t=184
**Verse References:** BG 15.10
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Vimudanas cannot see this. Foolish people cannot see this."
**Summary:** This segment discusses why scientists and learned individuals are unable to perceive the soul's presence in the body and its departure at death, attributing it to their foolishness (Vimudana) as described by Krishna.

So why scientists got stuck here? Why they are not able to understand this also? There are so many learned people in the world. So many engineers. So many doctors. So many PhDs. They don't understand. And that also Krishna explains. Why they don't understand. Krishna says in the 15th chapter. Very clearly he says why they cannot see. budhkramantam sthambapi bhunjanam vagunanam guranvitam vimudana anupasyanti pasyanti jnani chakshusah. That's why Prabhupada says raska. Krishna used the word. What to do? Prabhupada speaks as it is. What other Krishna says, as it is. That's why Prabhupada says raska. Krishna is using the word. Vimudana. Muda means foolish people. Vimudana means too much. That's why they cannot understand. Utkramantam means how the soul leaves the body. Utkramantam means coming out. Emanating out. They cannot see. Sthitam. How the soul is currently staying in the body. That also they cannot see. There are doctors called cardiologists. Heart surgeons. They open the heart. They see everything. They can't see that the soul is sitting there. Sthitam. They cannot understand. Similarly when they are doing the surgery and suppose something goes wrong and the patient dies. They don't understand what goes wrong. Utkramantam. They can't see the soul leaving the body. The heart or body, whatever. They can't understand. Why? Prabhupada says raska. Because Krishna says. Vimudana anupasyanti. Vimudanas cannot see this. Foolish people cannot see this.

How the soul is staying in the heart in this matter. But how it is experiencing the interactions of three modes. They cannot see. Finally when it leaves the body that also they cannot see. There is a book called Coming Back. In that book there is a letter by a heart surgeon. He wrote an open letter in the newspaper that I want to understand what is going on. And somehow one of the disciples of Prabhupada brought that newspaper to Prabhupada. That there is a heart surgeon and he wants to know. Because he had done so many heart surgeries. He imagined in that letter that I have experienced the life passing between my fingers. But he has no idea. Then Prabhupada wrote a letter back to him explaining what soul is, what are the attributes and how to perceive it. How to perceive it? Pasyanti Gyan Chakshasa. Pasyanti Gyan Chakshasa. By the eyes of Mahesh. Time of the word is Shastra Chakshasa. By the help of scriptures. That's where everything gets stuck.

#### senses, observation, limitations
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9k4VXR5266c?t=420
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So how the product of this eye which is faulty and which is limited can be perfect and without limit."
**Summary:** This segment explains the limitations of scientific understanding due to its reliance on observation through faulty and limited senses, contrasting it with the potential of scriptures.

Because the whole process of scientific discoveries or understanding is based on observation. When I was in school, I remember in my science class, science when we go to the lab, we have a lab manual we used to write there. The first thing we write is observations. It starts with observation. Then comes the theory, then comes the experiment, then conclusion. And all our observations are limited by our senses. They are called Gyanendriyas. Knowledge acquiring senses. So whatever we know about our surroundings is because of these Gyanendriyas. They capture the information around the surrounding and that's how we get them out. And they are limited, so our observations are limited. They are faulty, so our observations are faulty. We cannot see everything with the eyes. And with the same eyes, we design telescopes. With the same eyes, we design microscopes. With the help of this eye which has so many limitations and so many faults. So how the product of this eye which is faulty and which is limited can be perfect and without limit. That's where scientists get stuck.

#### scriptures, absolute knowledge, limitations of science
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9k4VXR5266c?t=532
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "But whatever the fortunate souls have learnt from the scriptures billions of years back is absolute. Today and in the past and even in the future. It's not changing."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the significance of scriptures as a source of absolute and perfect knowledge, contrasting it with the limited and ever-changing nature of scientific knowledge.

And that's where the significance of the scriptures come into place. Something which you cannot perceive by your senses can be perceived by the scriptures. By this transcendental knowledge. By this transcendental knowledge. Because this knowledge is absolute. This knowledge is perfect. This knowledge is beyond limits. Whereas what scientists can tell us is limited because the senses are limited. Their observations are limited. And imperfect. That's why the new scientist comes and he says, no, whatever the previous scientist said is wrong. And this keeps happening after every 50-100 years. My grandfather, if he could have gone to university, and may he never bend. If he could have gone to university, he might be learning, my great grandfather might be learning whatever Dalton told. But the atom is indivisible. The most fundamental particle. But I didn't learn the same thing. I learned something else. No, there are subatomic particles. Electron, proton, neutron. Now next generation are learning something else. They are learning, there are sub-subatomic particles. They are learning about quarks. Future generations will be learning about interesting theories. And I don't know what else theory will come. What else they will be learning. And they will be telling me, after some 3rd generation, if they happen to meet us, they will tell, whatever you learnt is wrong. Pushpa will be just, they will say like this. If I happen to meet my great grandfather, I will say, whatever you learnt from Dalton is wrong. But whatever the fortunate souls have learnt from the scriptures billions of years back is absolute. Today and in the past and even in the future. It's not changing. That's why it's called absolute knowledge. It's not relative. Relative in perspective of time. Or in perspective of different places. It doesn't change. It always remains the truth.

#### rebirth, karma, desire
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9k4VXR5266c?t=761
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "What Parikshit Maharaj is trying to do is, how not to come back. And what most of the people in this world is trying to do is, how to come back."
**Summary:** This segment elaborates on the two possibilities of afterlife: coming back (Aabhrittim) and not coming back (Anabhrittim), illustrating the common desire for rebirth with an analogy of hidden gold.

So there are two words used in Bhagavad Gita. Aabhrittim and Anabhrittim. Aabhrittim means, after you pass away, you come back. Aabhrittim. Comes. Anabhrittim. Not come back. So there are two possibilities. You may come back, or you may not come back. What Parikshit Maharaj is trying to do is, how not to come back. And what most of the people in this world is trying to do is, how to come back. You know, in villages, sometime back, my grandmother was telling, they used to keep gold in one pot, earthen pot. And they used to tuck the ground, and put that pot there. And, maybe nobody is aware that the home and the person passed away. And their next generation may not be knowing that there is a gold down. They may never have decked up. And this person might have kept with this intention, and next life he may get it back. So everybody is planning on to come back. But we all know, he will not get back. Somebody else will take over. So let's understand, what is Anabhritti and what is Abhritti. In what situation somebody can come back, in what situation somebody doesn't come back.

#### demotion, promotion, karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9k4VXR5266c?t=870
**Verse References:** BG 8.24-26
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "You can come back, by being demoted, or you can come back by being promoted."
**Summary:** This segment explains that coming back (Abhritti) can happen in two ways: demotion for those who don't follow scriptures and promotion for those who do, setting the stage for further explanation.

In 8th chapter of Bhagavad Gita, it says that there are two options, coming back or not coming back. Jagdha sasvade mathe Ekaya, Ekaya means the first option. Ekaya yati anabhritti The first option, the first process is, you will not come back. Anabhritti Anaya, Anaya means the second one, the other one. Avar tehe punha, you will again come back. So there are two processes, two paths. The first one, is make you sure that, make sure that you don't come back. Another one is, make sure that you come back. Now this coming back, happens in two ways. One is called demotion, another called promotion. You can come back, by being demoted, or you can come back by being promoted.

**Summary:** This segment details coming back by demotion, explaining that those who disregard scriptural laws are reborn into lower species of life with lower consciousness.

In the 16th chapter, Krishna talks about, coming back by demotion. So you may come back, but, you will be demoted, in the next life. In which case, it happens, it happens, when you don't follow the laws, of the scriptures. If somebody doesn't follow the laws, of the scriptures, in this life, they will again come back, but with demotion. Adamaam gathiyam Asunim Asunim means low grade. So those who don't follow the laws, of the scriptures, they will again come back, but, in a low grade. Some lower species of life. A lower consciousness. But there are some people, who follow the laws, who follow whatever scriptures say. They do lot of karma tanda, whatever rituals are mentioned.

They will go to higher planets. They will go to higher planets. Surya Naloka means, the planet of Indra. How? By following, the instructions of the scriptures. And why there are people, who have so much money, and so much food, that they can't even, enjoy that. Too much. Now, you got the idea, why it is like this? Because somebody, who follows the laws, in this life, they will have, they will have lot of resources, lot of luxury, in the next life. Those who don't follow, they may not have. #### disparity, karma, free will

So, you got the idea, from where the disparity comes? Because there are two ways, of coming, coming back, in this world. Abhirti. Either by, coming back is Abhirti. Either by demotion, or by, promotions. If you have done good karma, you followed the laws, next life, you will be promoted. With lot of resources, intelligence, looks, knowledge, better consciousness. Because there are two ways, of coming back. But somebody may not get, those resources. So disparity is because, what we have done, in our past life. God should not be, playing for it. But what people do, they say, where is God? There are so many people, dying because of, so many sicknesses, critical illness, where is God? God is the one, who made the system. You want to get demoted, you can get demoted. He made the law of karma. And he gave a free will, to the living entities. Tatha ichchasi tatha kuru. Whatever you want to do, you do. Law of karma will take care of it. So, people come back, with different, kind of backgrounds. And that's why, there is disparity, in this world. So, who should be blamed, for it? The living entity, himself, and not the Lord.

#### transcendence, maya, liberation

**Summary:** This segment introduces the second path, Anabhritti (not coming back), explaining that it is achieved by transcending the three modes of material nature and becoming situated in Brahman, thus escaping the grip of Maya.

Now, the second option, the second path, is called, anabhirti. Means, we will not come back. So, the second option, the second path, is called, anabhirti. In what situation, a person will not come back? Any idea? Just. If somebody is doing karma, he will be promoted. The karma will be demoted. If he does a karma, then he will go to the spiritual world. Good. A karma. In simple language, anybody who transcend, the three modes, of material nature. They will still be acting. But their acting, will neither promote, or demote. It will do, anabhirti. 14th chapter, Krishna mentions this. maam cha yohad vichare, na bhakti yogena sevate, sa gunaan samtite tan, brahma puyaya kalpate. They will transcend, they will transcend, the three modes. And they will be situated, in the Brahman. Then what happens? Then they will not be under, the grip of maya. Dehvi hi esa gulmayi, man maya duratvaya, maam eviye pripadyante, mayam etam tadantite. They will be out, of that scope of maya. The erudition of maya.

You have to study, the Vedas. No need to perform, the yagyas. Or execute, tapasya, austerity. Just because, they become a yogi. Sadhavan bhajade yomaam, saame yukta tanho mataa. The best yogi, is one, who keep me, in his heart, who keep me, in his thoughts, in his consciousness, mat gaten antaraatmana. Achrena adi dakshati. Simar Bhagavatam, same canto, which we are reading, second canto, which says, the sound incarnation of Lord Krishna, the super soul, enters into the heart of self-realized devotee, sits on the lotus flower of his loving relationship, and thus, cleanses the dust of material association, such as, lust, anger, and hankering. Thus, it acts like an autumnal rain, upon pool of muddy water.

## HG Chanchalapathi Dasa

### Are We Part of the Drama of This World? | Sri Chanchalapathi Dasa | SB 2.5.26-29 | 19.07.2024
**Speaker:** HG Chanchalapathi Dasa

#### creation, elements, transformation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/D1giBxi2ovc?t=0
**Verse References:** SB 2.5.26-29
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The whole process of creation is an act of gradual evolution and development from one element to another reaching up to the variegatedness of the earth."
**Summary:** The speaker reads verses from Srimad Bhagavatam describing the transformation of elements in the process of creation.

Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya. Reading from Srimad Bhagavatam, Canto 2, Chapter 5, Verses 26-29. I will read myself. Reading from Srimad Bhagavatam, Canto 2, Chapter 5, Verses 26-29. Because the star is transformed, the air is generated with the quality of touch. And by previous succession of the air is also full of sound and the basic principle of duration of life, sense perception, mental power, and bodily strength. When the air is transformed in course of time and nature's course, fire is generated, taking shape with the sense of touch and sound. Since fire is also transformed, there is a manifestation of water full of juice and taste. As previously, it also has form and touch and is also full of sound. And water being transformed from all life on Earth appears knew in order and as previously becomes qualitatively full of juice, touch, sound and form respectively.

---
#### creation, elements, sense perception
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/D1giBxi2ovc?t=149
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The qualities of sense perception are fully represented in the earth and they are manifested in other elements to a lesser extent."
**Summary:** The speaker continues reading from the Srimad Bhagavatam, elaborating on the qualities of elements and their relationship to sense perception, emphasizing the role of air and the benefits of yogic exercises.

Aged 15 years. Burokar Katanyankaha, from thesavravabhyan gods, Baba Akram, Maharaja Sakyamundrya. Based on this bogotan image, light, the world and consciousness are not turned towards one conspicuous feeling effected by the devils. Avarikasat jna kya ajanya jna jnakatseya? As we will. The whole process of creation is an act of gradual evolution and development from one. HomКyaka kttur. The whole process of creation is an act of gradual evolution and development from one element to another reaching up to the variegatedness of the earth as so many trees, plants, mountains, rivers, reptiles, birds, animals, and varieties of human being. The quality of sense perception is also evolutionary, namely generated from sound, then touch and form, from touch to form, taste and order are also generated along with the gradual development of strife, air, fire, water, and earth. They are all mutually the cross and effect of one another, but the original cross is the Lord himself in plenary portion as Maha Vishnu lying in the trouser water of the Mahat Tattva. As such, Lord Krishna is described in Brahma Samhita as the cross of all crosses, and this is confirmed in the Vajra Dhrita 10.8 as follows: The qualities of sense perception are fully represented in the earth and they are manifested in other elements to a lesser extent. In the strife there is sound only, whereas in the air there are sound and touch, in the fire there are sound, touch, and shape, and in the water there is taste also along with the other perceptions, namely sound, touch, and shape. In the earth, however, there are all the above mentioned qualities with an extra development of order also. Therefore, on the earth there is a full display of variegatedness of life which is originally started with the basic principle of air. Diseases of the body take place due to degenerate, due to derangement of air within the earthly body of the living being. Mental disease result from special derangement of the air within the body, and as such yodit exercise is especially beneficial to keep the air in order so that diseases of the body becomes almost nil by such exercise when they are properly done. The duration of life is also in traces and one can have control over death also by such practices. A perfect yoti can have command over death and treat the body at the right moment when he is competent to transfer himself to a suitable planet. The birthday yodi however surpasses all the yodit yodis because by dint of his devotional service he is promoted to the region beyond the material sky and is placed in one of the planets in the spiritual strike by supreme will of the Lord, the controller of everything. So.

#### creation, drama, roles
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/D1giBxi2ovc?t=372
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "We are all the living entities, actors, and we have to play our role, some as men, some as women, some intelligent men, some foolish men, some intelligent women, some not so intelligent women, all kinds."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of a film director and actors to explain the roles of Lord Brahma and living entities in the creation, emphasizing that we are all actors playing different roles in this cosmic drama.

The process of creation is being described in this chapter. The chapter is called the cause of all causes. So in this verse we are seeing today how sky is transformed, air is transformed, air is generated and air has the some of the qualities of touch and sound and so on. So actually this is a description and then we saw how from air, air and other different perceptions also come about. Suppose let's say I'm a film director and I'm planning a film, and in that film I want some of you to become the king, some of you to become the Queen, some of you to become warriors, some of you to become an invader villain who will come and do all nonsense, all wrong things, and some of you soldiers. And so now in filmmaking there is the one important step after the script, casting all that is done, that's called set design because it's a story of a king, queen, all this, we need a palace, we need a battleground, we need horses, we need elephants, we need soldiers. Next as a film director I'll start planning all of this so that when we turn on the lights and all of you can act out very correctly. King will play his role, Queen will play her role, and then the invader will come and all the different things will as per the story will unfold. So in the same way, Lord Brahma who is the director of this universe, creator of the universe, he is doing the set design as we read in the Bhagavatam. So we are all the living entities, actors, and we have to play our role, some as men, some as women, some intelligent men, some foolish men, some intelligent women, some not so intelligent women, all kinds. And for this drama to unfold, we need homes, we need buildings, we need plants, trees, planets. So Brahma is creating all of this.

#### drama, illusion, identity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/D1giBxi2ovc?t=632
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Very rarely people think, they are all busy with their roles, they have to play mother, father, husband, wife, and they want to play it out nicely so that everybody is impressed with the role I am playing."
**Summary:** The speaker poses the question of why we are part of this cosmic drama, explaining that although it feels real, it is temporary like a cinema, and people are generally preoccupied with playing their roles without questioning the purpose.

So a fundamental question is, why are we all part of this drama? But this is like a, this, okay, that's like a drama, cinema is all right, but this looks like real life. This doesn't look like drama. No, actually it is a fact. What is a drama, what is a cinema? After some time, the curtains will close and the lights are turned off and drama ends. So even in our lives, one day curtain will be drawn, lights will be turned off, our drama will end, but it doesn't appear like that. It looks like real, you know, this is my wife, this is my husband, this is my home, this is my father, this is my mother, these are my children, this is my house. Why all of this has come about? These are deeper questions. One is you are a king, play properly your role as a king, you Queen play properly Queen as a role as a queen, do your proper, be a strong king, all that is one thing, but a very fundamental deeper question is why have I become an actor in this drama? But very rarely people think, they are all busy with their roles, they have to play mother, father, husband, wife, and they want to play it out nicely so that everybody is impressed with the role I am playing. We rarely think why this drama is going on and why have I become part of this drama, what is all this?

So here from the Vedic literatures we get an understanding of this. We are all spirit souls, just like in the drama or in the cinema there is set designer and makeup artist, costume designer. The costume designer will design for the king, you have to play the role of a king so you should look like a king, you she must look like a queen, all the costume design will happen, palace is built, Brahma is doing all this, you should get a male body, you should get a female body, you should look like this, you should look like this, trees, plants, but inside this body is the spirit soul. We are the spirit soul, we are playing the role of a man, of a woman, of an animal, of a bird for some time and then the drama ends and then we will be an actor. After he finishes one drama he'll get commissioned to do another drama so we will get another opportunity, we will go to another situation. Now the Vedic literatures provides a very very significant perspective why have we become part of this drama, what is this going on and that is we are all spirit souls, we take on this body and the body has male body, female body, animal body has designed by a by Lord Brahma, the creator of all this. Why has the soul become entrapped like this and playing this different role?

#### Krishna, desire, independence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/D1giBxi2ovc?t=931
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This Jeeva Shakti has developed a desire, I want to be independent of Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that we are all amshas of Krishna, like sunlight is to the sun, but unlike sunlight, we have developed a desire to be independent of Krishna, which is an unconstitutional desire.

Very nicely Prabhupada has written in the purport, previous purport. As an Amsha, we are all spirit soul. Amsha of Krishna. Just like there is sunlight. Sunlight has come into the room but some minutes back it was in the sun and it has come all the way and traveled here. Isn't it? We understand that. Now can the sun, sunshine, sunlight, is it possible for the sunlight to be independent of the sun? Can sunlight exist without the sun? Not possible at all, isn't it? Constitutionally, it is not possible. The sunlight is an emanation of the sun. In the same way, we are all living entities. We are all conscious entities coming from Krishna. We are all living entities. We are all conscious entities coming from Krishna. We are all conscious entities coming from Krishna. We are all conscious entities coming from Krishna. But, this conscious entities have developed one desire. Sunshine has no desire. It is just light energy. Whereas, we are also energy, Jeeva Shakti of the Lord. And this Jeeva Shakti has developed a desire, I want to be independent of Krishna. I want to be independent of Krishna. I want to be independent of Krishna. I want to be independent of Krishna. Krishna. Just like sunlight has come from sun, we are also Jeeva Shakti coming from Krishna, the Shaktimaan. But the difference is that is a inert, lifeless energy, light energy, heat energy. Whereas we are a Shakti which has got Chetana, consciousness. And this Chetana Shakti, Chit Shakti has developed a desire because it's Chetana. Chetana means has desire, has thinking, feeling. And this Chit Shakti has developed a desire, I must be independent of Krishna.

#### illusion, independence, unconstitutional desire
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/D1giBxi2ovc?t=1129
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This desire of the living entity to be independent of Krishna is called unconstitutional desire."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the unconstitutional desire to be independent of Krishna, comparing it to a dream where sunlight exists without the sun, and explaining that Krishna creates the illusion of the material world to give us the impression of independence.

It is not possible. Sunshine cannot be independent of the sun. It is an emanation of the sun. This desire of the living entity to be independent of Krishna is called unconstitutional desire. It is not constitutional. It is not by our makeup. How we are composed, how we are formed, what are we made of? We are made of Krishna's energy. We have come from Krishna. So we can never sever that. But we have developed a desire. I want to be independent of Krishna. This is possible only in illusion. For example, we can think of sunshine without sun in our dream. We can say last night I had a dream, you know, there was so much sunlight. I looked up at the sky and I didn't see the sun at all. But there was sunshine. Yes, such a thing is possible in illusory dream. Dream will go away. It's a temporary existence in the night. And when we wake up, it's all gone. Right? In the same way, Krishna through Brahma creates an illusion of this world for the living entities to give an impression that we are independent of Krishna.

#### desire, God, independence

**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Our spirit of independence is such that I am here, I am independent, I have my independent desires and ambitions, and God is there, I want God's blessing to fulfill my independent desires."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses how even those who recognize God often seek His blessings to fulfill their own independent desires, rather than aligning with Krishna's plan, highlighting the pervasive spirit of independence.

We are here in the material world. But among the living entities here, some of them have some God consciousness. So they recognize God, they go to temples, they worship, they follow some religion, some Dharma. But they follow religion, they follow Dharma. But they follow religion, they follow Dharma with an aim that God will bless me so that all my various desires I have with my family, with my profession, with myself, I can fulfill all those desires with God's blessing. This is the large population of the society thinks like this. Recognize God. But I have my desires and I should fulfill my desires, my ambitions with God's blessing. But then the reality is that we are a spirit, chetana shakti, coming from Krishna. We rarely think, what is Krishna's plan for me? What does Krishna want me to do? I have come into this world, I am playing different roles. But our spirit of independence is such that I am here, I am independent, I have my independent desires and ambitions. And God is there, I want God's blessing to fulfill my independent desires. You see, I want to be independent is manifested in the spirit of I want to have independent desires.

#### desire, God, dreams
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/D1giBxi2ovc?t=1378
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "God as a supplier of my dreams, as a fulfiller of my dreams. It's my dream. And God shall kindly fulfill my dreams."
**Summary:** The speaker continues to discuss how we form our ambitions based on societal inputs and rarely consider Krishna's plan, viewing God as a means to fulfill our own dreams, which represents a minimal recognition of God.

That's what we pursue. We have our plans. We get inputs from the society, from newspapers, from the heroes of the world, from different sources. And we form our ambitions and we have to do that. And we want to pursue. These are my desires, my ambition. Rarely we think, if I am part and parcel of Krishna, I have come from Krishna, what was Krishna's plan for me? This is the kind of an independent desire that we want to have. Because we want this desire, the independent desire, the independent existence, which is unconstitutional. Krishna creates an illusion in the material world. Gives us different roles and gives us an opportunity to play out our role, trying to be independent. That is the situation of the material world. That is what is happening here. So we take on different roles. I am man, I am woman, this is the family, this is my business. I must have a house, I must have big house. I must have big car, I must have big bank balance. And we work for all these things. Rarely we think, if we are all children of God, what does God want us to do? When we start thinking, what does God want? Can I know that? Can I align myself with God's intention? That is called Sharanagati. That is the next level of recognition of God. In the first stage, where we recognize God, I have my independent desires, I have my independent plans. I want God's blessing so that I can succeed in my own plan. This is also recognition of God, but a relatively very minimal recognition. God as a supplier of my dreams, as a fulfiller of my dreams. My dreams. It's my dream. And God shall kindly fulfill my dreams. See, this is the large population of the world, in every religion. This is the consciousness of the living entity.

#### Krishna, surrender, relationship
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/D1giBxi2ovc?t=1559
**Verse References:** BG 18.66
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We must surrender to Krishna. We must align our desires, our plans, our activities, with Krishna's activity."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the next level of recognizing God, which involves surrendering to Krishna and aligning our desires with His plan, leading to the understanding of our true relationship with Him, contrasting it with the temporary relationships in the material world.

The next level Krishna teaches in the Bhagavad Gita, in the Vedic literatures, is that, if you are part and parcel of me, Krishna. We are all part and parcel of Krishna. We have come from Krishna. We are an energy of Krishna. What does Krishna want me to do? What is his plan for me? If I am his. If I am his. If I am his. So, this is how Bhagavad Gita ends, by bringing the concept of Sarva-Dharman-Paridhyajya. Ma mekam sharanam vraja. We must surrender to Krishna. We must align our desires, our plans, our activities, with Krishna's activity. And then what happens? Then, Bhagavatam describes further, after sharanagati, after you align, you will come to understand, you have a relation with Krishna. Relation. Relation is a very powerful thing. Just like somebody is a mother, there is a child. The mother is always conscious, I should take care of my child. And child also, playing around a little bit, if some difficulty, some danger comes, mummy, she will run to the mother, and hold on to the mother. That's the meaning of relationship. There is activity. There is feelings. So, now we, next the Vedic literatures inform us, we have a relation with Krishna. What about all these relationships in the material world? This is a drama. It's only a drama relationship. No substance. Just like in the dream also, we sometimes have so many relationships, so many activities. When we wake up in the morning, all the relationship, all the activities has no meaning.

**Content Type:** philosophy

**Key Quote:** "The real sense of the word ahamkara, aham, my identity. My identity truly is I am a part and parcel of Krishna. But now I have developed a false identity. I am independent of Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker illustrates the illusory nature of material relationships with the example of a dream, then introduces the concept of Ahamkara (false ego) as the root of our false identity, where we believe we are independent of Krishna.

Say in the drama, I earn one crore rupees, and I put it safely into the bank. I am feeling very good. One crore rupees in the bank now. The alarm rings, and then I wake up. Will I run to the bank? Let me draw the one crore rupees. No. It's a dream. It has no substance. Same thing will happen to us, when our life's alarm rings, and we go out of this world. Where is all this relationship? Where is my house? Everything is gone. Reset to a new situation. So for all this, very nicely Bhagavatam brings out the primary principle is called Ahamkara. See that's how this Bhagavad, this verse began. So ahamkara iti prokto vikurvan sama bhutritha. It all begins with ahamkara. Ahamkara means what? The living entity having a false identity. Usually in our normal language, ahamkara, don't have so much ahamkara. Somebody is very proud, very arrogant, we say that. Right? Yeah, that's okay. It's in one sense. The real sense of the word ahamkara, aham, my identity. My identity truly is I am a part and parcel of Krishna. But now I have developed a false identity. I am independent of Krishna. I have my own life. I have my own plans. I have my own ambitions. I have my own aspirations. And I want to fulfill all these things. And there is God. I understand. I am a pious person. I am a God fearing, God believing person. I need God's blessing to fulfill all my things, my ambitions. But these ambitions are independent of God's plan. I don't know. God is meant for, you know, for fulfilling my desires. That is God.

---
#### bhakti, relationship, Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/D1giBxi2ovc?t=1893
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The truth is that we have an intense, true relationship with Krishna. It is an intense, loving relationship with Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the limited recognition of God as a fulfiller of desires with the path of Sharanagati, leading to a true relationship with God, which is substantial and real, and the experience of that relationship is called Bhakti.

This kind of a recognition of God to sharanagati, to God. And next, I have a relation with God. And that is a true relation. And all these relations are without substance. It's a temporary relation. And I must come to experience that relation which is substantial, which is true, which is real. And that experience and percept, understanding and experience of that relationship is called bhakti. And that bhakti relationship can become intense. Just like relationships can be very intense between people sometimes. Sometimes, even grown-up men, they have very intense relation with their father and mother. We could have come across such people. Of course, between husband and wife, there could be an intense relation. Between children and parents, there is intense. We all have experienced that. When we had to leave our homes and join ISKCON, join temple, our heart was pulling, father, mother. It was very intense. You see? But these are, all these are drama relationships which we are thinking, it's so intense, it is this, that, all that. It's a drama. Not true. But the truth is that we have an intense, true relationship with Krishna. It is an intense, loving relationship with Krishna.

---
#### creation, illusion, desire
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/D1giBxi2ovc?t=2028
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The living entity has desire to be independent. Now Lord has to fulfill that. It is not possible to fulfill that. Because it is an unconstitutional desire."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Srimad Bhagavatam prepares us to understand how Brahma creates the material world as a set for conditioned souls to play different roles, driven by the false desire for independence, which is unconstitutional.

So, Bhagavatam is preparing us to understand all this and describing here how Brahma created all these sets in the form of the body, in the form of the dress for the body, in the form of the palace, in the form of the houses, and in modern world, in the form of my factory, my shop, my bank, my bank balance, my car. All these different things have come about. That's why, it is very nicely described here in this verse. Sohankara iti prokto vikurvan samabhut tridha. This ahankara, the false identity the living entity wants to take up. The living entity has desire to be independent. Now Lord has to fulfill that. It is not possible to fulfill that. Because it is an unconstitutional desire. It can only be fulfilled in a dreamlike situation. That is why, Srila Prabhupada gives another example of an unconstitutional desire. There was a child. Child, you know children, they become cranky sometimes. They cry, cry so much. They are small children. And the mother is trying to pacify the child, put the child to sleep. Not able to do anything. So when such things happen, you know, in homes, the mother brought the child, you know, sometimes due to heat also, the children go cranky. So she brought the child out of the house in the night. And hoping that, you know, cool, little cool breeze, child will become alright. The child was still very cranky. And now the child looked at the sky and there was the moon. And now the child started demanding, get me the moon. I want the moon. So the mother is very perplexed. What is this? How can I get the moon? Moon is in the sky. The child's desire is an unconstitutional desire. It is not possible for the child to get the moon from the sky. Impossible. It is not in line with the composition and what the child is made of, what the moon is made of. But the child has developed a desire.

---
#### illusion, desire, Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/D1giBxi2ovc?t=2193
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We are related to Krishna, we are meant to have a serving relationship with Krishna but we have developed a desire, I want to be independent, I want to enjoy, I have my plans, I want to have nice relationship in the material world without Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of a child wanting the moon to illustrate how Krishna creates an illusory world to fulfill our unconstitutional desire for independence, leading us to become busy with illusory forms and relationships.

The father was an intelligent man. He was watching all this, how the wife, mother is struggling with the child and the child demanding the moon. The father, what he did, he went and brought one big mirror. He said, I want the moon. And the child saw the reflection of the moon in the mirror. And the child saw, oh, the moon is there. And the child was holding the mirror and the child became satisfied. Now I got the moon. Actually the child did not get the moon, got a reflection of the moon. We are related to Krishna, we are meant to have a serving relationship with Krishna but we have developed a desire, I want to be independent, I want to enjoy, I have my plans, I want to have nice relationship in the material world without Krishna. So Krishna has said, all right, I will create an illusory world with illusory forms, with illusory relationships, with illusory activities and we become busy here. This is what is happening to us. Very few people know this truth from the Shastra. Our great Acharyas have presented this to make us understand. This is what is happening to us here.

---
#### creation, realism, temporary
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/D1giBxi2ovc?t=2305
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Lord Brahma is creating this material world, minutely tweaking it to get a sense of realism for the audience."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Brahma meticulously designs the material world to create a sense of realism for us, with all the sensory experiences, but it is a temporary world, and enlightenment comes from recognizing this truth.

So this is the, how this, and for the, you know when you have to play the role of a king, someone has to play the role of a queen, somebody has an enemy, palace and finally why the cinema film, director is making all this so that the viewers will come and look, oh such a nice cinema it was. King he played his role where you look at the king, look at the palace he had, they should appreciate. So that there is a sense of realism. In the same way, Lord Brahma is creating this material world, minutely tweaking it to get a sense of realism for the audience. The sense of realism for us, the sense of rupa, forms, the sense of gandha, smell, the sense of taste, rasa, the sense of sparsha, soft, touch, the sense of sound, you see because of these things. Rasa, gandha, smell. Because of this we are having an experience. So Brahma is minutely designing and creating all these experiences. So that it looks like a real world for us. Actually it's a, it's a temporary world. We have to remember this. This is endless. This is enlightenment. This is enlightenment, that this is the truth. What is true is this body is not truth, it will go away. The spirit soul, that is eternal. And that spirit soul has a relation with Krishna. So Srimad Bhagavatam is preparing us for all this understanding gradually. Gradually telling how the material creation is coming about. How the sets are being prepared for the actors to play the role in the same way how the material world is being created for the conditioned souls to come and play different roles in this material world. That's the sub, someone's substance.

---
#### enjoyment, false ego, Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/D1giBxi2ovc?t=2466
**Verse References:** BG 9.10, BG 9.11
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Save and accept the Lord, no one can be called the enjoyer, but the living entity falsely desires to become the enjoyer. This is the origin of false ego."
**Summary:** The speaker quotes Srila Prabhupada, emphasizing that Krishna alone is the enjoyer, and the desire to be independent is the origin of false ego, which is a confidential truth revealed in the Bhagavad Gita.

So there is one very nice statement by Srila Prabhupada. Srila Prabhupada, in the previous purport, I'll read that, where is that, oh it's here. Factually, save and accept the Lord, no one can be called the enjoyer. Krishna alone is the enjoyer. Save and accept the Lord, save and accept the Lord, no one can be called the enjoyer, but the living entity falsely desires to become the enjoyer. This is the origin of false ego. This is the origin of ahamkara. This is the important truth Krishna reveals in the 9th chapter of the Bhagavad Gita. In the beginning of the chapter, 9th chapter, Krishna said, idam tu te guhyam pravakshyam anasuyave. Idam tu te guhyam pravakshyam anasuyave. Idam tu te guhyam pravakshyam anasuyave. I am going to tell you the most confidential knowledge. Pravakshyam anasuyave dhyana vigyana sahitam yajnatva mokshase ashubhata. And if you know this truth, you will be freed from all inauspiciousness. What is that truth? Most confidential truth Krishna has revealed in the 9th chapter. There are many aspects of the truth. One of them he is aham hi sarvayagnanam bhogtacha prabhurevaca. This is I am the master, I am the enjoyer of everyone's activities and sacrifices. Natumam abhijananti tatvenathas cavantite. One who don't accept this, I am the master and I am the enjoyer. This is the truth about me. This is the tattva. One who does not accept this. They come to this material world, in this world of illusion. This is the most difficult thing for people to accept. I am not the enjoyer, Krishna is the enjoyer.

### Does Everyone Have a Spiritual Purpose in Life? | Sri Chanchalapathi Dasa @ChanchalapathiDas
**Speaker:** HG Chanchalapathi Dasa

#### purpose of life, materialism
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/8mdZafj-zZs?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Most people, they have no clear understanding about spirituality and they have some duties connected with their family, with their profession and mostly they are absorbed in that."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the question of whether everyone has a spiritual purpose, noting that most people are absorbed in family and professional duties and lack a clear understanding of spirituality.

The question: does everyone have a spiritual purpose in life? Not everyone has a spiritual purpose or a goal in life. Most people, they have no clear understanding about spirituality, and they have some duties connected with their family, with their profession, and mostly they are absorbed in that.

---
#### spirit soul, body, eternal nature
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/8mdZafj-zZs?t=28
**Verse References:** BG 2.13, BG 2.20
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna teaches that we must understand that there is a body and there is a spirit soul residing in the body."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the fundamental teaching of the Bhagavad Gita: the distinction between the body and the eternal spirit soul, emphasizing the soul's permanence even after the body's death.

But everyone should have a spiritual purpose because we must understand our composition in our present situation. This is the first teaching of the Bhagavad Gita. Krishna teaches that we must understand that there is a body and there is a spirit soul residing in the body. The spirit soul is distinct and different from the body. That is why one day this body can have a heart attack or some other kind of a big disease and finally I will die. When I die, my body will be lying there. The body that everybody was relating with will be lying there. But then people will say, oh he is gone. Who has gone? What has gone? The spirit has gone. So, there is a dual situation here. There is the body and there is the spirit soul. Because of the presence of the spirit soul, the body is animated. I can sing, I can speak, I can do work, I can think, I can solve problems. All this I can do because of the presence of the spirit soul. When the soul leaves the body, it's a dead body. It is just carried and set on fire into ashes. Nothing more can be done with it, when the soul has left the body. But the soul, Krishna teaches, is eternal: na jayate namriyate vā kadācin na hanyate hanyamāne śarīre. And so, we must realize that this body is just an external, temporary covering for the soul. And what happens is, we don't understand these things.

---
#### spiritual development, purpose of life, balance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/8mdZafj-zZs?t=144
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Spirituality is about understanding and exploring our spiritual potential, our spiritual aspect of life, and everyone is supposed to do this."
**Summary:** The speaker advocates for incorporating a spiritual dimension into life alongside professional and personal pursuits, suggesting that life can run on multiple tracks, including a spiritual development track.

We only have goals and purposes and aspirations connected with the body, and my things connected, relations with the body. So, spirituality is about understanding and exploring our spiritual potential, our spiritual aspect of life. And everyone is supposed to do this. And when you have spiritual purpose, one may think that what will happen to my profession? No. That can also go on. Profession can go on and our spiritual progress also can go on. We can run our lives on multiple tracks. All people do that. People run, they have their professional life, they have their personal life, and they have some hobbies, some music, something they'll be practicing, cultivating. They have that and another track. So, life runs on multiple tracks. And we must have one track called spiritual development track. And we may do many other things. Our professional life, personal life. Along with this, we should fire another track in our lives called the spiritual understanding and spiritual development track. And in this way, we can bring in a spiritual purpose to our lives, not just the professional purpose, personal purpose, and some hobbies and such things. We also cultivate and develop and understand a spiritual purpose in life.

### Doubtless Understanding of Krishna | HG Chanchalapathi Dasa | SB 5 .1.4 | 21.06.2024
**Speaker:** HG Chanchalapathi Dasa

#### Krishna consciousness, Grihavrata, Doubt
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/8E1xQmrJR0M?t=60
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "A Grihavrata, one who has taken a vow to execute family duties, has no chance to become Krishna conscious."
**Summary:** King Pariksit's doubt is introduced: how can someone attached to family become perfectly Krishna conscious, given Prahlada Maharaj's statement about Grihavratas?

King Pariksit wondered how a person so attached to wife, children, and home could become so perfectly Krishna conscious. Prahalad Maharaj has said, 'Matirna Krishne Paratasvatova Mithobi Padhyeta Grihavrata Nam.' A Grihavrata, one who has taken a vow to execute family duties, has no chance to become Krishna conscious. This is because most Grihavratas are guided by sense gratification and therefore gradually glide down to the darkest regions of material existence. 'Adanta Ghovir Vishatam Tamisram.' How can they possibly become perfect in Krishna consciousness? Maharaj Pariksit asked Shukadeva Goswami to resolve this great doubt.

---
#### Attachment, Perfection, Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/8E1xQmrJR0M?t=147
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This is very surprising that he became Krishne Mathihi. He became attached to Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker explains Pariksit's question about how Priyavrata, attached to family, attained perfection in Krishna consciousness (Krishnecha Mathirajyutha).

So we are seeing in the first chapter of fifth canto questions asked by Pariksit to Shukadeva Goswami. He heard about Priyavrata becoming perfect in Krishna consciousness and so that has triggered a thought. And in today's verse he is saying, 'Samsa Yoham Mahan Brahman.' This is my Mahan Samshaya. This is my great doubt. Brahman, O Brahmana, referring to Shukadeva Goswami, O great Shukadeva Goswami. 'Dara Gara Sutadishu Saktasya Yatsiddhirabhut.' Dara, wife, Agara, home, Suta, children, Adishu. This is what happens in a human, in a Grahasta life. One becomes attached to wife, home, and children. 'Dara Gara Sutadishu Saktasya.' One becomes attached. Asakti, sakta, one who has become attached. 'Yatsiddhirabhut.' But you are also saying, Asakti, sakta, one who has become attached. Yet Siddhirabhut. But you are also saying, He achieved perfection. Siddhi. Perfection. And what kind of Siddhi? 'Krishnecha Mathirajyutha.' Mathihi, Prabhupada has here translated as became attachment. His mind, his consciousness became attached. Mathirajyutha. To Krishna. Krishnecha Mathirajyutha. To Krishna. Krishnecha Mathirajyutha. To Krishna. So this is very surprising that he became Krishne Mathihi. He became attached to Krishna.

---
#### Doubt, Attachment, Perfection

**Verse References:** —

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker continues to elaborate on King Pariksit's doubt, emphasizing Priyavrata's attachments and the seemingly contradictory attainment of 'Achutasiddhihi' (infallible perfection) in Krishna consciousness.

So the king continued, O great Brahmana, Brahman, this is my great doubt. 'Samsa Yoyam Mahan Brahman.' How was it possible, for a person like King Priyavrata, who was so attached, Saktasya, to wife, Dara, children, Suta, and home, Agaara, to achieve the topmost infallible perfection, Achutasiddhihi. Infallible perfection. In Krishna consciousness, Krishnemathihi. So, Some important points, Prabhupada has pointed out, in the translation, in the purport. So, Siddhihi. Perfection in life. Siddhirabhut. Perfection came about in his life. Priyavrata's life. And what was his life about? He was Dara, Agaara, Suta, Dishu, Saktasya. He was attached to wife, children, home, and all of that. He had a big family, big palace. He was a king. All of that.

#### Perfection, Spiritual Knowledge, Quest

**Audience:** Newcomers, General public

And how he became perfect? So, first of all, we must appreciate that there is a, there should be a search for perfection in human life. And, that quest, understanding, and attainment of that perfection, should be the dominant aspect of human life. But of course, when we are not enlightened, when we have no spiritual knowledge, we don't know what is perfection. There are many people in the world, trying to, very, very passionately, pursue some perfection in their life. Someone is trying to become a big politician. Someone is trying to become a big businessman. Someone may be trying to become a big scholar. Someone may be trying to become a famous person. So sometimes people have these conceptions, or not sometimes, most of the times. People have some conception like this. And they are very devoted to that perfection. I must achieve that. So, quest for perfection is not a, quest for perfection is not an unusual thing. In human society, even in our Kali Yuga society, that quest for perfection is there.

But what is perfection? Because all of these perfections, like becoming rich, becoming powerful, this world has seen many, many powerful people, rich people, successful people, famous people. Prabhupada used to often mention, wherever Gandhiji went, because Prabhupada was a contemporary of Gandhiji. Of course, Gandhiji was a few decades older. But Prabhupada had seen, and Gandhiji was a very, very famous and very powerful person. And wherever he went, wherever he went, people would be glorifying, Bapuji, Bapu, Bapu, like that. And, sometimes, Gandhiji would get so fed up, of people screaming, he would close his ears. They are all screaming, shouting. He would close his ears. And we know that, when finally Gandhiji was assassinated, and then, his procession of his body was being taken, for the final rites in Delhi, New Delhi. You must have seen pictures. Thousands and thousands, and probably lakhs of people, participating in that. So you can see how powerful he was. But then, where is he now? Where is that person? Of course, in our country, every city has, a road named after him. Right? But mostly, it has all become MG Road, and many people don't even know what MG Road is. Right? So this is what happens to, powerful, powerful people. Okay, Mahatma Gandhi is in a, in one sense, quite recent memory, we may say. If you take hundreds of years before that, there were very powerful kings, Napoleon, they were also very powerful, in their own countries, in their own kingdoms. But what is their power now? Where are they now?

And that's how Srila Prabhupada explains, the Shastras explain, Krishna has all these opulences to an unlimited extent. And hence, He becomes captivating. That absolute truth has all of these attractive qualities, by which one can be drawn. And hence, that absolute truth, has the name, Krishna. Someone who can attract everyone.

#### Krishna, Attraction, Perfection
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/8E1xQmrJR0M?t=822
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

And all of this is present simultaneously, to an unlimited extent, in that Bhagavan. And hence, He is known as, all attractive. And in Sanskrit, the name for all attractives, Krishna. 'Sarvam Krishyuti.' Who can attract. So Siddhi, perfection, idea of perfection in human society is there. In this verse, Pariksit Maharaj is pointing out, He achieved the perfection of Krishnamatihi. Attachment to Krishna. Krishnamatihi. He became attached to Krishna. Krishnamatihi. That, when you become attached, when your consciousness is attached to Krishna, that is Siddhi. That is the infallible perfection.

Krishnamatihi. Krishna Consciousness. Abhipadhyate. Development. So in other words, this Krishna Consciousness is some, development that happens, in our consciousness. We are all conscious beings. This table is not conscious. We are conscious beings. And, if our consciousness becomes absorbed, consciousness becomes attached, to Krishna, that is considered to be, Siddhirabhut. That is considered to be the perfection, according to Srimad Bhagavatam, according to Bhagavad Gita. So we all have a consciousness, and, left to itself, our consciousness will become attached to, body, home, family, children. #### Absolute Truth, Devotion, Cause and Effect
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/8E1xQmrJR0M?t=1026
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Absolute truth, is the ultimate cause, of everything, including us."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that because we originate from the Absolute Truth (Krishna), we should naturally be devoted to Him, just as we are devoted to our parents, who are partial causes of our existence.

Why? Because, we are all, coming from the Absolute Truth. If somebody is attached to his father and mother, they will say it is a natural thing. If somebody is not at all attached, then something, what is this? He is not caring for his father, caring for his mother. They will say like that. Because, we have come from our father and mother, to love them, to show affection to them, and to serve them, is considered the right thing. But then, our father and mother, they are, our source, our cause, to a limited extent. Not to an unlimited extent. Not the complete cause, a partial cause. So, If we, this is a philosophical aspect, we have to, we have to reflect on it. Yes, what caused me? My father and mother caused me. Now what caused my father and mother? My grandfather, and grandmother, they caused my father, my mother. So in other words, they are, my father and mother were, it's a chain of causes and effects, and they were one cause, one level of cause. And even in that, they were a partial cause. We cannot attribute everything to them, philosophically speaking. Not that we are minimizing father and mother, but philosophically we are analyzing.

---
#### Absolute Truth, Devotion, Enlightenment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/8E1xQmrJR0M?t=1139
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Becoming absorbed in Krishna, that Krishnamati, is considered to be the highest perfection."
**Summary:** The speaker argues that the Absolute Truth is the ultimate cause of everything, and therefore deserves our absolute devotion. He connects this to enlightenment, stating that a more enlightened person seeks higher causes, ultimately seeking the highest cause, Krishna.

Like say for instance, there is a child who was, who was born blind. If you say father and mother, are the cause of the child, we must blame that father and mother. Why did you give birth to a blind child? You couldn't do something properly, child is nicely born. They'll say, what to do? It was not in our control. It was not in your control. That's exactly we are saying. They are partial cause, they are not the ultimate cause. So, Absolute truth, is the ultimate cause, of everything, including us. So just like we have to be devoted, to our partial cause, father and mother, we should also be, absolutely devoted, to our absolute cause, Krishna. Now this one, being, devoted to father and mother, being devoted to Krishna, the absolute father and mother, absolute truth, it is a, function of, one's enlightenment. If one is more enlightened, he will understand, No I must seek, higher and higher, causes, ultimate cause. And when one is fully enlightened, he seeks the highest cause, the ultimate cause. So becoming, Krishnamatihi, who is Krishna, who we are, and becoming attached to Krishna, being, becoming absorbed in Krishna, that Krishnamati, is considered to be the highest, perfection. In fact, Srila Prabhupada coined this word, Krishna consciousness, and he says, That I coined this word, from Rupa Goswami's, use of a certain verse, certain expression in Sanskrit, connected to with, connected with this.

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna bhakti is not a dry concept, no, it is full of Rasa, it's full of juice."

Rupa Goswami uses one expression, Krishna bhakti rasa bhavitha matihi, Matihi means, same, Krishnamatihi, consciousness, Krishna bhakti rasa, there is a mellow, Krishna bhakti is not a dry, concept, no, it is full of, Rasa, it's full of juice, Krishna bhakti rasa, Bhavitha, imbued, absorbed, immersed in, Matihi, such a matihi, absorbed, or imbued with, Krishna bhakti rasa, that is the expression, I took from Rupa Goswami's works, and coined the word, Krishna consciousness. So, Bhagavatam is pointing out, Krishnamatihi, Krishna bhakti rasa bhavitha matihi, that is the perfection of life, There is another expression, in the Bhagavatam, about Shukadeva Goswami, which describes, His matihi, consciousness.

#### Vedic Literature, Purushottama, Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/8E1xQmrJR0M?t=1650
**Verse References:** BG 15
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "What does the way all of the Vedic literatures finally boil down to? What is the final statement?"
**Summary:** The speaker introduces verses from the 15th chapter of Bhagavad Gita, where Krishna explains that He is the Purushottama, the supreme person, and that understanding this is the essence of Vedic knowledge.

How carefully Prabhupada has picked up all these things and presented to us in modern context. This is described in the Bhagavad Gita and Bhagavatam. There is one very nice verse, two verses I will read this in the Bhagavad Gita. It's a short chapter, 15th chapter. Krishna concluding, and in the concluding verses, Krishna is actually explaining what does the way all of the Vedic literatures finally boil down to. What is the final statement? It's one of the very very important and interesting verses. Yomam evam asam mudho janati purushottamam Maam janati asam mudho janati Asam mudho janati means doubtlessly know. Know what? Yomam evam asam mudho janati purushottamam I am the purushottamam. There are many purushas. Already Krishna explained there are two kinds of purushas. Some of them, some of the purushas are called kshara purushas. Some of the purushas are called akshara purushas. And kshara purushas are those who are in the material world and akshara purushas are those who are in the spiritual world. There are a few verses before Krishna points that out. Dvavimau purushau loke ksharascha akshara yevacha There are dvavimau purushau loke. There are two kinds of purushas.

#### Purushottama, Kshara, Akshara
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/8E1xQmrJR0M?t=1774
**Verse References:** BG 15.18-19
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "And I am transcendental to both kshara and akshara purushas."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the concepts of 'kshara' (fallible) and 'akshara' (infallible) purushas, and introduces the 'uttama purusha' (supreme person), who is transcendental to both and maintains the universes.

Ksharaha sarvabhuthani kutastho akshara ucyate Kutastha means situated in the spiritual world. Kutastha. Kutastha means fixed up. Only in the spiritual world you will be fixed up. In the material world you will not be fixed up, you will be vacillating. One idea, one concept, another concept, different pursuits we will be doing. Only when we are in the spiritual world, kutastha akshara ucyate. So he said that there are two kinds of purushas. But then uttama purushastva anyaha There is one more third purusha who is anya, different from these kshara and akshara purushas and he is uttama purusha. And that purusha is paramatmeti udakhrataha. He is the paramatma. He is celebrated as paramatma. lokatrayam avishya He enters the three worlds. vibhrithyavyayaishvaraha vibhrithi He maintains the three universes. All the universes. and yasmat ksharamatitoham aksharadapichottamaha And I am transcendental to both kshara and akshara purushas. Athosmi loke vedhecha pratithah purushottamaha And hence I am celebrated in the Vedic literatures and in the society as the purushottama.

And one who yomam evam asammudo purushottamam janati purushottamam One who knows Krishna as that supreme personality doubtlessly. So coming to a state of understanding, janati, asammudo purushottamam maam purushottamam Krishna is the purushottama. That doubtlessly, without confusion, without doubt, One who knows sa sarvavit He is the knower of everything. He is the knower of everything. sa sarvavit And when he knows everything, what does he do? bhajati maam He renders devotional service unto me. And how does he do it? sarvabhavena bharata sarvabhavena bhajati With all his heart, in all respects, with bhava, with devotion, with love, bhajati maam He renders devotional service unto me.

So, this is krishnamatihi. #### Material World, Reformation, Knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/8E1xQmrJR0M?t=2012
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker continues the prison analogy, contrasting the prisoner who simply performs tasks within the prison with the one who understands the purpose of the prison as an opportunity for reformation and understanding one's relationship with the Supreme.

In prison, there may be some, there may be one electrician who got, landed in the prison, and they will use him in the prison, you fix this bulb, fix this wire, and he will be doing that. So, somebody may say, oh, he is such a good electrician, you know, in our prison, he can fix all problems of electric problems, all those, good. Someone may be a cook, someone may be something else, all kinds of prisoners are there, but among all of them, one who has understood that, I made a mistake, I should become law-abiding, he has really served the purpose of the prison, he has understood the purpose of the prison, he has understood everything, and he is now. In the same way, in the material world, we may find many intelligent people, there are scientists, there are economists, there are so many people, they know so many things, they are like the electrician, cook and tailor, they know so many things of this world, they have not understood everything, why this world is there, why the material world is there, it's an opportunity for reformation, to understand our relation with the supreme.

One who has understood, sa sarva vith, he is the one who has understood everything, that is perfection of knowledge. **Speaker:** HG Chanchalapathi Dasa

#### devotion, Narayana, purpose
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/L-vx4MHi0T8?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Everything is related to Vasudeva, Narayana."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that just as businesses focus on pleasing customers, everything, according to the Vedas, is focused on and meant for Narayana, and without that connection, it lacks meaning or value.

Just like in business they say, we are very customer focused. That means they are saying, we are customer para. We want to please our customers. We want to satisfy our customers. We want to delight our customers. Like this, right? This is how management works. Brahma is saying, Vedas are Narayana para. Loka, focused on Narayana. Lokas are meant for Narayana. Makaha, the sacrifices are meant for Narayana. Everything is related to Vasudeva, Narayana. Without that, it has no meaning or value.

### Harness Body and Mind Spiritually | Sri Chanchalapathi Dasa | ISKCON Bangalore Sanga
**Speaker:** HG Chanchalapathi Dasa

#### mind control, self-control, spiritual practice
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/U6B5gqgV8aQ?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "As spiritualists, we do not trust our mind."
**Summary:** HG Chanchalapathi Dasa explains that spiritualists must understand the forces within the body, especially the mind, and not surrender to its dictates, emphasizing that intelligence and the soul are higher than the mind.

Prabhupada explains that a spiritualist has to be very sober, introspective, thoughtful, grave. Vacho vegam, manasa, krodha vegam, jihva vegam, udhara upastha vegam. It means a kind of a force in the body. Prabhupada gives the example, just like our body is designed in such a way, when there is a nature's call, it creates a force, irresistible force. We all have to rush to the washroom. In the same way, there are other forces in this body and one of them is the force of the mind. As spiritualists, we must know these workings of the mind, workings of the forces in this body, so that we know how to deal with them. Shukadev Goswami is giving one such example. He is saying, this mind which is unsteady and which comes with a force, don't trust this mind. This is the first instruction. So, Prabhupada explains, as spiritualists, we do not trust our mind. Means, we do not surrender to the dictations of our mind. The mind comes with a force. Mind comes with an idea. Let's do this. Let's do that. You do this. You do that. And it comes with a certain force. Don't trust your mind. This is the instruction. Watch the mind's action. In other words, intelligence is higher than the mind. Soul is higher than the intelligence. And we are that soul.

---

### How to Become Prideless? | Sri Chanchalapathi Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Chanchalapathi Dasa

#### humility, spiritual development, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/IyGx5N8hp2Q?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If we make an attempt to become prideless on a mental plane, there is a chance that we will become proud of pridelessness."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the paradox of becoming proud of pridelessness and emphasizes that genuine spiritual qualities arise from devotion to Krishna and attaining a spiritual platform, rather than directly trying to acquire them.

Some time back, one devotee asked me, what if a devotee becomes proud of pridelessness? Yes, it happens. It can happen. We can become proud of many other virtues that a devotee is supposed to have. But, our method of developing all these qualities is not by trying to acquire those qualities separately. We first become devotees of Krishna. To become genuine devotees of Krishna, we have to come to a spiritual platform. Our principle is, in such a person's heart, all divine qualities will come about. If we make an attempt to become prideless on a mental plane, there is a chance that we will become proud of pridelessness. But our attempt is to come to a spiritual platform. And when one comes to a genuine, spiritual platform, we will develop genuine spiritual qualities.

#### Privacy, Incorporating the society, Lawyer contact
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/irID2uAh7lY?t=310
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Actually it was around that time Srila Prabhupada begins to contemplate on incorporating the society there was one young American call who would come and meet Srila Prabhupada and call was half black, a young American who lived with his wife, Eva, not very far from 94 Bavari.
