#### suffering, death, divine sanction
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/HdPMySPtJv4?t=1100
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Why God is giving this suffering to me?"
**Summary:** The speaker shares a personal experience from Lucknow, where he was awakened by the wailing of neighbors due to the death of an old woman. This leads him to contemplate why a Supreme, powerful, and well-wishing Lord would sanction such intense suffering.

Sometimes people ask this question. Especially when there are some miseries in this world, somebody is suffering. Why God is giving this suffering to me? Very recently I was in a, you may know that we had a program in Lucknow. So we were staying in a in a devotees house. And so early in the morning at about 2.30 am I was suddenly woken up. What sounded like, you know sometimes in the night you hear the howling of dogs or something. It sounded like that. So woke up for a moment for a while. And then okay some dogs, stray dogs everywhere you know. And went back to sleep. And then again after a while at about 3.30 I was woken up. And then I realized what I had heard was not actually howling of dogs. It was wailing of people. And it was much louder at 3.30 am. I woke up and I tried to intently hear. I could make out there were at least two male grown up men's voice wailing. And many women wailing. And some children also wailing. Crying out. It was a very shocking thing. And you know it's all very cold in North India this time. So the windows were all shut. So I went to the window and tried to see. And then with great difficulty pushed the window open and I immediately as as the cold wave hit similarly there were loud wailing voices. Just hit me back. Just shut the door and I didn't know what to do. Something's wrong with the neighbors. And then I turned on the light and then I came out. And then the devotee of the house the host where we were staying he came down the steps and he said Prabhu, wait, wait some neighbor I'll just go see and come. And he wrapped himself with him. And then he walked out. And then and this wailing was continuing. And then after a while he came back and he said Prabhu, the neighbor somebody passed away. Some some old woman. And at 2.30 they rushed her to the hospital. And then at 3.30 they brought her back and they are dead. And she is dead. And there are her sons and daughters and grandchildren eight of them all of them are living there. And so then he went back and then by then I just got ready and we were chanting. So in the morning when I was chanting at that time it went on for about an hour. Loud wailing of men women children. So at that time when I was chanting you know when you come across this kind of a thing multiple thoughts come to your mind. At that time I was particularly thinking about how people suffer so much when there is a death in the family. Rightfully someone very beloved to them. And then I was also thinking about how people suffer when there is a death in the family. And it went on and on and on for nearly one hour. You know to be wailing like that so intense. And here we see Krishna is the best well-wishing friend of all living entities. And Krishna the Supreme Lord the Supreme Powerful Lord He has sanctioned such a misery to a family. Everyone is suffering. Why is this so? How do we understand why the Lord has sanctioned such intense suffering that people are crying so much? Why there is death like this?

#### modes of nature, knowledge, ignorance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/HdPMySPtJv4?t=1419
**Verse References:** BG 14.17
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "From sattva guna, jnana comes about. So that is why for spiritual life sattva guna is very important."
**Summary:** The speaker recalls a verse from the Bhagavad Gita (14.17) discussing the modes of material nature (sattva, rajas, and tamas). He explains that sattva leads to knowledge, rajas to greed, and tamas to madness, illusion, and ignorance.

At that time I was reminded of one verse in the Bhagavad Gita. Very interesting verse. In the fourteenth chapter of the Gita Krishna is talking about the modes of material nature. Krishna devotes an entire chapter on the modes of material nature. Of course Krishna talks about modes of material nature very early even in the second chapter onwards. But there is an entire chapter devoted even in the eighteenth chapter Krishna is talking. But there is a there is a this fourteenth chapter is specially dedicated for the three modes of material nature because they are very important for spiritual life. Why it is important is very nicely described before I come to the verse connected with the wailing family members. Here Krishna says, sattvat sanjayate jnanam rajaso lobha evaca pramada mohav tamaso bhavato ajnanam evaca In summary Krishna describes in this verse what happens because of the influence of sattva, rajas and tamo gunas. sattvat sanjayate jnanam From sattva guna jnana comes about. So that is why for spiritual life sattva guna is very important. sattvat sanjayate jnanam rajaso lobha evaca By rajas greed comes about. sattvat sanjayate jnanam rajaso lobha evaca pramada mohav tamaso bhavato ajnanam evaca And because of tamasa three things Krishna is listing. One is pramada madness moha illusion ajnana bhavata ha and ignorance comes about.

#### modes of nature, illusion, ignorance

Just see because of tamo guna, because of tamasic influence. So this is the influence of the rajas, tamas, sattvam, rajas and tamas. And hence if we are under tamo guna we will be mad, we will be in illusion and we will be ignorant about the realities. So after describing all these effects of the three gunas, Krishna says something, as a last verse in this discussion. And then Arjuna asks a question. And the last verse that Krishna is describing in this, not the last verse of the chapter, but in this series of verses describing the three gunas and their influence and what happens and all of that.

#### transcendence, liberation, immortality
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/HdPMySPtJv4?t=1632
**Verse References:** BG 14.26
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "One will be freed from the dukkha of janma mrutyu jara dukkhair vimukta and amrutam ashnute and one can enjoy immortality."
**Summary:** The speaker quotes Bhagavad Gita 14.26, explaining that one can transcend the three modes of material nature and be freed from the suffering of birth, death, and old age, ultimately enjoying immortality.

guna netan atitya triyam deha deha samud bhavan janma mrtyu jhara dukhair vimukto amrutam ashnute And then Krishna is saying there is one state of existence. What is that? etan gunan etan triyam gunan atityah gunan etan atitya triyam There is a state of existence when we can transcend the three modes of material nature. atitya triyam gunan atitya Dehi deha samud bhavan Deha samud bhavan The three modes, the three gunas are associated with the body. Deha samud bhava They are produced in this body. It's connected with the body. It's not connected with the Dehi. Dehi is the soul. Dehi is the occupant in the Deha. So there is Deha and there is Dehi. And the Deha, Deha samud bhavaha Trigunan The three modes of material nature are associated with the body. Gross body and the subtle body. Whereas Dehi, the soul is not connected with this and he is, he can be situated in a atitya gunan atitya ha stage. Then what happens if we do that? If we come to that stage of being transcendental to the influence of the three modes immediately Krishna gives a wonderful benefit. What is that? janma mrtyu jara dukkhair vimukto amrutam ashnute One will be freed from the dukkha of janma mrtyu jara dukkhair vimukta and amrutam ashnute ashnute means to enjoy. And one can enjoy immortality.

One can enjoy what is deathlessness. In other words one will come to a transcendental state. One will come to a spiritual state when one understands that I am not the body I am a spirit soul and when he is able to understand that he is a spirit soul and nothing can affect that spirit soul. No weapon can destroy. Nothing can touch that spirit. Material things can touch that spirit soul. So janma mrtyu jara all these are things connected with the body and he will be freed from the sufferings. janma mrtyu jara dukkhair vimukto amrutam ashnute So the Supreme Lord Krishna is very concerned about our suffering. He is the Supreme Friend. And he is naturally concerned about our suffering. And hence he has given us a process how we can become freed from that. And we close our eyes to all that become influenced by the rajoguna and tamoguna become greedy in the material world become involved in material activities because of the tamoguna become mad after material things be in illusion be in ignorance and then complain what is this so much suffering Krishna is giving us in this material world?

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker explains that Krishna has provided a process for enlightenment, but in Kali Yuga, the influence of rajas and tamas is strong. He stresses the need to adopt a spiritual process to overcome these influences and criticizes frivolous approaches to spirituality based on ignorance.

This is not fair. So Krishna has given us a process of becoming enlightened. If we don't take up this process by nature we will be like that especially in Kali Yuga. Kali Yuga things are not at all congenial for spiritual life. So in Kali Yuga the circumstances are arranged in such a way that rajas and tamas influence is very strong. So we have to take up some methods some spiritual process by which we will come out of the influence of rajoguna and tamoguna and then we will strive to come out of these influences come to sattva guna and even beyond guna netana tityatrin dehi deha samudbhavan we can come to that state. And this is what Krishna is teaching. And that is why Srila Prabhupada says so this is not some kind of a magic thing magical thing some ashes are given some other magic is given some gold is given these are all actually very silly things. Human life is meant for something very serious something very grave something serious is happening around us there is suffering in this world and we must know what to do how to solve this problem there is some there is a spiritual wisdom connected with this and how to get that spiritual wisdom our most well wishing friend Krishna has provided that information instead of taking all these things very seriously very frivolously we consider we pursue spiritual life somebody is giving some ashes somebody is solving some problem somebody is giving some gold and people run after all these this is simply because of ignorance not knowing what is spiritual life what is going on in this world and very unfortunately there are many many people like this in Kali Yuga who are misleading people so Krishna being the well wishing friend.

#### Krishna, devotees, spiritual master
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/HdPMySPtJv4?t=1962
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "No one can be a greater well wishing friend to any living entity than the Supreme Personality of Godhead he is so kindly disposed towards everyone that in spite of our completely forgetting our relationship with the Supreme Lord he comes himself."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes Krishna's kindness and willingness to come personally, as Lord Krishna and Lord Chaitanya, and through His bona fide devotees, to reclaim fallen souls. He highlights that Krishna's mission, Chaitanya's mission, and the spiritual master's mission are the same: to reclaim the fallen souls and give spiritual knowledge.

Prabhupada has made a very nice point just like sometimes it happens in our human interaction if someone doesn't recognize us or respect us we will become very upset and turn away from that person next time he is coming this way you turn away that side look at that man he didn't recognize me last time why I should even smile at him we become very upset like that in the same way in a sense we have turned away from Krishna we have forgotten Krishna Krishna Baheer Mukha and that's what Prabhupada is saying here no one can be a greater well wishing friend to any living entity than the Supreme Personality of Godhead he is so kindly disposed towards everyone that in spite of our completely forgetting our relationship with the Supreme Lord he comes himself sometimes personally we have forgotten him we have no interest in him and yet he comes personally Krishna comes personally as Lord Krishna appeared on this earth and sometimes as his devotee as did Lord Chaitanya Mahaprabhu and sometimes he sends his bona fide devotees to reclaim all the fallen souls so sometimes Krishna comes himself in fact it is explained that Krishna came Prabhupada explains this Krishna came and he gave the spiritual knowledge he gave this there were many many occasions where Krishna gave this information but still people of Kali Yuga did not pick it up they did not understand so Krishna had to come once again next time as a devotee to demonstrate what is spiritual life and that happened as Chaitanya

### Srimad Bhagavatam | HG Chanchalapathi Dasa | SB 3.15.34 | 22-01-2016

#### impurity, desire, sense gratification, Krishna consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/LfFYYZdRfuk?t=0
**Verse References:** BG 7.27
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Training in Krishna consciousness will help one be promoted to Vaikuntha."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the Srimad Bhagavatam verse and explains how impurity leads to material existence and how Krishna consciousness can lead to Vaikuntha.

Reading from Srimad Bhagavatam, Canto 3, Chapter 15, Text number 34. He is stated in Bhagavad Gita, 7th Chapter, Verse 27. It is stated that as long as a living entity is pure, he is in complete harmony with the desires of the Supreme Lord, but as soon as he becomes impure, he is in disharmony with the desires of the Lord. By contamination, he is forced to transfer to this material world, where the living entities have three enemies, namely, desire, anger, and lust. These three enemies force the living entities to continue material existence, and when one is free from them, he is eligible to enter the kingdom of God. One should not, therefore, be angry in the absence of an opportunity for sense gratification, and one should not be lusty to acquire more than necessary. In this verse, it is clearly stated that the two doormen should be sent into the material world, where criminals are allowed to reside. Since the basic principles of criminality are sense gratification, anger, and unnecessary lust, persons conducted by these three enemies of the living entity are never promoted to Vaikunthaloka. People should learn Bhagavad Gita and accept the Supreme Personality of Godhead Krishna as the Lord of everything. They should practice satisfying the senses of the Supreme Lord instead of trying to satisfy their own senses. Training in Krishna consciousness will help one be promoted to Vaikuntha. Thus ends the Bhaktivedanta purport.

---
#### Vaikuntha, material world, duality, punishment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/LfFYYZdRfuk?t=231
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Let them go to this material world, where sinful people live with three enemies."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the four Kumaras cursing the doormen to the material world due to their lack of intelligence and their developed vision of duality, emphasizing the presence of three enemies in the material world.

So we are seeing in this part of the Bhagavatam, the four Kumaras trying to enter Vaikuntha and they have been stopped by the two doormen and the sages have become angry and now they are speaking their angry words. And we are seeing in this part of the Bhagavatam, in this particular verse, they sort of take an action, what should be done with these two doormen. Manthadhi means intelligence. Manthadhi means they are not acting very intelligently. Manthadhibhyam, these two. So let us consider, dhimahi, let us consider what should be done. Karatumprakrshtumiha, we should do something to them that will actually eventually benefit them. Lokan itho vrajatam. Let them itaha, from here, in this Vaikuntha, lokan vrajatam, let them go, go away from here. Lokan itho vrajatam. Because, antharabhava drishtya, they have developed a vision of antharabhava. The Lord has one bhava, but they have developed an antharabhava. So, they have developed this kind of antharabhava drishtya. So they should not be here. Lokan itho vrajatam. They should go away from here. Where should they go? Paapi asas, place where paapi asa, paapis, sinful, wicked people, go. And where? Traya ime ripavo asya yatra. Ripaha, ripavaha, is enemies. Traya ime ripavaha, where people in this material world, let them go to this material world, where paapi asaha, sinful people live with three enemies. Let them live there, let them go to the place where living entities live with three enemies. Therefore let us consider, dhimahi, how these two contaminated persons, mandadhibhyam, should be punished. Kartum prakristam. The punishment should be apt. For thus, benefit can eventually be bestowed upon them. Prakrshtam ehah! Since they find duality in the existence in vaikunta life. Antharabhava drishtya. They are contaminated, mandadhibhyam, and should be removed from this place, vrajatam, to the material world, lokan, itho vrajatam, where living entities have three kinds of enemies, traya ime ripavo asya yatra ha.

#### spiritual world, material world, delusion, duality
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/LfFYYZdRfuk?t=579
**Verse References:** BG 7.27
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The living entities, bewildered, come into this material existence."
**Summary:** This segment explains how living entities fall from the spiritual world due to the delusion of duality, as described in the Bhagavad Gita, and how this relates to the concept of antarabhava drishtya in the Bhagavatam.

So this verse, Prabhupada referred to this verse. Even during Srila Prabhupada's time, there would be discussions among devotees. So the living entities fall from the spiritual world. If they're in the spiritual world, the moral Ninevayas must be perfect. But, they fall to the material world. How does that happen? So that is explained in the seventh chapter of the Bhagavad Gita. Ichcha dvesha samuthena dvandva mohena bharata. Sarva bhutani sammoham sargeyanti parantapa. Sarga means creation. Remember that verse in the Bhagavatam we read some time back. There are ten topics in the Bhagavatam. Sarga, visarga, creation, secondary creation and so on. So Krishna is referring to the creation. Sargeyanti. Yanti means to come or to go, to go to the sarga, to go to the material creation. Yanti deva vratan devan. So yanti means to go. Sargeyanti parantapa. So this is actually referring to living entities going to the material creation. So living entities falling to the material world. So why does that happen? Sarva bhutani sammoham sargeyanti parantapa. The living entities, bewildered, come into this material existence. What is that bewilderment? Dvandva mohena bharata. Ichcha dvesha samuthena dvandva mohena bharata. Dvandva moha. Delusion of duality. That's what Krishna is saying here in the Gita. The same thing we are seeing here in the Bhagavatam. Antarbhava drishtya. Dvandva moha. Dvandva means duality. Having a desire and interest different from the Lord. That is called dvandva moha.

---
#### desire, envy, supreme, duality
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/LfFYYZdRfuk?t=755
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Itchha means desire...specifically referring to desire to become the supreme."
**Summary:** This segment elaborates on the concepts of itchha (desire) and dvesha (envy), explaining how they contribute to the delusion of duality and the desire to become supreme, which is contrary to the natural inclination of a living entity to serve the Lord.

Just like here, antarabhava drishtya. So as living entities, we are part and parcel of the Lord. It is natural that we must have an interest like the Lord. Our interest must be perfectly aligned with the interest of the Lord. But when we develop a different desire, a different understanding, a different mentality, antarabhava, then that is called dvandva moha. And then, because of that dvandva moha, itchha dvesha samuthena comes about. Prabhupada has explained very nicely what is this itchha dvesha. Itchha means desire. Here it is specifically referring to desire to become the supreme. In fact, if you see in this material world, everyone in every field tries to become the foremost. Businessmen try to become the richest. Students try to become the smartest. Famous people try to become the most famous. Powerful people try to become the most powerful. So they are actually, this is the desire. The living entity is driven by that desire. Itchha. And dvesha is envy of Krishna. When you hear that Krishna is the supreme, the thought, why should I accept Krishna is the supreme? Why Krishna is the supreme? Why can't I be the supreme?

**Summary:** This segment emphasizes that a living entity should naturally serve the Lord, like a part of the body serves the whole, and introduces the importance of understanding the context when reading verses in the Gita, similar to looking at surrounding words in a dictionary.

These two desires, itchha dvesha samuthena dvandva mohena bharata. Actually, a living entity is not expected to have this kind of a thinking. A living entity is part and parcel of the Lord. Just like every part of the body tries to serve the whole body. That is natural. And when the part develops a different attitude, it is called antarabhava drishtya. Dvandva moha. Actually, it's interesting to see in what context Krishna says this. It's good to, like I remember sometime long ago, someone had told, when you read a, when you see a word in a dictionary, like sometime, of course now you don't see a dictionary, all these days are gone. You google. When you see a word in a dictionary, some, you know, some old people's good advice, you see one word before that and one word after that and learn two words, so you learn three words. In the same way, when you read a verse in the Gita, it's good to see one verse before and one verse after. And you'll get to see the context. And it'll help us develop a certain interesting perspective.

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** This segment explores the context of Krishna's statement in the seventh chapter of the Gita, explaining that Krishna knows the past, present, and future, but people don't know Him because they are born into delusion and overcome by desire and hate.

So let's see why Krishna is saying this in the seventh chapter and we'll come back to the Bhagavatam verse soon after that. Krishna is saying, vedaham samatitani vartamanani caarjuna bhavishyani chabhutani mamtu vedana khaschana. Krishna is saying, I know vedaham aham vedah I know what is in the past, what is in the present, what is in the future. I know the past, I know the present, I know the future. I know all the living entities. But me nobody knows. They don't know me. Actually Krishna is developing this idea how people don't know me. Anyway I won't go into those things. There is a very interesting line of thinking that Krishna is developing and then he has come to this point that me nobody knows. Why they don't know? Krishna is explaining in the next verse why is it that they have come to this state that they don't know me in the material world because all living entities are born into delusion overcome by the dualities of desire and hate. This is the reason why they don't know me because they are born into this material existence with this duality, delusion of duality and they have been overcome by this desire and hate.

#### Vaikuntha, material world, wickedness, devotees
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/LfFYYZdRfuk?t=1223
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Let them go to the place where such evil, wicked people go."
**Summary:** This segment returns to the Bhagavatam verse, explaining that the sages are deciding to send the doormen to the material world, a place for wicked people, and it clarifies the meanings of 'papi' and 'punya,' with 'punya' referring to serving a pure devotee.

So, the sages are deciding that these two living entities, they have developed this, so they should leave this Vaikuntha and they should go to the material world. And what is that material world? Actually, the word Papi, we all generally understand as one who has done Papa, right? So, actually, originally the meaning word, the word Papi means most wicked person. Evil person. Such a person. So, let them go to the place where such evil, wicked people go. And what do they do? They live with three enemies. Let them go to that world. See? That is why here, in this verse also, this contrast is there. Punya also, again, one understanding is pious. Papa means sinful. Actually, Punya means very elevated. Very exalted. Opposite of Papa, means very wicked. Lowest. So, here, Punya-Karmana means activities in relation with Punya. Virtuous. Acharyas describe that Punya-Karmana refers to serving a pure devotee.

#### liberation, devotees, enemies, degradation

If you read the purport also, Prabhupada is talking about that. It is stated in the Srimad Bhagavatam that if one actually wants to be liberated, he must render service to the devotees. See, Punya-Karmana actually refers to serving devotees because they are the most exalted people and should have ended antagatam papam, that sinful desires, sinful activities. One should have ended. So, here, the sages are saying, they should go to Papiyasaha. The three enemies, that is also Prabhupada has referred. That is also here in the Bhagavad Gita. Krishna refers to in the 16th chapter, the three enemies of the living entity. This is the chapter which we are all chanting now. This is the chapter which we are all chanting now. And this is one which, this can destroy. How soul can never be destroyed? What do we mean by that? Here, it is referring to degradation of the soul. It does not mean that soul will be destroyed because soul can never be destroyed. It is already understood. Here, that is why when we are reading the Vedic literatures, we should see it is to be, the meaning should be taken according to the context.

#### hell, lust, anger, greed, Kali Yuga
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/LfFYYZdRfuk?t=1473
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** This segment identifies lust, anger, and greed as the three doors to hell, according to Krishna, and notes that these qualities are often glorified in modern civilization, making Kali Yuga a difficult age for practicing Krishna consciousness.

And what are the three things? The three doors to hell. Lust, anger and greed. And Krishna's recommendation is therefore, Prabhupada says, whenever is used, it is like a conclusive statement. Krishna is making a conclusion after having analyzed something. These three are the gateways to hell and leads to the degradation of the soul. So, therefore, one should give up these three activities, kama, krodha and lobha. And if you see in the material world, in our present civilization, these three qualities are highly glorified. Kama, lust. It is highly promoted. Okay, krodha to some extent it is very, even for good living, comfortable material living, is not very encouraging. So, it is not so much advised. Lobha, greed. There is no such thing. One can be, one could, material civilization, Kali Yuga civilization encourages one to rise to higher and higher and higher. Keep trying, keep struggling, working hard. So, these are the three which are considered to be enemies of the living entities. And, this is a world where. So, just see the things that are, how in the material world, how we look at things. And this is highly glorified. All the newspapers, media, everywhere, cinemas, everything, karma is encouraged. And, there is nothing wrong about it. People think. It is highly promoted. And, on the other hand, Krishna is saying. So, that is why, Kali Yuga is considered a very unfortunate age. For those who are trying to practice Krishna consciousness, maybe we will find many, many kinds of hurdles.

#### purification, spiritual world, Bhagavatam, Prabhupada
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/LfFYYZdRfuk?t=2188
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We have come to this material world developing a Dvandva Moha, Antara Bhava Drishti."
**Summary:** This segment concludes the lecture by summarizing that we have come to the material world due to delusion and a different vision, but we can rectify this, get purified, and return to the spiritual world, as discussed in the purports.

So we have come to this material world developing a Dvandva Moha, Antara Bhava Drishti and now we can rectify that and get purified and go back to the spiritual world all of these things are being discussed here in these purports we will stop here. Grantharaj Srimad Bhagavatam Ki. Srila Prabhupada Ki.

#### devotion, offenses, consequences
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/U6_43FQ2sh8?t=0
**Verse References:** SB 3.15.35
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "For a brāhmaṇa's curse cannot be counteracted by any kind of weapon."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the verse and its translation, highlighting the doormen's fear of the brāhmaṇas' curse and the gravity of offending a devotee.

Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya. Reading from Srimad Bhagavatam, Canto 3, Chapter 15, Text No. 35: teṣām itīritaṁ bhāvava dhārya-gōraṁ tam brahma-dandam anivāraṇaṁ astra-pūgaiḥ sadyo hareranu ca rāvuru bhibhyatāstāt pāda-gṛhāva patatārāṁ tāmatikātare nāṁ teṣām itīritaṁ bhāvava dhārya-gōraṁ tam brahma-dandam anivāraṇaṁ. Translation and purport by His Divine Grace A. C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Srila Prabhupāda.

---
#### devotion, offenses, consequences
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/U6_43FQ2sh8?t=61
**Verse References:** SB 3.15.35
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "There are many kinds of offences, but the greatest offence is to offend a devotee of the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker reads Srila Prabhupada's purport, emphasizing the doormen's awareness of their mistake, the gravity of offending a devotee, and their subsequent fear.

When the doormen of Vaikunthaloka, who were certainly devotees of the Lord, found that they were going to be cursed by the brāhmaṇas, they at once became veiled. They were very much afraid and fell down at the feet of the brāhmaṇas in great anxiety. For a brāhmaṇa's curse cannot be counteracted by any kind of weapon. Although by chance the doormen committed a mistake by checking the brāhmaṇas from entering the gate of Vaikuntha, they were at once aware of the gravity of the curse. There are many kinds of offences, but the greatest offence is to offend a devotee of the Lord. Because the doormen were also devotees of the Lord, they were able to understand their mistake and were terrified when the four kumaras were ready to curse them. Thus ends the Bhaktivedanta purport.

---
#### fear, brahmanas, curses
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/U6_43FQ2sh8?t=138
**Verse References:** SB 3.15.35
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "One may think that I am very powerful, I have so many weapons, you may have any kind of weapons, but by these weapons you cannot, anivaranam, you cannot counteract brahmadandam."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the verse, focusing on the doormen's reaction to the curse, their understanding of its gravity, and their fearful surrender to the brāhmaṇas.

So we are seeing here, the four kumaras, who were checked by the two doormen of Vaikuntha, from entering the Vaikuntha loka, and they were angry and they cursed the two doormen, and here we are seeing the response. We saw them cursing, or considering, in the previous verse, and in this verse we see their reaction of the two doormen. When they heard that they were cursed to go to the material world, they understood what a grave thing, mistake, they have done. avadharya ghoram. tad brahmadandam anivaranam astrapugaihi. And they also realised, they became fearful about brahmadandam, the curse of a brahmana, which is anivaranam, nivaranam, anivaranam, which cannot be counteracted. astrapugaihi. One may think that I am very powerful, I have so many weapons, you may have any kind of weapons, but by these weapons you cannot, anivaranam, you cannot counteract brahmadandam. So realising this, avadharya ghoram, brahmadandam, anivaranam, astrapugaihi, anivaranam, what did they do? sadyaha, immediately, harer anucarao, those devotees, who are followers of hari, uruvibhyatas, full of fear, tadpada grahao apatatam, they fell at the feet of the sages, holding to their feet, apatatam, fell down, atikatha rena, with great anxiety in their heart.

#### material world, spiritual world, curse
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/U6_43FQ2sh8?t=290
**Verse References:** SB 3.15.35
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So when the doorman of Vaikunthaloka, who was certainly devotees of the Lord, found that they were going to be cursed by the brahmanas, brahmadandam, they at once became very much afraid, uruvibhyatas, and fell down, apatatam, at the feet of the brahmanas, pada grahao apatatam, in great anxiety, atikatha rena, for a brahmana's curse, brahmadandam, cannot be counteracted, anivaranam, by any kind of weapon, astrapugai."
**Summary:** The speaker reiterates the verse's meaning, emphasizing the doormen's fear and surrender, and then contrasts the perspective of those in the spiritual world with that of people in the material world, who often see it as a desirable place.

So when the doorman of Vaikunthaloka, who was certainly devotees of the Lord, harer anucarao, found that they were going to be cursed by the brahmanas, brahmadandam, they at once became very much afraid, uruvibhyatas, and fell down, apatatam, at the feet of the brahmanas, pada grahao apatatam, in great anxiety, atikatha rena, for a brahmana's curse, brahmadandam, cannot be counteracted, anivaranam, by any kind of weapon, astrapugai. So, we can see here, and Prabhupada has mentioned, because they were themselves devotees, they understood how grave ghoram it was, to have offended, the sages, and the sages, becoming upset with them, and cursing them. So they became, they were, they became aware, they understood, avadharya ghoram, they understood, what a grave mistake they have committed, and they became fearful, urubhibhyataha, and they fell at the feet of the brahmanas, atikatha rena, with great anxiety. And, so, what was the curse, that the brahmanas, the sages had, pronounced on them, that we saw in the previous verse, loka nitho vrajatam, go to the material world. So that was the curse, about which they became very fearful, they became very anxious. So in other words, when they were told, go to the material world, that was considered to be a curse. So now we should understand.

#### material world, perspective, enlightenment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/U6_43FQ2sh8?t=456
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "This material world is considered to be a punishment posting for those living entities who are not willing to cooperate and be in harmony with the spiritual atmosphere."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of a 'punishment posting' to describe the material world from the perspective of enlightened beings, emphasizing the importance of understanding its true value.

We are all here in this material world, what people of the spiritual world, how they estimate this world. This is a world where people, when they are cursed, they go there. Right? But we think this is a very wonderful place, maybe some things are not alright, we will fix it. In course of time, we will fix it, and we will make it a very good place to live. In, in Indian government language, there is one expression, punishment posting. If some IAS officer, bureaucrat, they don't cooperate with the politicians, or something, then the politicians will transfer them to Andaman, Nicobar Islands. And that's considered to be a punishment posting. So in the same way, this material world is considered to be a punishment posting for those living entities who are not willing to cooperate and be in harmony with the spiritual atmosphere. If we want to estimate, so in this way, we have to look at, how this world is estimated, by enlightened sages, or by residents of the spiritual world, how they rate this material world. We get a certain understanding from this verse.

---
#### knowledge, truth, guru
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/U6_43FQ2sh8?t=575
**Verse References:** BG 4.34
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "We have to get an understanding of this, of this world, from those who are enlightened."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of a goldsmith to illustrate the need to seek knowledge from enlightened sources, quoting Krishna's instruction to approach those who have seen the truth (tatvadarshinah).

Just like, if you want to know the value of something, say some ornament, sometimes we, some people drop some jewellery in the hundi, and then when we get that jewellery, and we want to make an estimate, how much it is, is it real gold, or some imitation jewellery, somebody dropped, now if you want to make an estimation of that, you have to go to a goldsmith, a goldsmith will check out, and then, because he is an expert in that, he will tell you, it's worth, how much money. So in the same way, we have to get an estimation of anything, we have to go to a goldsmith. If you go to a blacksmith, and take a piece of jewellery, you are not going to get very good understanding. So in the same way, we have to get an understanding of this, of this world, from those who are enlightened. That is the spirit, that is the perspective, that we must maintain about this material world. Because, if people who don't, who are not enlightened, their views are not very important. That is why, when Krishna says, tad viddhi pranipatena pariprasnena sevaya, upadekshanti te jnanam jnaninas tatvadarshinah. Krishna is using the word, you must approach, tatvadarshinah, those who have, those who have seen the truth. In other words, those who are experiencing the truth, tatvadarshinah, no use going to an ordinary person, if you want to get spiritual knowledge. In this context, Prabhupada also said, we should read books written by tatvadarshis, those who have actually seen the truth, very enlightened people. From their point of view, we should understand our life, our situation, our world, everything. Then, we will be able to derive benefit from that.

#### knowledge, desire, action
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/U6_43FQ2sh8?t=730
**Verse References:** BG 4.19
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "One is understood to be in full knowledge, whose every act is devoid of desire for sense gratification."
**Summary:** The speaker references Bhagavad Gita 4.19, explaining that true wisdom is characterized by actions devoid of desire for sense gratification, and that such individuals are recognized as wise by learned men.

There is another verse in the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna refers to the similar thing, that how, actually many times, but in this particular place, yasya sarve samarambha, kama sankalpa varjitaha, jnanagni dhadda karmanam, tamahuhu panditam buddhaha. In the chapter 4, text number 19, this is same as the, tamahuhu panditam buddhaha, these people are considered to be, buddhaha, wise, by panditas. Sometimes somebody may consider them to be a wise man. Right? Like all our parents consider us to be very intelligent, my son is so intelligent. We may not be doing very well, but still our parents, out of love, they may say. So that's not important, we have to, whether we are wise, considered to be buddhaha, by panditas, tamahuhu panditam buddhaha. That is important. So Krishna is saying here, yasya sarve samarambha, kama sankalpa varjitaha, jnanagni dhadda karmanam, tamahuhu panditam buddhaha. In all endeavors, sarve samarambha, in all endeavors, kama sankalpa varjitaha, they have given up the desire for enjoyment, kama, self-enjoyment, or sense gratification. Because in every endeavor, a conditioned soul tries to enjoy. What is it that I can get for my sense gratification? Because that's the nature of the conditioned soul, when we come to spiritual life also, in spiritual life also we will be seeing how I can get some sense gratification. So that's the sad part. That is what we have to be watching out. So, yasya sarve samarambha, kama sankalpa varjitaha.

---
#### knowledge, desire, karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/U6_43FQ2sh8?t=871
**Verse References:** BG 4.19
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If in all our endeavors we are looking for sense gratification, that is an indication we are not situated in jnana, we are still in ajnana."
**Summary:** The speaker continues to elaborate on Bhagavad Gita 4.19, explaining that freedom from desire for sense gratification is a result of being situated in knowledge, and conversely, the pursuit of sense gratification indicates a lack of knowledge.

So they have given up varjitaha, kama sankalpa, that determination or that desire for kama, varjitaha, jnanagni dhadda karmanam, such people who have become free from desire for sense gratification, in all their endeavors, such people have actually burned their fruitive reactions in the fire of knowledge. Which means, Prabhupada points out very nicely, kama sankalpa varjitaha is a result of being situated in jnana. So, if we are situated in transcendental knowledge, the effect of that is, in all our endeavors, we will not be looking for sense gratification. Conversely, if in all our endeavors we are looking for sense gratification, that is an indication we are not situated in jnana, we are still in ajnana. So, this is what great learned men consider. One is understood to be in full knowledge, whose every act is devoid of desire for sense gratification. He is said by sages to be a worker, whose fruitive action is burnt up by the fire of perfect knowledge. So, this kind of a perspective, we must have an understanding, we must have an estimation of this material world. What is this material world about? From the perspective of enlightened people.

#### material world, perspective, association
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/U6_43FQ2sh8?t=995
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "If we don't have an estimation from enlightened perspective, we will be very mislead."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of viewing the material world from the perspective of enlightened individuals, warning against being misled by unenlightened views, and introduces the concept of purusha-pashuhu.

And they consider this place to be a place, a most undesirable place, when compared to the spiritual world. If we don't have an estimation from enlightened perspective, we will be very mislead. Bhagavatam says, there is another verse in the Bhagavatam, shva-vid-varaha-ustra-karai samsthuta-purusha-pashuhu. Samsthutaha means to be praised by another person. samsthuta-purusha-pashuhu. In the material world, sometimes we come across one person praising another person. If one person is praising another person, who is that person who is praising? That is important. Otherwise, the example here given is shva-vid-varaha-ustra-karai shva-vid-varaha-ustra-karai shva is dog, vid-varaha is a hog, pig, and ustra is a camel, karaha is a donkey. If these kind of animals, samsthutaha, they are glorifying, oh, he is such a great man. So these kind of animals will glorify another animal only. Because that is their perspective, after all.

---
#### material world, perspective, detachment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/U6_43FQ2sh8?t=1129
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The person who is praising must have requisite qualifications."
**Summary:** The speaker continues the discussion on purusha-pashuhu, emphasizing that the value of praise depends on the qualifications of the person offering it, and reiterates the Bhagavatam's perspective that the material world is a terrible place.

So, samsthutaha-purusha-pashuhu. Another person, they are glorifying, he is also like another, he is an animal only. So, the person who is praising must have requisite qualifications. So, if he is qualified, then his estimation, oh, he is praising somebody, he is praising something that has value. So, we see from the perspective that is presented here in the Bhagavatam about the material world, is that this is actually a terrible place. Constantly the living entities are absorbed in sense gratification. They are completely in harmony in disharmony with their constitutional position. They are completely in disharmony with the Lord. Although they are part and parcel of the Lord, they are not in harmony. Just like any part of our body, if it is not in harmony with the rest of the body, that part of the body, that organ of the body will become, is considered to be a diseased part. It will be a source of pain for us. So, all the parts of the body are meant to cooperate for the whole body. That's the constitution, that is the makeup, that is the way this body is constituted. If any part of the body is not in harmony with the whole body's well-being, then it will become a problem. So, just see what is our position in this material world. All the living entities who are not willing to cooperate with the Supreme Lord. And ours is a society full of people who are in this kind of a mentality. We are all, this material world is meant for people who are unwilling to cooperate. This is the understanding. And so when the two doormen heard that these sages are cursing us to go there. Oh! Uruvibhyataha! Became very, very fearful. Aniva... Avadharya ghoram! Oh! What a mistake! What a ghoram! What a big thing, mistake we have done. They started realizing that. So, this is how, this is the conception we must always maintain. About this material world.

---
#### detachment, Krishna consciousness, calamity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/U6_43FQ2sh8?t=1274
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "There is nothing but great calamity at this moment."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that maintaining a detached perspective on the material world helps one focus on Krishna consciousness, and shares a quote from Srila Prabhupada describing the modern world as a place of great calamity.

Then we will not become attached. We will not become attached to any particular circumstance, particular situation. We can remain detached. And it will help us to be focused on Krishna. Krishna Consciousness. Otherwise there is every chance that we will become very attached to this material world. Not knowing, actually this is not a very desirable place. Once in 1974, Srila Prabhupada wrote a letter. He had one disciple who had some law background and educated person. And then Prabhupada was encouraging him to... I mean he was also in a very senior position. At that time he was a GBC. And Prabhupada was saying, Prabhupada was encouraging him. Even in an earlier occasion, Prabhupada had encouraged him after he became a devotee, he should try to participate even in American politics. And... And Prabhupada had a wish that if devotees can become leaders in the society and if the society can be changed in that way, then it can be great. It is a great benefit. So in 1974, Prabhupada writes in a letter, At the present moment, there is nothing but great calamity. But by injecting Krishna consciousness into the masses, we can change the situation greatly. So Prabhupada saw our material world, modern world situation to be, there is nothing but great calamity. In Kali Yuga, material world itself is a very fearful place. And in the material world, there are phases of time and Kali Yuga is considered the worst of all the phases of time, of all the Yugas. And we are here in this material world.

#### devotee protection, divine intervention
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JGJcFBuz4gk?t=0

In Bhagavad Gita, the Lord declares that his devotees cannot be vanquished at any time. The Lord could understand that the quarrel between the doorman and the sages was taking a different turn. And therefore, he instantly came out of his place and went to the spot to stop further aggravation so that his devotees, the doorman, might not be vanquished for good.

#### Srimad Bhagavatam, divine appearance, offense
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JGJcFBuz4gk?t=42
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Lord did not want further aggravation and further more something more should not happen to his devotees, the doormen."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the Srimad Bhagavatam verse describing the Lord's arrival with Lakshmi to address the offense committed by the doormen against the sages.

Thus ends the Bhaktivedanta purport. We are reading in the Srimad Bhagavatam, in this part, about the two doormen stopping the sages. And the sages becoming angry and we just saw in the previous verses, they cursed that they should go down to the earthly planet. And we also saw that the two doormen very graciously accepted the curse that was delivered to them. And in today's verse, we are seeing how the Lord responded when he was aware that this was going on at the seventh gate of his Vaikuntha. The Lord did not want further aggravation and further more something more should not happen to his devotees, the doormen. So, the Lord arrived there.

#### verse analysis, lotus feet, devotee attraction
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JGJcFBuz4gk?t=180
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Walking on those lotus feet, which is sought after by the great transcendentalists and the great sages, the Lord arrived there."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the verse, focusing on the Lord's awareness of the offense, his arrival with Lakshmi, and the significance of his lotus feet being sought after by elevated transcendentalists.

And so here, the svanam sadatikramam vibhudhya. Svanam, his own servants, sadatikramam, they had, atikramam, they had committed an offense. They had gone beyond what they should do. Atikramam. Towards whom? Sadatikramam. Towards the great sages, the four sages. Sadatikramam, svanam sadatikramam vibhudhya. The Lord became aware of that. And, immediately, the Lord tasmin yaya. And, tasmin yaya, how did he go there? Charanau chalayan yaya. With his own feet, he arrived there. Sahashrihi, along with Laxmidevi. Now, what is the Lord's lotus feet like? There are, there is an interesting adjective here. Paramahamsa mahamuninam anmeshaniya charanau. Anmeshana, means to search out or to seek after that lotus feet. So, the lotus feet which are sought after by whom? Mahamuninam, Paramahamsa mahamuninam. Paramahamsa means the most elevated transcendentalists. And, mahamuninam, and very very great sages, transcendentalists, great sages, they seek out that lotus feet. Walking on those lotus feet, which is sought after by the great transcendentalists and the great sages, the Lord arrived there.

---
#### verse analysis, divine qualities, devotee delight
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JGJcFBuz4gk?t=304
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Lord who is the delight of the righteous, who is Aravindanabha, and whose lotus feet are sought after by Paramahamsa Mahamuninam Anveshaniya Charanah Chalayan Sahashrihi along with Lakshmi Devi, he arrived there."
**Summary:** The speaker continues explaining the verse, highlighting the Lord's qualities as Aravindanabha and Arya Hridyaha, emphasizing his delight to the righteous.

And, another expression that is used here is Arya Hridyaha. The Lord who is Arya Hridyaha, the Lord who is Aravindanabha, Bhagavan Aravindanabha, one who has a lotus in his navel, referring to the Garbhakshayi Vishnu, and from the lotus sprouts, from his navel sprouts the lotus on which Lord Brahma is situated and all the lokas are in the stem of the lotus, so he is known as Aravindanabha. And here he is also described as Arya Hridyaha. Arya, righteous, Prabhupada has used that word, Hridyaha, the delight of one who is attracted to. So, Arya Hridyaha, the Lord who is the delight of the righteous, who is Aravindanabha, and whose lotus feet are sought after by Paramahamsa Mahamuninam Anveshaniya Charanah Chalayan Sahashrihi along with Lakshmi Devi, he arrived there.

#### devotee concern, civilization, progressive values
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JGJcFBuz4gk?t=434
**Verse References:** BG 2.1
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Human life is meant for progressing in values in understanding and one who is conscious of this that human life we can achieve something which is very special which is not possible in other living entities."
**Summary:** The speaker transitions to discussing the Lord's concern for his devotees and introduces the concept of 'Arya' as a civilized person who understands the progressive values of life, contrasting it with modern superficial definitions of civilization.

So, very beautiful verse. Describing how the Lord so concerned about his devotees and what was happening in the Vaikuntha and he personally arrived there immediately along with Lakshmi Devi. So, there are some very interesting points here from the verse. The Lord here is described as Arya Hridyaha the delight of the righteous. This word Arya Prabhupada also describes in other places. Arya refers to a civilized person. Aryan refers to civilized. In the Bhagavad Gita when this word comes up in the second chapter Prabhupada explains the word Arya is one who knows the progressive values of life. So, human life is meant for progressing in values in understanding and one who is conscious of this that human life we can achieve something which is very special which is not possible in other living entities. So, human life and human consciousness is very special and so we must attain that higher understanding. One who is conscious of this they are called Aryans one who is conscious of the progressive values of life.

#### civilization, consciousness, taste
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JGJcFBuz4gk?t=548
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "A person has to be judged by his tastes. What are his tastes? What is he attracted to? What is he what delights him?"
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the Vedic understanding of a civilized person with the modern materialistic view, emphasizing that true civilization is defined by refined consciousness and elevated tastes.

Unlike in today's world you see who is called a civilized man? A man who is wearing a nice suit and a tie and a boot and going in a big car and living in a big house and these kind of things he is considered to be a very civilized man. That is what is thought of. But actually in the Vedic culture it is not just your attire. So, somebody may be in those kind of a very polished attire but in his consciousness may have some other kind of a very lowly understanding. So, that is not important. What is important is Arya Hridyaha and so the yardstick the test that is used here what Srila Prabhupada is setting forth is Arya Hridyaha a person has to be judged by his tastes. What are his tastes? What is he attracted to? What is he what delights him? You see. So, Arya Hridyaha means those who are very civilized who know the progressive values of life who have a refined consciousness who have actually refined their consciousness and how do we know they have refined their consciousness? Because their tastes have changed. That is the test. What is it that delights them? What is it that they are attracted to? What is it that they go after? So, that is an indication of the level of consciousness of the person.

#### consciousness, attraction, transcendental Lord
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JGJcFBuz4gk?t=674
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "They are not some lowly material objects of this world but the highest level of consciousness they are attracted to the transcendental Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the concept of 'Arya', explaining that it refers to someone whose consciousness is evolved, leading them to be attracted to the transcendental Lord rather than material objects.

So, Arya refers not simply external dressing and those kind of things but somebody who is evolved in his consciousness and hence his likes his what engages him what attracts him those objects have changed and they are not some lowly material objects of this world but the highest level of consciousness they are attracted to the transcendental Lord. And so, the Lord is known as Arya Hridyaha. In that sense Arya Hridyaha means those Lord who is the delight of the most civilized people. So, the Lord is the delight of the most civilized people. Most enlightened people. That is why Krishna says itimatva bhajante maam budha bhavasamanvitah itimatva bhajante maam budha bhavasamanvitah. They are enlightened Buddha and they are bhavasamanvitah their heart is filled with bhava for the Lord because they are enlightened and they know who is the Lord what is Krishna's position He is the source of everything. So, this is the position of the Lord.

**Summary:** The speaker analyzes a verse from the Bhagavad Gita, explaining that while abstaining from sense objects may suppress desires, only transcendental experience can eliminate the taste for sense enjoyment.

It's a very interesting verse the verse talks about two things niraharasya dehinam niraharasya niraharasya one meaning of the word nirahara means not eating food ahara nirahara so not eating food but here niraharasya refers to negative restricting principles negative principles don't do this don't do this vishaya vinivartante niraharasya dehinam rasavarjam rasopyasya here the niraharasya dehinam one who is abstaining following all of these negative restrictions niraharasya dehinam dehinam a person who has an embodied person a living entity niraharasya dehinam vishaya vinivartante rasavarjam vishaya vinivartante vishaya vinivartante means he is abstaining from the vishayas that's what the meaning of niraharasya he is not supplying the vishaya vishaya refers to indriyavishaya so he is abstaining from vishaya vishaya vinivartante niraharasya dehinam rasavarjam only vishaya vinivartante rasavarjam vishaya meaning rasa other than rasa rasavarjam means apart from the sense of enjoyment in other words the sense of enjoyment is still there so he may be niraharasya dehinam vishaya vinivartante but rasavarjam vishaya vinivartante he is still attracted to that rasa for enjoying the vishaya is there other than that other than that he is abstaining from the vishaya but rasopyasya paramdrishtva what is that paramdrishtva nivartante rasopyasya paramdrishtva nivartante but when you do paramdrishtva when you have the transcendental experience raso api nivartante even the desire even the sense of enjoyment goes away so it is not that he is only abstaining strictly on the basis of certain principles that no I must not one he has one who has developed paramdrishtva rasopyasya paramdrishtva nivartante rasavarjam rasopyasya paramdrishtva nivartante.

### Srimad Bhagavatam | HG Chanchalapathi Dasa | SB 3.15.38 | 26-02-2016
**Speaker:** HG Chanchalapathi Dasa

#### seeing the Lord, devotional service, maturity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4ooHAtaPjas?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "For ordinary persons the Lord is not visible, however when one can understand the significance of his holy name and one engages himself in the devotional service of the Lord, beginning with the tongue, by chanting and tasting Prasad, then gradually the Lord reveals himself."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the verse and explains how the Lord, initially unseen by ordinary eyes, becomes visible to pure devotees through devotional service and maturity in their love for God.

In this verse we find the word Achakshat Akshavishayam. The Supreme Lord cannot be seen by ordinary eyes, but he now became visible to the eyesight of the Kumaras. Another significant word is Samadhi Bhagyam. Meditators who are very fortunate can see the Vishnu form of the Lord within their hearts by following the yogic process, but to see him face to face is a different matter. This is only possible for pure devotees. The Kumaras therefore upon seeing the Lord coming forward with his associates, who were holding an umbrella and a chamara fan, were struck with wonder and they were seeing the Lord face to face. It is said in the Brahma Samhita that devotees being elevated in love of God always see Shama Sundara, the Supreme Personality of Godhead within their hearts, but when they are mature, the same God is visible before them face to face. For ordinary persons the Lord is not visible, however when one can understand the significance of his holy name and one engages himself in the devotional service of the Lord, beginning with the tongue, by chanting and tasting Prasad, then gradually the Lord reveals himself. Thus the devotee constantly sees the Lord within his heart, and in a more mature stage, one can see the same Lord directly as we can see everything else. Thus ends the Bhaktivedanta Parbat.

---
#### appearance of the Lord, paraphernalia, description
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4ooHAtaPjas?t=120
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** description
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "They were carrying appropriate paraphernalia when the Lord appeared."
**Summary:** This segment describes the appearance of the Lord with his associates, highlighting the paraphernalia they carried, such as an umbrella decorated with pearls and chamara fans creating a pleasant breeze.

So we are seeing in this part of the Srimad Bhagavatam, the sages were upset with the two doorkeepers and they cursed them. Just then the Lord appeared along with his consort Lakshmi Devi. We saw that in the last verse and in this verse further, the Lord appeared with his associates and all of them carried many paraphernalia. Swapumbhi, his own personal associates, Pratihruta Aupayikam. They were carrying appropriate paraphernalia, Agatam, when the Lord appeared. And they, the sages, Achakam, Achakshata, the sages now saw Vishnu. Achakshata, Akshavishayam. Now he had become an object of their sight. Achakshat, Akshavishayam, Achakshata, Akshavishayam. They saw the Lord who has now become a subject matter of their sight. Akshavishayam, Svasamadhi Bhagyam, Svasamadhi Bhagyam. Formerly they had seen the Lord, but was in samadhi. Svasamadhi Bhagyam, so in their deep meditation and in their meditation they had seen Samadhi Bhagyam, Svasamadhi Bhagyam. And how did he appear now? The associates were offering chamar, Vya janayaha, Vya janayo, and there was a cool pleasant breeze coming from the chamar. So chamar should be done nicely. So there was a cool pleasant breeze, Shiva vayu, Vayu, Shiva vayu, very pleasant breeze and the two, they were offering them on either side and they looked like Hamsashriyo, like two white swans on both sides as the Lord was coming.

#### majesty, opulence, associates

**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** This segment summarizes the description of the Lord's appearance, emphasizing the majestic opulence and regality that surround Him, noting that He always appears with His consort and associates.

So basically, this is a description of the grand appearance of the Lord. This should give us a sense about the Lord's majestic opulence, the regality that surrounds Him, and the opulences, and how He appears. He did not appear alone. When the Lord appears, He appears with His consort most of the time, and with His associates, Swashrihi in the previous verse, Sahashrihi, along with Lakshmi, and here, we are seeing Swapumbhihi, along with His associates, all of them carrying different paraphernalia, umbrella, and offering chamar, and in that way, the Lord appears. So this is to give us an idea, this is to help us understand the Lord's position, and how the Lord appears in Vaikuntha.

#### analogy, material power, entourage
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4ooHAtaPjas?t=534
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "When we see in this material world, a big person coming, it's marked with his entourage, and all of these kind of manifestations of his power and position. And that's natural."
**Summary:** This segment uses the analogy of the President of India's visits to temples to illustrate how even in the material world, powerful figures are accompanied by entourages and displays of power, suggesting that the Lord's appearance with associates is natural.

Some years ago, we had a president of India visiting our temple here, and we had gone and invited him in Delhi, and then he agreed to come, and when he came, there were a lot of preparations made, and security personnel, protocol officers, they would advise us how to, what he should do, etc. And finally when he came, he didn't come alone. There was a motorcade of 24 cars all over on Hare Krishna hill. By the time the long motorcade came, and he got off, and there were a few cars before, and a few cars behind him, and it was, and all of them stood, the next two hours or so that he was there, all these cars were parked on the road, and all big cars, and big officials, like that. And when the president of India comes, it is a protocol that the chief minister, or the chief minister himself must come, the governor must come from the state. So all of them were there. So it's not a small thing when the president comes. And quite recently we had president of India coming to our temple in Vrindavan, which is far away. And there when he came, three helicopters landed. And so we were all waiting there and receiving, to receive him. First the governor's helicopter came from Lucknow. So that was another one. And then these three Indian Air Force helicopters, massive helicopters. And they all landed there. So, when we see in this material world, a big person coming, it's marked with his entourage, and all of these kind of manifestations of his power and position. And that's natural.

#### anthropomorphism, human ideas, divinity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4ooHAtaPjas?t=690
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Some people think and say that our human ideas, we are imposing on God. So this is called anthropomorphic concepts."
**Summary:** This segment recounts an interaction with a doctor who suggested that concepts of pleasing Lakshmi to please Narayana are anthropomorphic, meaning human ideas imposed on God, a view the speaker intends to refute.

And so here we see, when the Lord is appearing, naturally, and he also has his associates and entourage, all of them appear, and it's a grand appearance. Long ago, I had, I remember one interaction with a doctor. I had fallen sick, not very sick, some extent sick. And I had gone to a doctor. And this doctor was a very friendly, very, very, he talks a lot, with, he engages in a conversation with the patients. That's his, one of his techniques and puts them in comfort and talks to them nicely. So he started talking to me, and he saw me because I was a devotee. Then he started asking, I can't remember the exact subject that we discussed. Something I mentioned about Krishna, Radharani, Lakshmi, Vishnu, and something like that. When you should please Lakshmi, then Narayana will be pleased. You please Radharani, Krishna will be, something like that we were talking. And then he heard all that and he said, very interesting. All of these are anthropomorphic concepts. Meaning, he was saying that, anthropomorphic means having human attributes. Like here we see, we have experience, if you want to get the check from a big man, our devotees often do. They go and please his wife. And when the wife is pleased, there is a good possibility you will get the check. So these kind of things are human experiences. And so some people think and say that our human ideas, we are imposing on God. So this is called anthropomorphic concepts.

#### mythology, human creation, acharya's view
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4ooHAtaPjas?t=828
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "These are all ideas that have come from human society, now we are mapping on to the spiritual domain. And this is a kind of a human creation."
**Summary:** This segment elaborates on the idea that some view religious concepts like Vishnu's four arms as mythology, human creations imposed on divinity, a perspective the speaker contrasts with the views of Acharyas.

So this is what he was trying to say. And of course, I don't remember what I said, maybe I was not well enough to answer this kind of a question. So anyway, so people think that these are all ideas that have come from human society, now we are mapping on to the spiritual domain. And this is a kind of a human creation. Basically, it's an idea that it's a human creation. So that's how some people think. And the Mayavadis also have a similar idea. And they say that these are all just stories. Vishnu, Vishnu having four arms. Okay, here we have a human experience of two arms. And for the, because he is God after all, he should have something more than us, and let's give him four hands. You know, these kind of ideas. So these are all just mythology. They dismiss it like that. And they think that you should see some ideas, some good message behind that that you must appreciate. So how do Acharyas explain this? If you study, none of the Acharyas, including Mayavadi Acharyas, will say like this. Especially the Vaishnava Acharyas. They never derided these as mythological stories. A human idea carried forward to a spiritual realm. This is not the kind of an expression. No Acharya has ever said these things. Even Shankaracharya, he said, Narayano Paro Vyaktad. He said, yes, Narayana's existence, Para, is transcendental. So none of the Acharyas have said these things. And Srila Prabhupada, also following this, the line of the Acharyas, Srila Prabhupada, none of the, our Acharyas in our Sampradaya, none of them have said that these are all mythological, imaginary, some human idea imposed on divinity. This is not the understanding.

#### transcendental, sense perception, limitations
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4ooHAtaPjas?t=976
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Vishnu or Krishna, he is transcendental. He is beyond material sense perception. He is not the subject matter of the mundane senses."
**Summary:** This segment explains Srila Prabhupada's perspective, emphasizing that Krishna or Vishnu is transcendental, beyond material sense perception and the limitations of human senses, mind, and intellect.

How does Srila Prabhupada explain this? Srila Prabhupada, like here it is explained in this verse. He is, Desa, Achakshata, Akshavishayam. Vishnu is not Akshavishayam. He is not a subject matter of these eyes. He is not a Indriyavishaya. But here, Achakshata, Akshavishayam, he, they began to see him. So, Vishnu's position, Krishna's position is, he is not Akshavishaya, he is not a Indriyavishaya. He is not seen, he cannot be accessed by our senses. This is again and again explained in the Vedic literature, Athashree Krishnanamadhi, Nabhavet Grahya Mindriyaihi. Indriyagrahya Nabhavet. Senses cannot access him. He is beyond the senses. He is beyond sense perception. This is what the Vedic literatures have often explained about Krishna's position. And Srila Prabhupada also says that, yes, Vishnu or Krishna, he is transcendental. He is beyond material sense perception. He is not the subject matter of the of the mundane senses. And he is beyond the range of human sense and mental and intellectual conceptions. This is the understanding.

#### inaccessibility, mundane senses, analogy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4ooHAtaPjas?t=1086
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Krishna's reality, the reality of Krishna or Vishnu, is inaccessible to our mundane senses."
**Summary:** This segment uses the analogy of a mobile phone unable to make international calls to illustrate that our mundane senses have limitations and cannot access the higher reality of Krishna or Vishnu.

This understanding is presented throughout in the Vedic literatures. And Srila Prabhupada also says the same thing. He is, Krishna's reality, the reality of Krishna or Vishnu, is inaccessible to our mundane senses. Our mundane senses can only access a certain amount of reality. Not even all of the material reality, right? Our senses cannot understand. Our eyes cannot see the mind and intelligence. But we know people have mind intelligence, and they are working, and it is working. We can see the effects of the mind and intelligence, although we cannot see the mind and intelligence. So our senses have limitation. Even our mind and intelligence has limitation. What they can access in this reality as material reality. So there is a certain reality beyond what is accessible to the mind, senses and intelligence. And Krishna or Vishnu belongs to that higher reality. So our senses don't have the powers to access that realm of reality. That is the important point. Just like suppose you have a mobile phone. And your mobile phone, you don't have international, you only have facility to make local calls, India calls. You can't make any call outside of India. And somebody comes and tells you, I have a friend in America, and this is the number of my friend. And you use your phone and try to call that number. It's an American number. Call will not go through. You try many times, it will not go through. Now somebody may say, no, no, you charge properly, then it may go through. You can have charge many times, still it will not go through. Somebody may say, no, no, you change your phone version, get a upgrade, a better phone. You can get a better phone, still you try, it will not go through. Go to a place where there is very good signal, and try. You can do anything you want, anything you want, you will not get the call. Then can you say, see, I have tried so much. I don't think you have a friend in America. Or even better, I don't think there is something called America. Because if there is, I must be able to make a phone call and talk. No, it is not so. Just because our phone, our instruments cannot reach out, we cannot say such a reality does not exist.

#### spiritual reality, access, process
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4ooHAtaPjas?t=1270
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This spiritual reality is beyond the access, beyond the powers of our senses."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes that spiritual reality is beyond the reach of our senses, but a process exists to perceive it, as described in Vedic literature and today's verse, involving samadhi and trance.

This is the principle that is used by the Acharyas, by Srila Prabhupada. Srila Prabhupada gave very contemporary articulation of this idea, that this reality, this spiritual reality is beyond the access, beyond the powers of our senses. This is the perception, this is the perspective that is presented in the Vedic literature. Now, if this is the reality, how do we ever know that reality? Is there a way to know that reality? Yes. Although the senses cannot perceive, atha shri krishna naam aadhi na bhaved grahyam indriyaihi sevon mukho hi jihvadau svayam eva sphurathi adaha. This is the way, this is the method that is given in the Vedic literatures, how to perceive the transcendental reality. And here in this verse, in today's verse, also it is described that he is, he became akshavishayam, although he is not akshavishayam, he is adhokshaja. Adhokshaja means not accessible to the senses. Because, and he was samadhi bhagyam, sva-samadhi bhagyam. Lord is seen in samadhi, in trance. This is the idea. The senses ordinarily cannot perceive, but there is a process. If we follow that process, then we will be able to perceive the Lord in samadhi.
