### The Theory of Everything - The Vedanta Perspective (Part 1)
**Speaker:** HG Chanchalapathi Dasa

#### Theory of Everything, Grand Unification Theory, Stephen Hawking
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/c6T8cIJthGg?t=115
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "it should be something that it should be very simple and elegant and understandable by everyone not just it is some very complex mathematical things and only a few scientists can read and understand that no, he expected that it is something understandable by everyone not just few scientists"
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the concept of the 'Theory of Everything' and its relation to the Grand Unification Theory, mentioning Stephen Hawking's popularization of the idea.

what is this theory of everything? Did I make up this name or something like that? No, it's actually there is something serious about it. About two years ago, if you had asked somebody, a young person, what is the theory of everything, से सेलियों के बाद रहे हैं। उम्मीद करें कि यह आज एक भारतिल फॉलाफिकल फिल्म है स्तीफेन हार्किंग का सोचता था। और उसको आगे बाद भी बढ़कर हुआ है। इस वर्ष का साथ स्टीफेन हार्किंग आफ्ट यॉराइस्ड है। world this name theory of everything, he is not the one who coined it. It was there. I have learnt that it was there already. The theory of everything is a is a mathematical scientific framework which scientists have been working, if I remember right, maybe last 80 years. I remember as a college student attending a lecture in Bangalore City and those days it was known as the grand unification theory. Basically it's like this: scientists have figured out that there are four fundamental forces of nature. There is the gravitational force, the electromagnetic force, and two of them called the nuclear force. One is called a strong nuclear force and other is a weak one nuclear force. Basically these four forces have come into play and led to the world as we see it today, whether it's the stars, the galaxies, and the black hole, or whether it's elements and all the things that we see in close proximity on our planet. And according to scientists, even the evolution of life has all happened because of these four forces. And now what scientists have been trying to do is, is there a way that these four distinct forces can further be united to come to one which has led to the four forces which has led to the world and the universe? This is known as the grand unification theory and scientists have been working on this all over the world. And at some point this theory, if it can explain everything, because that's one force or whatever that is which led to these four forces and these four forces has led to the entire cosmos that we see including us. And we are talking about all of these things, it is something that should be able to explain everything. And that was the grand unification theory and that became more popular by Stephen Hawking. Stephen Hawking was not only a brilliant scientist, he was also a great popularizer of science and so that's how this his biographical film came to

#### Stephen Hawking, Philosophy, Science
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/c6T8cIJthGg?t=326
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy

all over the world. It's interesting to reflect on what Prabhupada is saying here. Now you see this Socratic method of reasoning, this is some Socrates was the Greek philosopher hundreds of years ago and at that time Socrates developed this kind of a reasoning and this is a kind of a reasoning used to stimulate critical and analytical thinking and it is so powerful, basic fundamental and universal. In fact the Socratic method of reasoning is something that modern world has used in every branch of knowledge it is very very common to use and bring critical thinking about science or even about modern day matters medicine politics, business how you analyze, you ask some very basic questions to find out what's going wrong, what is happening so use this in business you use this in justice systems in the country practically everywhere this Socratic method of questioning and reasoning to bring about critical thinking, analytical thinking about an issue. So this Socratic method should not be restrained should not be confined only to Athens or Greece it should go everywhere because it is so powerful, so basic so fundamental and universal. So Prabhupada is giving that example and saying that in the Vedanta there is a certain philosophical perspective there is a certain way of looking at the world at the universe, at oneself at our lives and that is so basic, so fundamental so powerful and it is universal it cannot be confined only to a few religious people in India or a few Brahmins or a few Hindus it is something that should benefit everyone and I imagine Socratic method of thinking and reasoning had remained only in Greece and had not gone to the rest of the world and the world had not adopted it used it and we have the world as we have today is because at the fundamental level there is a certain analytical questioning and thinking which was introduced by Socratic method of questions and that is known as the Socratic method of thinking, reasoning. **Summary:** The speaker explains the meaning of 'Veda' as knowledge and how Vyasadeva compiled and structured Vedic knowledge, culminating in Vedanta as the ultimate or end of all knowledge.

and he was writing a letter to the Prime Minister of the country at that time soliciting some help from the Prime Minister and now let's go to this subject, what is the Vedanta message and you would have all come across this in Prabhupada's books the word Veda it means to know or to or knowledge it comes from the root word in Sanskrit with with actually and with means to know or and the word Veda means knowledge and so actually when Vyasadeva was compiling the Vedas he was not thinking that I am compiling a certain set of beliefs of the Hindus he was compiling the knowledge that was prevalent and was being transmitted from generation to generation in an oral tradition and that knowledge at the dawn of Kali Yuga he was compiling that into a written form and that came to be known as Veda. Now what Vyasadeva did was he was not only compiling the knowledge and he was creating a hierarchical structure for knowledge because every knowledge has basic knowledge, a more advanced knowledge, a most advanced and the most advanced knowledge, just like say for instance you take mathematics that is basic mathematics we all studied in school and then high school mathematics then we studied a little more mathematics in college and then if you do your masters and your PhD in mathematics and it will be very very advanced mathematics. So in knowledge there is a hierarchical structure as we begin to comprehend and appreciate and able to understand more and more of it you can go to higher and higher levels of learning and that's very true with the philosophy philosophical knowledge the very basic knowledge and so he created hierarchical stages of knowledge and he Vyasadeva wrote the most advanced part of the Vedic knowledge in the form of Vedanta. Anta means the end or the ultimate and so Vedanta actually means the ultimate or the end of all knowledge and that is about Vedanta and it was that knowledge of Vedanta which Srila Prabhupada found that to be very fundamental universal powerful basic and this should benefit everyone in the world and that was the driving force and that was the instruction he had received from his spiritual master and his spiritual master and so on so this is the Vedanta knowledge and which is considered even among the Vedic knowledge the ultimate or the end of all knowledge

**Summary:** The speaker describes Vedanta as the last word in Vedic wisdom, summarized by Krishna to Arjuna in the Bhagavad Gita.

now what is the Vedanta knowledge about? Prabhupada writes in one of the purports Vedanta is the last word in Vedic wisdom. So you can imagine that there is a certain Vedic wisdom it's put in the form of the Vedas, the Puranas, the Upanishads and all of that and the last word in that is the Vedanta very important. And in fact that Vedanta knowledge was summarized by Krishna to Arjuna in the Bhagavad Gita that's why the Bhagavad Gita becomes extremely important because in a very succinct manner the Lord himself spoke that Vedanta, the last word of Vedic wisdom in the form of the Bhagavad Gita and and later on we hear from Srila Prabhupada and from Chaitanya Mahaprabhu's explanation and the Vedanta that Vyasa Deva wrote was in the form of Sutras we will talk about that a little while later also that's how it is known as the Vedanta Sutras or also known as Brahma Sutra. So Vedanta Sutras are in the form of Sutras. Sutras are very very short statements which are not always following grammatical rules so it is agrammatical it's very concise it is full of meaning because those days there were no hard disks and search engines and all of those kind of things and so people had to memorize they could easily commit to memory these Sutras and you can walk around anywhere and then pull out a Sutra from your memory and then open it up expand on it. So all of these things were possible and that's how Sutra is another kind of a composition in Sanskrit literature you not only have prose you not only have poetry you have drama you have place there is another kind of a literary structure in Sanskrit known as the Sutras and these are very very concise and it is compressed with meaning it is meant for insiders it's not necessarily outsiders may not understand they require commentaries and explanations of that. So this is the Vedanta Sutras and Vyasadeva compiled Vedanta the last word of Vedic wisdom in the form of Sutras

and the other one is the conception of absolute truth indicates a samambonam or the ultimate source of all energies we look into all of these things in little greater detail and now let's take a look at the nomenclature of absolute truth in Vedanta philosophy this word absolute truth is obviously a very English word and Prabhupada used this capital A capital T absolute truth even Bhakti Siddhanta Saraswati Thakur had used that word and what are the equivalents for us to get a sense of what this word means Tattva or Paratattva Tattva means the truth and Paratattva, Tattva means actually the ultimate truth or the absolute truth another word another word that is used in the Vedic literature is Vastu or Paravastu Vastu, in the Srimad Bhagavatam it says Vastu means the substance even Western philosophers have used this word Vastu to mean substance what is the substance of this world compared to, contrasted with shadow so substance and shadow are two words used even among Western philosophers so Vastu or Paravastu and Tattva Tattva Vadanti Tattva Vidas Tattvam Tattva Darshivihi Tattva Jnanaha so Tattva is another word used for indicating absolute truth Vastu or Paravastu another word is Satya or Paramsatya Satyam Paramdhimahi Srimad Bhagavatam also talks about, it is taught so these words are used in Vedic literatures and there is one more word Brahman or Parabrahman so these are all the words commonly used in the Vedic literature to indicate the absolute truth or when these words were being used in the Vedic literature to convey that in English language Prabhupada used the word absolute truth

now let's look at the next word in the nomenclature of God in Vedanta philosophy God, the common word is Ishwara and as Prabhupada pointed out Ishwara means indicates a controller actually the word Ishwara is controller meaning like say for instance I am a controller of my body to some extent I can control certain things of the body certain things, if a virus attacks even I go crazy I can't control everything I can control everything I can control some things about this body so we are everyone and I can be, I am a controller in my family and I am a controller in my organization I am a controller in my country in my state, in my country so you can see that Ishwara indicates a controlling and there is a hierarchy of controllers small to big, big, big and so on so that's why the gradations of controllers even in this world and there are some very very powerful controllers and they are known as Devatas and they are also Ishwara, a controller so there is a, we may talk about these controllers or Devatas or even human beings or big personalities in this material world in human society but when we are talking about absolute truth we are not talking about some some controller of a certain sort of a certain level but we are talking about something Samambonam or the source of everything we will be coming to that a little more later let's hold on to this topic what is absolute truth? now reading Srila Prabhupada's books I have gleaned a few attributes of absolute truth that Srila Prabhupada talks about by the very word absolute truth a truth that is not dependent on any other truth it is absolute truth

#### Absolute Truth, Relative Truth, Independence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/c6T8cIJthGg?t=1724
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "a truth that is not dependent on any other truth it is absolute truth"
**Summary:** The speaker defines absolute truth as a truth that is not dependent on any other truth, contrasting it with relative truth and introducing the principle of absolute independence (Swarat).

this is one understanding. Now sometimes if you want to understand something you should understand the opposite of it then also you will understand the opposite of absolute truth is obviously the relative truth which means a truth that is dependent on another truth so it may still look little philosophical I will give you an example for this say for instance my existence is a truth I exist I am a person I think I act and I do different kinds of activities and so I am a truth I am my existence is a truth now however my existence depends on the existence of my parents if my parents don't exist I would not have come about isn't it so my existence is a truth but it is dependent on the existence of my parents hence my existence is a relative truth it is dependent on the existence of another two individuals my parents and they existed and because of that I have come into existence if they did not exist I would not have existed in this way right and so my existence as I see here in this form in this body is a relative truth. Now the existence of my parents is also a relative truth as their existence depends on the existence of my grandparents now you get a sense truth relative truth if it is dependent on another truth and now we are looking for in Vedanta philosophy an absolute truth which is not dependent on any other truth is there a truth that is not dependent on any other truth yes it is and from this we understand one attribute of absolute truth the principle of absolute independence and in Sanskrit this is known as Swarat Swarat means it is not dependent on anything else for its existence it exists by itself and hence is an attribute of absolute truth

#### Absolute Truth, Endurance, Time

**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "absolute truth is true in all the three phases of time past present and future something that is that exists in all three phases of truth is called absolute truth if it does not exist in all three phases of time it is called relative truth"
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the second attribute of absolute truth: endurance through all three phases of time (past, present, future), referencing a verse from the Bhagavad Gita to support this principle.

now there is a second test or a second attribute of absolute truth and Prabhupada talks about this in the Krishna book second chapter absolute truth is true in all the three phases of time time is divided into three phases past present and future something that is that exists in all three phases of truth is called absolute truth if it does not exist in all three phases of time it is called relative truth I will give an example let's take the beauty of a flower now the beauty of a flower you take a flower and it is very beautiful fragrant colorful and all of that but before some days ago it was a bud enclosed in a with its calyx and it was not beautiful not as beautiful as a colorful flower would be and you leave it for a few days it will crumble wither and it will not be beautiful anymore so beauty of a flower as we experience in this world is a relative truth because it does not exist in all three phases of time so now we come we come to the second principle for absolute truth for understanding absolute truth called the principle of endurance there is a verse in the Bhagavad Gita Krishna talks about this na sato vidyate bhavo na bhavo vidyate sataha ubhayor api drishtom thas tvanyo statva darshivihi see Krishna is using the word tattva darshivihi people who have seen the absolute truth people who know the truth they are tattva darshivihi they are seeing the truth and they are talking about na sato na asataha sat and asat if you remember I had pointed out as the nomenclature for talking about sat absolute truth and relative truth now this verse I have slightly opened it up because of the sandhis I have I have removed this sandhis and opened it up look at this now na asataha vidyate bhavaha na bhavaha vidyate sataha ubhayor api drishtom thas tvanyo statva darshivihi second line I have not broken it up now you can see na asataha relative truth vidyate bhavaha relative truth does not endure and na bhavaha vidyate sataha absolute truth does not undergo changes that's how the translation is those who are seers of the truth tattva darshivihi have concluded that of the relative truth asat there is no endurance and of the absolute truth there is no change so non-changing it is fixed it's not going to become something else tomorrow in all three phases of time it will remain the same that is one of the qualities of absolute truth

#### Cause and Effect, Material Cause, Multiple Causes
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/c6T8cIJthGg?t=2263
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "for any effect like an earthen pot is an effect there are multiple causes which makes it happen"
**Summary:** The speaker uses the example of an earthen pot to illustrate the concept of multiple causes, including material, instrumental, and efficient causes.

is something that's very common. Now let's take one example cause effect analysis what is the cause of an earthen pot so we are all familiar with an earthen pot what is its cause someone came up and said ah come on I know what is the cause of an earthen pot it's the earth because it's the earth that was made into a pot someone else said ah no no no it was the potter's wheel if you have earth and you don't have a potter's wheel how will you get a pot it will just remain as Earth so you need a potter's wheel and the potter's wheel is actually the cause of the pot then someone else came up and said no not the earth not the potter's wheel it was actually the potter if there was no potter if there is earth and there is a potter's wheel and if there is no potter where will you have a pot so it's actually it's the potter who is actually the cause of an Earth of an earthen pot so this kind of analysis and actually there are different kinds of causes what is known as a material cause an instrumental cause an efficient cause so the earth is the material cause potter's wheel is also a cause it's an instrumental cause and the potter himself is an efficient cause so in other words we are beginning to see that for any effect like an earthen pot is an effect there are multiple causes which makes it happen

I'll give another quick example for a good understanding of this we look at a fast moving car and we want to analyze what is the cause of a moving car someone will say the petrol someone will say the engine, the gearbox, transmission etc. and someone will say the driver yes, actually it's true petrol is the material cause engine, gearbox, transmission etc. all of that is the instrumental cause and the driver is the effective cause now this discussion about multiple causes effect and cause and for a certain effect there are multiple causes comes up in the Bhagavad Gita in the 18th chapter where Krishna identifies five causes and he says panchaithaani maha paho karanani nibhodame nibhoda reflect on this Arjuna consider this, deliberate on this he is saying panchaithaani maha panchaithaani there are five karanani five causes sankhye krithante prokthani siddhaye sarvakarmanam sarvakarmanam siddhaye siddhaye means for perfection for success for completion of all activities sarvakarmanam siddhaye according to Sankhya philosophy krithante prokthani it is said that there are five causes and in the next verse Krishna delineates what are the five causes he says adhishthanam tatha kartha karanam cha pruthak vidham vividhas cha pruthak cheshta daivam chaivatra panchamam in this way there are five causes

sometimes we come across people saying that you know our business became successful because we had good team we had wonderful people and that was the people who made business successful I have heard of one person saying that a very very successful businessman he said you take away everything from me and you leave my team with me and you give me ten years time I will build my business to the same level of making it into a big billion dollar business I can do it because I depend on my team and I have very good people and they are the ones who make my business successful so people talk about that one so that means they are talking about karta they are all recognizing this as the cause of that effect success in business a third example third cause is sometimes people say you know we worked very hard we were very very persevering and we were execution focused and that's how our business became successful they are identifying cheshta cheshta means endeavor and so they are giving importance to endeavor and that endeavor led to the success of the business they are saying you know so that's the kind of thing sometimes people come up with this another explanation they say that we used appropriate technology we used appropriate strategies and because of that our business became successful so they are actually referring to karanam certain instrumental causes which led to the success of the business so actually when you do a empirical study why a business has become successful invariably people identify these kind of reasons or if you ask a successful businessman they will say government policies were good market conditions were good we were very hard working good team you know these kind of things

**Summary:** The speaker introduces the principle of the chain of causes, using the example of a cool breeze to illustrate how any effect can be traced back to a series of causes, each of which is itself an effect.

#### Vedanta philosophy, Purpose of existence, Nature of God
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/evLBY6yRyDA?t=72
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Vedanta philosophy says something more, not just the purpose of our creation, purpose of our existence, purpose of what was in God's mind when he created this world, but what is also the nature of God, God's existence, and what is it like, all of that is also part of Vedanta subject matter."
**Summary:** This segment introduces Vedanta philosophy as a means to understand the purpose of existence, God's plan, and the nature of God.

very happy to be with all of you. Yesterday, we began talking about science and the cutting edge of research in science, and how the scientists are trying to formulate what is known as a theory of everything. Some of the eminent scientists say that if we were to do that, that would be the triumph of human reason, and that would explain why we exist and why the universe exists, and perhaps by that we will know the mind of God. We talked about how in the Vedanta philosophy, to understand the purpose of our existence and the purpose of the existence of the universe, and what was God's plan for us and for the universe, is actually the central theme of Vedanta, and that is why we are here today. Vedanta philosophy, it's actually not the entirety of Vedanta philosophy. Vedanta philosophy says something more, not just the purpose of our creation, purpose of our existence, purpose of what was in God's mind when he created this world, but what is also the nature of God, God's existence, and what is it like. All of that is also part of Vedanta subject matter, but nevertheless, this is an important and a central theme of Vedanta philosophy.

#### Srila Prabhupada's vision, Absolute truth, Conception of God
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/evLBY6yRyDA?t=169
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Srila Prabhupada wanted this aspect of this Vedanta philosophy as given in the Gita is so fundamental, universal, basic and very powerful and this should influence and enrich people's life, not just in India, not just a few religious people, but everyone in the world."
**Summary:** This segment discusses Srila Prabhupada's vision to spread Vedanta philosophy universally and differentiates between the conception of absolute truth and the conception of God, explaining gradations of controllers.

Then we also discussed how Srila Prabhupada wanted this aspect of this Vedanta philosophy as given in the Gita is so fundamental, universal, basic, and very powerful, and this should influence and enrich people's life, not just in India, not just a few religious people, but everyone in the world. That was his plan, that was his vision, that was the vision of the previous Acharyas. Then we started talking about the absolute truth and the conception of absolute truth and the conception of God. That was one of the things that Srila Prabhupada brings up. He says the conception of absolute truth and the conception of God are not on the same level. We discussed how the concept of God, how absolute truth represents the source of everything, the ultimate cause of everything, and the conception of God is in the sense of an Ishwara or a controller. We also talked that there are gradations of Ishwaras, and like say for instance, I am the Ishwara of my house, we can say all of you are Ishwaras in your house, you control many things in your house, but you cannot control what is there what's happening in your neighbor's house, so you are a controller, but a limited controller. There are bigger controllers who can control the whole state, there are controllers who can control the whole country, but there are others who control even them, so they also come under the control of many other things, so there are gradations of controllers, and that's the conception of God we talked about that.

#### Attributes of absolute truth, Cause and effect, Vedic philosophy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/evLBY6yRyDA?t=272
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We live in a universe which is actually a complex web of causes and effects, all of them aligned on the time dimension."
**Summary:** This segment delves into the attributes of absolute truth, its independence, and its nomenclature in Sanskrit, further explaining the analysis of cause and effect in Vedic philosophy and the concept of a chain of causes.

Then we also discussed a little bit of the attributes of absolute truth, truth not dependent on any other truth, and in Sanskrit that's known as the Swara or absolute independence. We also talked about the nomenclature of absolute truth: Tattva, Paratattva, Sat and Brahman, Parabrahman, and these are some of the words used to describe absolute truth. We also started discussing how this analysis of cause and effects is very much prevalent in Vedic philosophy. We also discussed how in the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna identifies five causes for any successful action: Panchayathani, Mahabhaho, Karanani, Nipodavi, and so on. We analyzed, we took some examples of these five causes, and then we also found that in yesterday's discussion, you may find an effect and you may find its cause, but then the cause itself is an effect caused by another cause. So in other words, we realize that there are chain of causes, and then we talked about how from the Vedanta perspective, we live in a universe which is actually a complex web of causes and effects, all of them aligned on the time dimension, and these were some of the things that we talked yesterday.

#### Science, Technology, Cause and effect
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/evLBY6yRyDA?t=367
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "While science tries to discover effects and its causes, there is something known as the technology, which applies that learning to improve our lives."
**Summary:** This segment compares the quest for causes and effects in modern science with technology's application of scientific discoveries to improve life.

Going further, the quest for causes and effects is an important feature even in modern science. The most interesting example is that there was a man sitting under an apple tree and he found an apple fall to ground, and unlike most people who would have picked up an apple and eaten it, this man started thinking why did the apple fall to the ground, meaning he saw an effect, apple fall to the ground, and he was looking for its cause, and that led to the discovery of the theory of gravity. Now another thing that happens in science is that while science tries to discover effects and its causes, there is something known as the technology which applies that learning to improve our lives. That's how we have got air travel, we have got communication, computers, internet mobile phones and all that. That's the part of technology which takes advantage of science and what science discovers in terms of cause and effects in this world, and technology uses that to improve our life situation.

As a result, today science and technology are considered to be very very powerful and reliable and dependable sources of knowledge and understanding about this world, so that's how the science has reached that kind of an eminence and respectability in modern society. Now what happens is that yes, Newton discovered that the apple fell to the ground because of the theory of gravity, because there is something known as gravity, another phenomenon called gravity. Now did Newton question what is the cause of gravity? Like that in all the different discoveries that science does, it's not that science does not do, but it does to some extent, but the benefit that technology provides by such discovery becomes overwhelming, and as a result science and technology gets a high level of esteemed position in people's estimation. But I would say the Vedanta philosophy is far more rigorous probing and penetrating in its analysis of causes and effects, and especially to find cause its cause its cause and what is that ultimate cause, looking for that ultimate cause, looking for that final cause, the absolute truth is something that Vedanta philosophy engages in much more rigorously, much more probing penetrating way.

#### Cause and effect, Ultimate cause, Engine analogy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/evLBY6yRyDA?t=550
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Trying to find the ultimate cause which is not dependent on any other cause, but can cause all other causes, this is actually the search that is going on in the Vedanta philosophy."
**Summary:** Using the analogy of a train, this segment explains the concept of cause and effect, emphasizing the search for the ultimate cause that is independent and the source of all other causes within Vedanta philosophy.

Now Srila Prabhupada gives an interesting example of this in one Conversations which is recorded in the book Life Comes from Life. Prabhupada gives this example: suppose you are sitting in a train bogie and that bogie is moving, that's the effect, a bogie is moving and your question will be why is my bogie moving? A quick answer is there is another bogie in front of your bogie and that's pulling it and hence your bogie is moving, fair enough, accurate, correct, rational, scientific answer. Now we will shift the question, why is that bogie moving? We will get an answer that bogie is moving because there is another bogie in front of it that is pulling it, fair enough. We shift our question, why is that bogie moving? You will say another bogie is moving and on and on and on and finally you will say you will come to an engine, a locomotive engine which is pulling all of these bogies. Then you will say very good, now tell me what is moving the cause of the locomotive engine that is moving all of this? Is there another bogie in front of it which is pulling the locomotive engine? Hey no no no, an engine doesn't require something else to be pulling it. An engine is such that it can pull all the bogies and also can pull itself. That's why all the bogies are in one category, engine is of another category altogether and engine doesn't require anything else, it is by itself able to move and can cause the movement of all other bogies. So this is the cause effect cause effect and trying to find the ultimate cause which is not dependent on any other cause but can cause all other causes, this is actually the search that is going on in the Vedanta philosophy.

**Summary:** This segment argues that parents are not the complete cause of our existence, using the example of a blind man to illustrate the limitations of parental influence and emphasizing the importance of philosophical understanding.

the father and mother of a blind man and ask those father and mother, I mean you father and mother, you are the father and mother of this blind man. I said, why couldn't you do a better job in your life? Why did you create a son who has become, who is blind? Both of you can see, right? You have sight, you have eyes, you see properly, why didn't you cause a son who also has proper sight? And they will say, oh no, we couldn't do anything, you know, it was not in our hands, and they would say. So actually you are the father and mother of this blind man, why couldn't you do a better job in your life? Actually our parents are our cause, but not the complete cause, they are one of the causes. It's not that actually the parents, you know, the father and mother sat one day and they said, okay, you know, let's have a child and let the child have why one heart, let the child have two hearts in case one heart fails there will be another heart. No, they didn't do any such thinking. They didn't think that, you know, let the child, let our child have two kidneys, no, no, let our child have four kidneys, you know, in case two of the one pair fails there is another pair, okay, let's decide on two pairs of kidneys. They didn't do any such thinking, it was not in their control. And in fact they didn't even know whether it's going to be a boy or a girl, nine months they were thinking it's going to be a boy, it's going to be a girl, no boy girl boy girl, ah, at last they were not in control of everything. So actually if you remember that analysis, they were instrumental causes, there were many other causes. So it's not to mean any disrespect for our parents or anything, it's more philosophically to trying to understand things and set records right.

#### Ultimate cause, Personal story, Importance of cause
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/evLBY6yRyDA?t=1388
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Why is it important to know the cause, its cause, its cause, and the ultimate cause?"
**Summary:** The speaker transitions to a personal story to illustrate the importance of understanding the chain of causes and the ultimate cause.

Now one more question, okay, it's important to know the cause, like we discovered that the cause is like our parents, the cost and our existence, we owe it to them and all of that. But then we also discussed that there are only a limited cause, not the everything, not the complete cause. Now the question is why is it important to know the cause, its cause, its cause and the ultimate cause? I'll tell you one interesting story from my personal life. I was studying in a college, I think I have told this story in some places and I was thinking this story in some places in some forums but appropriate and relevant for this particular situation. So I was studying in a college and I was in a hostel and the beginning of the month, every month there would be one man in khaki dress, he would come and during lunch break he had a small piece of khaki and he would take out a piece of paper, a folded paper and he would unfold that and then he would call out different students name and we would all be eager to hear if our name is called out and then if our name is called out we would line up, if our name is not called out, okay, he didn't call and we would go away and then next day again come wait for this man in khaki dress and he would call out the name, he takes our signature and he gives money and I would get about 250 rupees or something like that and that 250 rupees given away by this man in khaki dress was very important for me because I had to pay the mess bill which was about 220 rupees or so and then I would have another 30 rupees for the month as some small expenses or so. So this man calling out my name, taking my signature and giving 250 rupees was very, very important for me because it will sail me through for another month and I can go to college and I can be in the hostel, I can eat food in the mess and all of those kind of things.

#### Immediate cause, Remote cause, Ultimate cause
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/evLBY6yRyDA?t=1532
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "You must also know that this man, this postman, is not doing some charity every day coming and distributing his money or something, he's not doing any such thing, it's because my father had deposited that money and then it had moved through the postal system and this man was the last man who would come and deliver to me."
**Summary:** The speaker shares a story about receiving money orders in college, distinguishing between the immediate cause (the postman) and the remote, ultimate cause (his father), emphasizing the importance of recognizing the ultimate cause.

And so when you receive that money you would naturally say thank you Mr. man in khaki dress and all of those kind of things and I may say that oh you are such a wonderful man you really made my life I can be I am so happy with you you know you understand already you must have figured out the man in the khaki uniform was a postman and he would come and give that money in those olden days 35 years back or so I don't know if some of the young people may not know these things there used to be a system called money order in the post office maybe it's there even now I am not sure and so this postman would come and give the money that's because my parents my father who was in another city he would go to the post office and put that money there and because that money was deposited there and there was a postal system finally this postman will come and give me the money now it's not that I take the money from the postman and say get lost that's also not right it's curt it's not it's rude being that way but you must also know that this man this postman is not doing some charity every day coming and distributing his money or something he's not doing any such thing it's because my father had deposited that money and then it had moved through the postal system and this man was the last man who would come and deliver to me he was probably my immediate cause but the remote and the distant cause was my father had deposited that money in the post office now you see how important it is not only we recognize our immediate cause we must know its cause its cause its cause and this may be a distant cause but an important cause the ultimate cause because without my father depositing that money this postman would never ever have delivered that money to me and so the ultimate cause which which gives the first push is of immense importance and we must recognize that

#### Sentient existence, Insentient existence, Categories of creation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/evLBY6yRyDA?t=1893
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We find two categories of creation, two categories of truth, two categories of existence: one is the sentient existence, sentient meaning conscious, with cognitive functions like perceptions, feelings, desires, emotions, and with personal attributes; another category of existence that we see in the creation is insentient existence, which is lacking consciousness, perceptions, lacking cognitive functions, impersonal or lacking personality."
**Summary:** This segment categorizes creation into sentient (conscious) and insentient (lacking consciousness) existence, stating that both originate from the absolute truth.

we find two categories of creation, two categories of truth, two categories of existence. One is the sentient existence, sentient meaning conscious with cognitive functions like perceptions, feelings, desires, emotions, and with personal attributes like all of us are human beings. Not just human beings, even animals, they also are conscious, they are sentient. Even an ant is sentient, it is conscious, it has cognitive functions, it has feelings, emotions, desires, aspirations, goals in life, all of these things. So this is one category of sentient existence. Another category of existence that we see in the creation is insentient existence, which is lacking consciousness, perceptions, lacking cognitive functions, impersonal or lacking personality, just like the table, the building, the mountains, the river, land, and all of these things. This is also part of this creation. So broadly, this creation has two kinds of categories of creation: one is the sentient and the insentient. Now, both categories of creation have come from the absolute truth. That's the Vedanta Sutra Janma Dhyasya Yatha, that truth from which all of this creation has come, meaning both categories of creation have come from that absolute truth. Now it's a simple logic, the cause should be more than the effect. Like, say for example, we look at the sun and we see the sun is so bright, so much heat, and the sunshine is coming from the sun and we see the sunshine is so bright and so hot, so it has got heat and light. Now what can we say about the source of the sunlight, the sun?

#### Krishna, Source of everything, Superior energy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/evLBY6yRyDA?t=3046
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Inferior energy has no power unless superior energy is involved."
**Summary:** This segment quotes Srila Prabhupada, stating that Krishna is the source of everything and that inferior energy is dependent on superior energy, using the analogy of an engineer controlling a train to illustrate Krishna's role in the cosmic manifestation.

And Srila Prabhupada points out, "Lord Krishna is the source of everything, *aham sarvasya prabhavaha*." And thus, the end of all knowledge, Vedanta. In this world, there may be knowledge, and the end of all knowledge is to know the Lord, to know our relationship with Him, and to act according to that relationship only, because we have no other work, we have no other business in this world. We only, the truth is that Krishna is there, and we are caused by Him, from Him we have come, and we have a relationship, and we must act in that relationship. So we are offered from various sources, as I mentioned earlier, in the book *Life Comes From Life* by Srila Prabhupada, Prabhupada mentions this. Srila Prabhupada, inferior energy has no power unless superior energy is involved. This sea, indicating the Pacific, Prabhupada was taking a walk in the morning in Los Angeles, and he is pointing out, indicating the Pacific Ocean is calm and quiet, but when the superior force, air, pushes it, it manifests high waves. The ocean has no power to move without the superior force of air. Similarly, there is another force superior to the air, and another, and another, and ultimately we arrive at Krishna. This is real research. Krishna controls nature just as an engineer controls a train. The engineer controls a locomotive which pulls one car, and that car in turn pulls another, which pulls another, and so the whole train is moving. Similarly with the creation, Krishna gives the first push, and then by means of successive pushes, the entire cosmic manifestation comes into being and is maintained.

#### Material nature, Seed-giving father, Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/evLBY6yRyDA?t=3172
**Verse References:** BG 9.10, BG 14.4
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This material nature is working under my direction and is producing all moving and non-moving beings."
**Summary:** This segment quotes Krishna from the Bhagavad Gita, explaining that material nature works under His direction and that He is the seed-giving father, the primary cause of everything, while scientists only observe intermediate causes.

This is explained in the Bhagavad Gita, "This material nature is working under my direction and is producing all moving and non-moving beings." And in the fourteenth chapter Krishna says, *sarvayon nishukam teya murtaya sambhavantiya tasam brahma mahad yonir aham bija pradham pitah*, "All species of life are made possible even by birth in this material nature, O son of Kunti, and I am the seed giving father." For example, if we sow a banyan seed, a huge tree eventually comes up, and along with it millions of new seeds. Each of these seeds can in turn produce another tree with millions of new seeds, and so on. This is how Krishna, the original seed giving father, is the primary cause of everything we see. Unfortunately, the scientists observe only the intermediate cause. They cannot perceive the remote cause. Krishna is described in the Vedas as *sarvakarana karanam*, the cause of all causes. If one understands the cause of all causes, then he understands everything, *yasmin vignate sarvam evam vignatam bhavati* Upanishad. If one knows the original cause, the subordinate causes are automatically known. Although the scientists are searching after the original cause, when the Vedas, perfect knowledge, declare the original cause to be the supreme personality of Godhead, the scientists won't accept it. They keep to their partial imperfect knowledge. This is their disease. All this Prabhupada is telling in that morning walk conversation.

---
#### Prasthana Traya, Absolute truth, Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/evLBY6yRyDA?t=3276
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The demigods give information that the absolute supreme absolute truth is Krishna."
**Summary:** This segment references the Prasthana Traya and states that the demigods declare Krishna as the supreme absolute truth, existing in all phases of time and the source of all relative truth.

There is another reference from Chaitanya Charita Amruta. According to learned scholars, there are three different sources of knowledge which are called Prasthana Traya. According to these scholars, Vedanta is one such source for its presence, Vedic knowledge on the basis of logic and sound arguments. In the Bhagavad Gita, the Lord says, *Brahma Sutra Pada is Chai Vahet Umad Pir Vini Shchitai Vini Shchitai Hi*, understanding of the ultimate goal of life is ascertained in the Brahma Sutra by legitimate logic and argument concerning cause and effect. That's what we have been discussing yesterday and today, causes and effects and all of these things. There is another reference, this is too fine, and I think I'll read this slowly. Then the demigods address the Lord as *Satyam Param*, or the supreme absolute truth. Everyone is searching after the truth, that is a philosophical way of life. The demigods give information that the absolute supreme absolute truth is Krishna. One who becomes fully Krishna conscious can attain the absolute truth. Krishna is the absolute truth because unlike relative truth, he is the truth in all three phases of eternal time. Time is divided into past, present, and future. Krishna is truth always, past, present, and future, being controlled by supreme time in the course of past, present, and future. But before the creation, Krishna was existing, and when there is creation, everything is resting in Krishna, and when this creation is finished, Krishna will remain. Therefore, he is the absolute truth in all circumstances. If there is any truth within this material world, it emanates from that supreme truth, Krishna. If there is any opulence within this material world, the cause of the opulence is Krishna. If there is any reputation within this material world, the cause of the reputation is Krishna. If there is any strength within this material world, the cause of such strength is Krishna. If there is any wisdom and education within this material world, the cause of such wisdom and education is Krishna. Therefore, Krishna is the source of all relative truth, so...

#### Krishna, Cosmic manifestation, Superior force
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/evLBY6yRyDA?t=3507
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna gives the first push and then by means of successive pushes the entire cosmic manifestation comes into being and is maintained."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Krishna initiates the cosmic manifestation and sustains it through successive pushes, emphasizing His role as the superior force.

In the Bhagavad Gita, "This material nature is working under my direction and is producing all moving and non-moving beings." And in the 14th chapter Krishna says, "All species of life are made possible by birth by the material nature, O son of Kunti, and I am the seed giving father." For example, if we sow a banyan seed, a huge tree eventually comes up, and along with it millions of new seeds. Each of these seeds can in turn produce another tree with millions of new seeds, and so on. This is how Krishna, the original seed giving father, is the primary cause of everything we see. Unfortunately, the scientists observe the intermediate cause. They cannot perceive the remote cause. Krishna is described in the Vedas as *Sarva Karana Karanam*, the cause of all causes. If one understands the cause of all causes, then he understands everything Upanishad. If one knows the original cause, the subordinate causes are automatically known. Although the scientists are searching for the original cause, when the Vedas perfect knowledge declare the original cause to be the supreme personality of Godhead, the scientists won't accept it. They keep to their partial imperfect knowledge. This is their disease. All this Prabhupada is telling in that morning walk.

### Three Dangerous Diversions from Bhakti-yoga | Sri Chanchalapathi Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Chanchalapathi Dasa

#### bhakti-yoga, diversions, karma, mukti, yogasiddhi
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/j8IUXnNznv8?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "If you are able to somehow resist the temptation of these three exits, then you can head towards the supreme personality of Godhead."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of driving on an expressway to illustrate how karma, mukti, and yogasiddhi can be diversions from the path of bhakti-yoga.

You're driving on the expressway, you're heading very quickly, but then you see exit, food court, and so you exit, and then you'll see so many different kinds of food all displayed there, and then you get absorbed and lost there, and then you missed the purpose that you were heading in the expressway. So, the shastras point out, when you are trying to advance in the mission of human life, you will encounter three exits. The first exit is called karma. The second exit is called mukti, and the third exit is called yogasiddhi. If you are not careful, you can take that exit, and you can be lost there completely, and forget coming back to the exit. So, you're driving on the expressway, and heading towards the goal that you set out. If you are able to somehow resist the temptation of these three exits, then you can head towards the supreme personality of Godhead. Because, if you want to associate with Krishna, you should be free from the temptations of karma, jnana, and yogasiddhi. That is a pure devotee, who only wants to serve Krishna. He is Krishna.

#### Srimad Bhagavatam, Creation, Mode of Passion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/bpXAJdRP-d4?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "By further transformation of the mode of passion, the sense organs, like the ears, skin, nose, eyes, tongue, mouth, hands, genitals, legs and the outlet for evacuating, together with intelligence and living energy, are all generated."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the verse from Srimad Bhagavatam describing the creation of sense organs, intelligence, and living energy from the mode of passion.

Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya. Coming from Srimad Bhagavatam, 2nd Canto, 5th Chapter, entitled The Cause of All Causes, text number 31. Taija Sat Tuvikurvanad Indriyanidashad Bhavan. Jnana Shakti Kriya Shaktir Buddhiv Pranascha Taija Sav. Shrotram Tvagrana Drukjikva Vagdor Medra Angripayavaha. Shrotram Tvagrana Drukjikva Vagdor Medra Angripayavaha. Translation in purport by His Divine Grace A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Ishwara Prabhupada. Translation: By further transformation of the mode of passion, the sense organs, like the ears, skin, nose, eyes, tongue, mouth, hands, genitals, legs and the outlet for evacuating, together with intelligence and living energy, are all generated.

#### False Identity, Vedic Knowledge, Ahankara

This life we have taken a male form. In our previous life what we were? Maybe female form, maybe some other form. And in that body we think I am male, I am female. And because we have a male or a female human form, we think I am a human being. This is also part of our self-identity. You see, Vedic knowledge is dealing with these subjects about identity. What is going on in our mind? What is going on in our consciousness? Let's understand. What is the truth behind these things? And since we are born in India, we think I am an Indian man. I am an Indian woman. Someone else born in some other part of the country, they think I am American man. I am German woman or whatever. So, Ahankara. This is the kind of a false egoism that is so intrinsic. We live with it. It comes with us all our life. Many things are decided because of these things. I am a man, I am Indian, I am woman. And many other things. I am intelligent. I am smart. I am foolish. I am not so intelligent. #### Gunas, Enjoyment, Ignorance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/bpXAJdRP-d4?t=896
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The most important thing that Rajoguna does is, it puts us in a framework as I am an enjoyer."
**Summary:** This segment discusses how Ahankara is influenced by the three modes of nature (Sattva, Rajas, Tamas), with Rajoguna leading to the desire for enjoyment, often mixed with enlightenment and ignorance.

Next thing the Vedic literatures are pointing out. Now this Ahankara can come under three kinds of influence: Sattva, Rajas and Tamas. And this verse is discussing about this Ahankara under the influence of Rajas. Rajoguna. The most important thing that Rajoguna does is, it puts us in a framework as I am an enjoyer. So, I am an enjoyer. I must enjoy. Now I have a body. I must enjoy with this body. Sattva Guna creates a certain kind of an understanding. It enlightens. But usually in the material world, Sattva Guna is never alone. It gets mixed with Rajoguna. So, with a certain kind of an enlightened outlook, we try to become enjoyer. And it is not just that, Sattva Guna and Rajoguna mix. There is also always an element of Tamoguna. And Tamoguna's influence is to create ignorance. So, a sense of enlightenment and knowledge and understanding with the fundamental desire to enjoy, but not covered with a certain amount of ignorance.

---
#### Knowledge, Enjoyment, Material World
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/bpXAJdRP-d4?t=1021
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We use our intelligence, we use our systematic thinking, we use our school and college education, everything, how to manipulate the material world, for my enjoyment."
**Summary:** This segment describes how individuals combine knowledge, the desire for enjoyment, and a degree of ignorance to manipulate the material world for their own gratification.

This is actually what each one of us are. There is some knowledge. And knowledge, when it is charged with enjoying tendency, we look at this world, and we look at, we develop knowledge about this world, and how to manipulate this world, with our knowledge and understanding, for our enjoyment. You see? That's why Prabhupada says in the purport here, the living condition in material existence depends more or less on one's intelligence and powerful living energy. Intelligence to counteract the hard struggle for existence is assisted by the senses for acquiring knowledge. And the living energy maintains himself by manipulating the active organs, like the hands and legs. But on the whole, the struggle for existence is an exertion of the mode of passion. That is why you see, today, there is so much knowledge of science, technology, economics, management. So, all of this is a certain kind of a knowledge. But, it is charged and influenced with Rajas. And so, we use our intelligence, we use our systematic thinking, we use our school and college education, everything, how to manipulate the material world, for my enjoyment. Now, this thinking that, I am a citizen of this world, and I must enjoy this world, this is actually an aspect of ignorance. Because we are not really a citizen of this world. We have come here. We are not these human bodies. We have got a human body. We see around us, there are so many other non-human bodies also. And there are living entities there also. So, this passion, knowledge, and ignorance, combine together, in different ways, and create the material world, of which we are also part.

---
#### Soul, Purpose of Life, Kali Yuga
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/bpXAJdRP-d4?t=1172
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We become so absorbed in these things, our whole life passes, without understanding the true position of the soul."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes how absorption in material pursuits obscures the true nature of the soul and the purpose of life, highlighting the predominance of passionate egoism in Kali Yuga.

We become so absorbed in these things, our whole life passes, without understanding the true position of the soul, the true position of the living entity, and understanding why this elaborate arrangement is all there. Who made them? What was the purpose of making this? So, this passionate egoism, taijasa, this ahankara, charged with rajoguna, is what we become, all of us become engaged in. And in Kali Yuga, this taijasa, passionate egoism, is predominant. It is very powerful. Now, the truth of the matter is, you see this passion basically puts us in a mode of enjoyment. We seek enjoyment in different ways. In the outside world, people are seeking enjoyment for sense gratification. More money, you go to a restaurant and eat, dress up nicely and have, go to a mall, go to a social function. And, that's what they are doing. And for maintaining a certain standard of that, they are working very hard. So, it's basically a sense of enjoyment through hard work, and expression, using their senses, in different kinds of enjoyment activities. For that, we need money. And, and then, if you have nice position, position of power, position of authority, position of fame, that can enhance your ability to enjoy your senses even more. You see, this is what the material world is.

#### Detachment, Devotional Service, Enjoyment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/bpXAJdRP-d4?t=1325
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "I just want to render service to you, life after life."
**Summary:** This segment introduces Chaitanya Mahaprabhu's teachings on detachment from material desires (wealth, followers, beauty, fruitive activities) and emphasizes pure devotional service (bhakti) focused on Krishna's enjoyment, not personal gratification.

So, Chaitanya Mahaprabhu teaches, na dhanam, na janam, na sundareem, kavitam vajagadisha kamaye, kamaye, na kamaye, I don't want to desire these things, na dhanam, kamaye, na janam, kamaye, na sundareem, kamaye, na kavitam, kavitam here refers to, kavita means poetry. Vedas describe in a very poetic way that how you can enjoy now and in future in swargaloka. These are called poetic descriptions in the Vedic literature. Flowery, pushpitam vajam, Krishna says in the Gita, flowery words. This is the kavita refers to fruity activities described in the Vedic literatures. Na dhanam, na janam, na sundareem, kavitam vajagadishakamaye, mama janmani janmanishvare, janmani janmanishvare, janmani janmani, life after life. So, in spiritual, in Vedic culture, we learn these are all not, these are all fleeting things, all the pleasures of the senses, the pleasures of wealth, the pleasures of women, the pleasures of honor, and fame, and position, and all these things, they are fleeting. Even going to swargaloka, that is also fleeting. Next, we should aspire for moksha, so that we can end this birth and death cycle, and we can be situated in moksha, and in that way, in moksha there is ananda of moksha. Again, ananda came there, enjoyment came there. No. So, Chaitanya Mahaprabhu is saying, mama janmani janmanishvare, bhavata bhakti rahayi tukitvayi, I just want to render service to you, life after life. So, I do not want even moksha also. So, Chaitanya Mahaprabhu is establishing the principles of pure devotional service, pure bhakti.

---
#### Bhakti, Krishna's Pleasure, Constitutional Position
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/bpXAJdRP-d4?t=1488
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Our focus is not our enjoyment, our focus is Krishna's enjoyment."
**Summary:** This segment clarifies that pure bhakti focuses on Krishna's pleasure, not personal enjoyment, and emphasizes that serving Krishna is the natural, constitutional activity of the soul.

So, in pure, so somebody may say, oh with pure bhakti, you will get bhakti, ananda of bhakti, no? That is the natural ananda, in that, the specific thing about bhakti is, our focus is not our enjoyment, our focus is Krishna's enjoyment. And as a result of serving Krishna, giving pleasure to Krishna, I may get happiness, I may not get happiness, it doesn't matter. Whether I am in heaven or hell, I simply want an opportunity to please Krishna. This is a new mode. But of course, we are constituted in such a way that when we serve Krishna, we get happiness naturally. The other methods were not natural to our constitution. Just like, if the hand puts food into the stomach, the hand gets nourished. Natural. But the hand putting the food in the stomach is a natural constitutional activity. But if the hand says, no, I won't put food in the stomach, I'll only be touching the food. I'll only be mixing rice, chapati, dal, and just, I won't, why should I feed the stomach? Stomach should enjoy, is it? No, I won't allow. Only I'll be touching. Or I'll be only seeing food. We know that won't nourish us. You see, in the same way, all attempts of karma, all attempts of jnana, are attempts for happiness in an unconstitutional manner. Constitutional meaning, Swaroopa, our makeup, how we are made, how we are constituted. This is the most important thing that we learn in Krishna consciousness. That we are not the enjoyers.

---
#### Krishna as Enjoyer, Service, Disease
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/bpXAJdRP-d4?t=1628
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna alone is the enjoyer."
**Summary:** Using the analogy of the body and its parts, this segment explains that Krishna is the ultimate enjoyer, and our role is to serve Him, just as the hands and legs serve the body. A diseased condition arises when we become uncooperative and unwilling to serve Krishna's pleasure.

Krishna is the enjoyer. Krishna alone is the enjoyer. We have only business is to make him happy. Just like the hands, only business is to keep the body in a comfortable situation. We want to have bath, the hands and the legs will work to give bath. We want to dress up, we want to put tilak, hands and legs assist me to put the tilak. We want to eat, hands and legs help. See how hands and legs are parts of the body, they are constantly working for the upkeep and well-being of the whole body. If hands and legs don't cooperate, it is called a diseased condition. We have to go to a doctor, we have to take some treatment, we have to take some treatment. Hand is not working, I am not able to lift my hand, I can't bend, fold my hand. Oh, you have to go to your doctor, you should examine. See hand is not able to work for the whole. Today our material situation is, we are also components, parts of Krishna, but we are the diseased, uncooperative, unwilling to serve the pleasure, for the pleasure of Krishna. This is our situation. We are that ailing arm. We are the diseased part of the body. This is what we have become today. This is our position. And this is coming because of our spirit of enjoyment. And that spirit of enjoyment is not natural to us. It is infused through the Rajoguna.

---
#### Desire, Material World, Spiritual Life
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/bpXAJdRP-d4?t=1752
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna has made this elaborate arrangement to fulfill our desire."
**Summary:** This segment explains that Krishna facilitates our desire for enjoyment by creating the material world and providing us with bodies and senses. Even in spiritual life, the desire for enjoyment can persist, leading to a struggle for control.

Why, who is doing this? Krishna is only doing this. Oh, Krishna is, why Krishna is doing? Because we want it. Our desire, our desire, to fulfill our desire, Krishna has made this elaborate arrangement. He has given us a material body with the five senses, dasha indriya, dasha buddhi, and he has created this material world. You need a platform for activity. Suppose I am a great singer, or I am a great dancer, I am a great performer. All the artists, they always say, can we come to your temple and sing? Can we come to your temple? Of course, they are devotional people, they say. Because they want a platform for their activity. In the same way, we want a platform for our spirit of enjoyment. So Krishna has created this material world. See how this is what's happening. Now another thing that happens to us, Srila Prabhupada, Chaitanya Mahaprabhu won. Now we come to spiritual life, spiritual understanding, we come to a spiritual organization, but that spirit for enjoyment won't go away so easily. In the spiritual society, in the spiritual organization, we will try again, I should become the enjoyer, I should become the controller, you see how it is.

---
#### Determination, Krishna's Grace, Self-Preservation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/bpXAJdRP-d4?t=1861
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "To free ourselves from that enjoying spirit, first is our determination and our desire that yes, I want to be free from this."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the importance of determination and sincere desire to be free from the enjoying spirit. It highlights that Krishna's grace is essential for overcoming the powerful influence of Rajoguna. It also discusses using the body for self-preservation only, to enable productive service.

To free ourselves from that enjoying spirit, first is our determination and our desire that yes, I want to be free from this. And when Krishna sees that, when Balarama sees that, when Nithyananda Prabhu sees that, they will bless us. Because by our own we cannot get out. It's a Daivi Guna. These forces of Rajoguna, they are all divine. So it's very powerful. On our own we cannot. That is why we must desire, we must make that desire sincere, and we must make that desire strong. And a strong desire is nothing but determination. And through that determination, we demonstrate to Krishna that I want to abstain from acts of enjoyment. We use our body, we act, we feed our body, take care of our body, to the extent of, to the extent required for self-preservation only. If you don't feed the body, we cannot do, the body will not have energy, we cannot do anything productive. So, some Shakti is required. So we eat, we sleep, we accept these some comforts, only to the extent of self-preservation.

And otherwise, our intent is to only give pleasure to Krishna. So, this enjoying tendency created by passion, how this is all brought about, is what this verse is describing. So, and by discussing among ourselves, and by telling others, because when we are telling others, first person who is hearing is ourself. Because mind and intelligence, mind and intelligence we can understand it's active and it mind into things feels wills whereas senses are not okay actually when Krishna is saying mind intelligence senses actually it looks like intelligence senses are dull parts of it actually even the mind and intelligence is Jada now today what is happening in our situation mind intelligence senses all of them have been energized by prana Shakti so it is the prana Shakti energized mind intelligence senses which is the sitting place of lust suppose there's a dead body and in the dead body there are as eyes are there comma is sitting there you know karma what is karma the lust lust is the spirit of enjoyment that enjoying spirit that enjoying spirit is coming from prana it is expressed through mind intelligence and senses ultimately it is the prana it is the that's why Prabhupada has used the word prana Shakti prana living energy it is that living force and that living force is coming from the consciousness from the consciousness of the soul and that consciousness is contaminated and in that contaminated state it is energizing the mind intelligence and the senses in a certain way that is what is happening and hence ultimately you may say where is the lust sitting it is sitting in the consciousness and that is why we have to of the living entity that's correct statement that's an accurate statement but today in our present situation where is the last sitting from the consciousness it is now manifest through mind intelligence and the senses so like that it is getting further this is the anatomy.

### Who Truly Owns Everything? | Sri Chanchalapathi Dasa @ChanchalapathiDas
**Speaker:** HG Chanchalapathi Dasa

#### ownership, detachment, surrender
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/gjUWKTLaLSU?t=0
**Verse References:** BG 9.4, Isha Upanishad 1
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "We must use all of this to serve Rama."
**Summary:** This segment establishes the central idea that everything, including our bodies and senses, belongs to Sri Rama, and we should use them in His service.

I have come into this world, I have this Panchabhuta Sharira, Antribhuta Sharira, Sukshma Sharira. To whom all this belongs? Am I the owner of all this? Am I the owner of this body and the owner of the senses? Am I the owner of myself? To whom do I belong? Oh, we have this body, whatever strength we have, may not be much, but all of this belongs to Sri Rama, and we all belong to Sri Rama. We must use all of this to serve Rama. This is the thinking of a Bhatta. This world belongs to the Lord. Isha Vasya Midam Sarvam. Everything that is there, everything, the Lord is the owner. The Lord is the owner. The Lord is the owner.

---

### Who is a Paramahamsa? | Sri Chanchalapathi Dasa  @ChanchalapathiDas
**Speaker:** HG Chanchalapathi Dasa

#### Paramahamsa, detachment, Krishna Bhakti
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/MVHjpcwTGxY?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Unless one comes to the stage of Paramahamsa, one cannot experience Krishna Bhakti in its full form."
**Summary:** The speaker defines Paramahamsa as a stage of detachment from the material world, necessary for experiencing Krishna Bhakti, and applicable to all individuals regardless of their social role.

Rishabhadeva, Paramahamsa in Avadhoota Vesha, he appeared. Unless one comes to the stage of Paramahamsa, one cannot experience Krishna Bhakti in its full form. Paramahamsa stage, where you are uncaring for the material world. You don't care for honour, dishonour, pleasure. You are no more searching for pleasure in this material world. Bhagavatam describes Parikshit Maharaj, who was an emperor, was a Paramahamsa. He was in kingly robes. Prabhupada writes, Paramahamsa is required in every field of human activity. Because Paramahamsa always thinks, how I can do good for others. But this Paramahamsa Dharma can be practiced in other ways. In Avadhoota Vesha also, somebody who is a ruler, he should be a Paramahamsa. Somebody who is a businessman, he should be a Paramahamsa. Somebody who is a housewife, she should be a Paramahamsa. Somebody who is a child, child can also be a Paramahamsa, like Prahalad Dhruvavahar. That should be the goal of human life, to come to the Paramahamsa stage. Because in that Paramahamsa stage, one can actually experience that attraction to Krishna.

---
